# Results and Discussion

## Structural and Textural Characterization

The crystallinity and phase purity of the synthesized zeolite were confirmed by powder X-ray diffraction analysis. The XRD pattern displayed characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 7.9°, 8.8°, 23.1°, 23.3°, 23.9°, and 29.9°, which correspond to the (011), (020), (501), (051), (303), and (501) planes of the MFI framework structure, respectively. No diffraction peaks attributable to amorphous phases or crystalline impurities were observed, indicating successful synthesis of phase-pure zeolite material.

Nitrogen physisorption measurements revealed a BET surface area of 387 m²/g and a total pore volume of 0.19 cm³/g. The micropore volume, determined by the t-plot method, was calculated as 0.14 cm³/g, while the external surface area was determined to be 62 m²/g. These textural parameters are consistent with values reported in the literature for microporous zeolites with MFI topology, where typical surface areas range from 300–450 m²/g and total pore volumes fall within 0.15–0.25 cm³/g.

## Acid Site Characterization by NH₃-TPD

The acidity of the zeolite catalyst was investigated by ammonia temperature-programmed desorption, and the resulting desorption profile is presented in Figure 2. The NH₃-TPD spectrum exhibited three distinct desorption maxima centered at approximately 185°C, 325°C, and 425°C, corresponding to weak, medium, and strong acid sites, respectively. The total acid site concentration, calculated by integrating the desorption peaks, was determined to be 1.24 mmol/g.

Quantitative deconvolution of the desorption peaks using Gaussian functions permitted estimation of the concentration of each acid site type. Weak acid sites were quantified at 0.38 mmol/g, representing 30.6% of total acidity, while medium-strength acid sites accounted for 0.49 mmol/g (39.5% of total). Strong acid sites, associated with Bronsted acid sites in the zeolite framework, comprised the remaining 0.37 mmol/g (29.8% of total acidity).

The high-resolution desorption peak at 425°C indicates the presence of strong Brønsted acid sites characteristic of the zeolite's tetrahedral aluminum sites. The shoulder observed between 280–350°C suggests moderate acid strength distribution, likely arising from variations in local Si/Al ratios and framework aluminum siting. The low-temperature desorption peak at 185°C is attributed to physically adsorbed ammonia and weak Lewis acid sites.

## Comparison with Literature Values and Structure-Property Relationships

The acid site concentration of 1.24 mmol/g falls within the expected range reported for zeolites with Si/Al ratios of 15–25, where literature values typically range from 0.8–1.5 mmol/g. The distribution of acid site strengths is consistent with previous reports on conventional ZSM-5 catalysts, where the strong acid site concentration generally comprises 25–35% of total acidity.

**Recent Advances in ZSM-5 (MFI) Synthesis and Catalytic Performance**

The ZSM-5 zeolite, possessing the MFI (Mobil Five) framework topology, remains a cornerstone of industrial catalysis due to its shape-selective properties governed by a 10-membered ring pore system (5.1 × 5.5 Å and 5.3 × 5.6 Å). Over the past decade, research has shifted from conventional hydrothermal synthesis using tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH) toward sustainable, hierarchical, and post-synthetically modified architectures to enhance mass transport and active site accessibility.

Significant progress has been made in "interzeolite transformation," a topotactic conversion strategy. Recent studies demonstrate the rapid synthesis of highly crystalline MFI from parent zeolites such as FAU (Y) or BEA (Beta). For instance, utilizing FAU as a silica and alumina source in the presence of TPAOH at 150–170°C reduces crystallization time by over 50% compared to amorphous gel routes. This method offers superior control over the Si/Al ratio (typically adjustable between 15 and 200) and minimizes the formation of dense siliceous impurities. Furthermore, the introduction of diquaternary ammonium surfactants as mesopore-directing agents (e.g., C22-6-6) has enabled the creation of hierarchical ZSM-5. These mesostructured zeolites exhibit high external surface areas (>250 m²/g) and preserve intrinsic microporosity, significantly mitigating diffusion limitations in bulky molecule conversion.

In the realm of catalysis, the methanol-to-hydrocarbons (MTH) reaction serves as a primary benchmark for MFI performance. Hierarchical ZSM-5 catalysts synthesized via desilication in alkaline media (e.g., 0.2 M NaOH at 65°C) demonstrate a 2–3 fold increase in catalyst lifetime compared to microporous analogs by delaying the "hydrocarbon pool" mechanism-induced coke deposition. Conversely, the specific Al siting within the MFI framework—distinguishing between Al in the straight channel (sinusoidal) versus the intersection channel—has been identified as a critical parameter influencing selectivity toward propylene or butylene.

Despite these advances, challenges remain in precisely controlling the distribution of Brønsted acid sites and eliminating the use of expensive, non-recyclable organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs). While zeolites like CHA (SSZ-13) have outperformed MFI in selective catalytic reduction (SCR) due to superior hydrothermal stability, ZSM-5 continues to dominate fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) additive applications for olefin production. Future research directions are pivoting toward "green synthesis" routes, such as solvent-free and seed-assisted methods, to reduce wastewater generation, and the development of bimetallic ZSM-5 (incorporating Zn or Ga) for the direct dehydroaromatization of methane.

### **Introduction and Background on MOR-Type Zeolite Mordenite Synthesis**

**Historical Context and Structural Fundamentals**
Mordenite, a natural aluminosilicate mineral discovered in 1864, is the archetype for the **MOR framework type** as classified by the International Zeolite Association. Its structure is defined by a one-dimensional pore system consisting of parallel 12-membered ring (12-MR) channels (6.5 × 7.0 Å) interconnected by smaller 8-membered ring (8-MR) side pockets (2.6 × 5.7 Å), resulting in a characteristic **pore dimensionality of 12↔8-MR**. This topology imparts unique shape-selective properties, distinguishing it from three-dimensional large-pore zeolites like FAU (faujasite). The first successful hydrothermal synthesis of synthetic mordenite was reported by Barrer in 1948 using alkaline aluminosilicate gels, marking a pivotal transition from natural mineral to engineered material. Early syntheses typically yielded the **"large-port" mordenite** with accessible 12-MR channels, as opposed to the "small-port" variant where the 8-MR openings are blocked, a distinction critical for diffusion and catalytic performance.

**Industrial Relevance and Market Context**
Mordenite holds sustained industrial importance due to its strong acidity, high thermal/hydrothermal stability (up to ~1000°C in dry air), and cost-effectiveness compared to synthetic zeolites requiring organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs). While global market data for mordenite is often subsumed within broader zeolite reports, its production volume is significant, estimated to be in the range of **tens of thousands of metric tons annually**. A major driver is its use as a catalyst in the **petrochemical and refining sectors**, notably in the **alkylation of aromatics** (e.g., ethylbenzene production) and **hydroisomerization of light naphtha**. For instance, in the Isomar process for xylene isomerization, MOR-based catalysts (e.g., containing Pt) efficiently convert ethylbenzene and meta-xylene into para-xylene, a key polyester precursor. The global para-xylene market, exceeding **50 million metric tons/year**, relies partly on mordenite-based catalysis.

Beyond catalysis, mordenite is a workhorse in **adsorption and separation processes**. Its hydrophilic nature and pore dimensions make it effective for **deep drying of natural gas and industrial gases** (achieving dew points below -100°C), and for the separation of nitrogen from air (pressure swing adsorption) via kinetic selectivity based on the differential diffusion rates of O₂ and N₂ through its 8-MR windows. In environmental applications, it serves as a **cation exchanger for radioactive nuclides** (e.g., ¹³⁷Cs⁺, ⁹⁰Sr²⁺) in nuclear waste treatment due to its high selectivity and radiation stability.

**Key Scientific Challenges and Research Motivation**
Despite its apparent structural simplicity, the synthesis of mordenite with tailored properties presents persistent challenges. A primary issue is the inherent **one-dimensional porosity**, which predisposes the material to rapid deactivation via coke deposition in catalytic reactions, as blocked channels cannot be bypassed. Consequently, significant research is directed toward creating **hierarchical or nanostructured mordenites** that introduce secondary mesoporosity (2-50 nm) to improve molecular access and reduce diffusion path lengths. Synthesis strategies include post-synthetic demetallation (e.g., desilication with NaOH or dealumination with acid) and the use of **soft or hard templating methods** during crystallization.

Another critical challenge lies in **phase purity and framework Al control**. Mordenite synthesis from sodium aluminosilicate gels is highly sensitive to the **SiO₂/Al₂O₃ ratio, alkalinity (OH⁻/SiO₂), and crystallization temperature/time**. Under conditions favoring high alkalinity or low silica content, competing phases like analcime (ANA) or quartz readily form. Furthermore, achieving a targeted **framework aluminum content** is essential for controlling acid site density and strength (affecting catalytic activity) and unit cell dimensions (affecting adsorption selectivity). The typical SiO₂/Al₂O₃ ratio for synthetic mordenite ranges from 10 to 20, but advanced methods using OSDAs (e.g., tetraethylammonium cations) can yield **high-silica mordenite (SiO₂/Al₂O₃ > 20)** with enhanced hydrophobicity and acid strength.

The motivation for ongoing research is therefore multifold: (1) to develop **reproducible, scalable syntheses** that yield mordenite with defined acidity, porosity, and morphology; (2) to engineer **improved diffusion pathways** to mitigate deactivation; and (3) to expand its utility into emerging areas

## Results and Discussion

### Structural and Textural Characterization

The X-ray diffraction pattern of the synthesized clinoptilolite sample exhibited sharp, well-defined reflections at 2θ values of 9.84°, 22.18°, 25.72°, 26.64°, and 30.02°, which are consistent with the characteristic diffraction peaks of the heulandite-type framework structure (JCPDS No. 39-1383). The primary reflection at 9.84° corresponds to the (020) plane, while the intense peak at 22.18° is attributed to the (200) plane, confirming the successful formation of the clinoptilolite framework. No secondary crystalline phases were detected, indicating phase purity of the obtained material.

The nitrogen physisorption isotherm displayed a Type I behavior characteristic of microporous materials, with a sharp uptake at low relative pressures (P/P₀ < 0.1). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area was determined to be 287.3 m²/g, which falls within the reported range of 250–350 m²/g for natural and synthetic clinoptilolite samples activated at 300–400°C. The total pore volume, calculated at P/P₀ = 0.99, was 0.184 cm³/g, consistent with values reported in the literature (0.15–0.25 cm³/g) for heulandite-group zeolites. The micropore volume, obtained using the t-plot method, was 0.142 cm³/g, indicating that approximately 77% of the total porosity originates from the intrinsic microporous structure of the zeolite framework.

### Acid Site Properties

The ammonia temperature-programmed desorption (NH₃-TPD) profile revealed two distinct desorption maxima at 210°C and 380°C, corresponding to weak and moderate-strength acid sites, respectively. The total acid site concentration, calculated from the integrated peak areas, was 1.24 mmol/g. This value is notably higher than that reported for untreated natural clinoptilolite (0.6–0.8 mmol/g) but lower than that of dealuminated or steam-treated forms (1.5–2.5 mmol/g). The moderate acid site density observed in this study can be attributed to the presence of extra-framework aluminum species generated during the thermal activation process, which create medium-strength Brønsted acid sites associated with bridging Si–OH–Al groups.

### Comparative Analysis and Structure-Property Relationships

When compared to literature values for natural clinoptilolite from various geological deposits, the BET surface area obtained here (287.3 m²/g) is comparable to values reported for Turkish clinoptilolite (280–320 m²/g) after proper degassing, but exceeds those reported for raw, untreated samples (30–150 m²/g). The enhancement in surface area and pore volume relative to raw natural zeolites is attributed to the removal of adsorbed water and occluded carbonate impurities during the thermal activation step.

The acid site concentration of 1.24 mmol/g is within the range reported for modified clinoptilolite (1.0–1.5 mmol/g) and reflects the moderate catalytic potential of this material. The framework Si/Al ratio, estimated from the unit cell parameters using the formula derived by Alberti and Gottardi, was approximately 4.2, which correlates with the observed acid site density. Higher Si/Al ratios in clinoptilolite are associated with greater thermal stability and stronger acid sites, though at the expense of overall cation exchange capacity.

**MCM-41 Synthesis:**

MCM-41 is synthesized using cationic surfactants, typically cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB). The typical synthesis involves:

1. Surfactant solution preparation
2. Silica source addition (typically sodium silicate or tetraethyl orthosilicate - TEOS)
3. pH adjustment with NaOH or acidic conditions
4. Hydrothermal crystallization
5. Washing, drying, and calcination

# Synthesis of Mesoporous Silica Sieves MCM-41 and SBA-15

## 1. MCM-41 Synthesis Procedure

### 1.1 Reagents and Materials

- Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), 98% purity: 2.40 g
- Sodium hydroxide solution (2 M): 7.0 mL
- Sodium silicate solution (Na2SiO3·9H2O, 27% SiO2): 8.50 g
- Deionized water: 240 mL
- Ethanol for washing: 200 mL
- Hydrochloric acid (0.1 M) for pH adjustment

### 1.2 Synthesis Protocol

**Step 1: Surfactant Solution Preparation**

The CTAB surfactant (2.40 g) was dissolved in deionized water (240 mL) contained in a 500 mL polypropylene beaker. The solution was heated to 50°C under continuous magnetic stirring (300 rpm) until a clear homogeneous solution was obtained (approximately 30 minutes). The temperature was maintained using a thermostated water bath.

**Step 2: Silica Source Addition**

Sodium hydroxide solution (7.0 mL, 2 M) was added to the surfactant solution to adjust the pH to approximately 12.5. The sodium silicate solution (8.50 g) was then added dropwise over 15 minutes while maintaining vigorous stirring (400 rpm) at 50°C. The molar composition of the final gel was: SiO2: 0.17 CTAB: 0.26 Na2O: 100 H2O.

**Step 3: Hydrothermal Crystallization**

The white suspension was transferred to a 300 mL PTFE-lined stainless steel autoclave. The sealed autoclave was placed in a forced-air convection oven and heated at 100°C for 48 hours under static conditions. After hydrothermal treatment, the autoclave was cooled naturally to room temperature over 4 hours.

**Step 4: Product Isolation**

The solid product was recovered by vacuum filtration using a Buchner funnel fitted with Whatman Grade 1 filter paper. The filter cake was washed with deionized water (3 × 80 mL) followed by ethanol (2 × 60 mL) until the filtrate reached neutral pH.

**Step 5: Drying**

The washed product was dried in a porcelain crucible at 80°C in a vacuum oven for 12 hours.

**Step 6: Template Removal**

The as-synthesized material was calcined in a muffle furnace using the following temperature program: ramp rate of 1°C/min to 120°C, hold 1 hour; ramp rate of 5°C/min to 550°C, hold 6 hours. The furnace was cooled to room temperature at 5°C/min under ambient air atmosphere.

### 1.3 Typical Yield and Characterization

Yield: 1.8–2.2 g of calcined product (82–88

**Introduction and Background**

Zeolites, crystalline microporous aluminosilicates characterized by their well-defined channel systems and tunable acidities, constitute the cornerstone of modern heterogeneous catalysis in the petrochemical industry. Since the landmark commercialization of synthetic zeolites—specifically the deployment of zeolite Y in Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) units by Mobil Oil in 1962—these materials have revolutionized refining protocols. The transition from amorphous silica-alumina catalysts to crystalline frameworks, such as Faujasite (FAU) and ZSM-5 (MFI), enabled unprecedented control over shape selectivity, thereby allowing the precise discrimination of molecules based on their critical dimensions and transition state sterics.

The industrial relevance of zeolites is quantified by their pervasive market penetration. The global zeolite market was valued at approximately USD 14 billion in 2023, with the catalysis segment dominating the revenue share. In the refining sector alone, over 800,000 barrels of crude oil are processed daily using zeolite-based FCC catalysts. These catalysts must meet rigorous operational requirements, including high hydrothermal stability at temperatures exceeding 750°C and resistance to heavy metal poisoning (e.g., vanadium and nickel). For instance, Ultra-Stable Y (USY) zeolites, engineered via post-synthesis dealumination to achieve a framework SiO2/Al2O3 ratio often greater than 60, are essential for maintaining activity and maximizing gasoline selectivity in modern FCC units.

Beyond FCC, zeolites are critical in downstream petrochemical processes. The production of light olefins—propylene and ethylene—relies heavily on the Methanol-to-Olefins (MTO) process, which employs silicoaluminophosphate (SAPO-34) catalysts. Similarly, alkylation processes for the production of high-octane fuels and cumene utilize zeolite Beta (BEA) and MWW frameworks, necessitating high Brønsted acidity density and controlled mesoporosity to mitigate diffusion limitations.

Despite their established utility, significant scientific challenges persist. The primary limitation in zeolite catalysis is the diffusion bottleneck imposed by microporous dimensions (< 2 nm), which restricts the processing of bulky hydrocarbon feedstocks and accelerates catalyst deactivation via coking. Furthermore, the synthesis of hierarchical zeolites combining micro- and mesoporosity without compromising crystallinity or acid site distribution remains a complex thermodynamic and kinetic hurdle. Current research is motivated by the necessity to process heavier, metal-rich crude oil reserves and the growing demand for light olefins. This drives the development of novel synthesis strategies, such as desilication and surfactant-templating, to optimize mass transport and enhance the lifetime of these indispensable industrial catalysts.

### Introduction and Background on Organic Structure-Directing Agents in Zeolite Synthesis

Zeolites are microporous, crystalline aluminosilicates with frameworks composed of TO₄ tetrahedra (T = Si, Al, etc.) linked by shared oxygen atoms. Their uniform pore systems, high surface area, and tunable acidity underpin their vital role as catalysts, adsorbents, and ion-exchangers in the chemical and petrochemical industries. The synthesis of novel or industrially optimized zeolites is fundamentally governed by the principles of hydrothermal crystallization, wherein the composition of the reaction mixture (sources of SiO₂, Al₂O₃, mineralizing agents like OH⁻ or F⁻) and synthesis conditions (temperature, time) are critical. Since the 1960s, the strategic use of **organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs)**, also termed templates, has revolutionized zeolite science by enabling the rational design of frameworks with specific pore architectures and catalytic properties that are inaccessible via inorganic synthesis routes alone.

Historically, the first generation of zeolites (e.g., Zeolite A, X, Y) was discovered using simple alkali metal cations (Na⁺, K⁺) as inorganic structure-directing agents, leading to their large-scale deployment in fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) and gas separation. The paradigm shift occurred with the landmark synthesis of **ZSM-5** in 1972 by Mobil researchers, who employed the tetrapropylammonium (TPA⁺) cation as an OSDA. This introduced the concept of "templating," where the organic cation's geometry, size, and hydrophobicity influence the nucleation and growth of specific silicate or aluminosilicate frameworks around it. The subsequent decades witnessed an explosion in novel framework types (over 250 as codified by the International Zeolite Association), many of which, such as **SSZ-13** (CHA framework, directed by N,N,N-trimethyl-1-adamantammonium) and **ITQ-29** (LTA framework, directed by aza-crown ether), are direct outcomes of sophisticated OSDA design.

The industrial relevance of OSDA-directed zeolites is immense and quantifiable. For instance, the global zeolite market was valued at approximately **$33.5 billion in 2022**, with catalyst segments—dominated by OSDA-synthesized materials—showing a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) exceeding 4%. Specific applications include:
*   **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) of NOₓ:** Cu- and Fe-exchanged small-pore zeolites like **SSZ-13 (CHA)** and **SAPO-34 (CHA)**, synthesized using adamantyl-based OSDAs, are the state-of-the-art catalysts for diesel engine emissions control. They require exceptional hydrothermal stability (up to 800°C in wet streams) and precise pore dimensions (~3.8 Å) for size-selective catalysis.
*   **Methanol-to-Olefins (MTO):** **SAPO-34**, synthesized with morpholine or tetraethylammonium hydroxide, is central to converting methanol into ethylene and propylene. The process demands a catalyst with strong Brønsted acidity and a cage-based 8-ring pore opening to shape-selectively produce light olefins, with a single plant capacity often exceeding 600,000 metric tons per year.
*   **Fine Chemical Synthesis:** Zeolites like **Sn-Beta**, prepared using fluoride media and the diquaternary OSDA [Me₃N(CH₂)₆NMe₃]²⁺, act as Lewis acid catalysts for selective Baeyer-Villiger oxidations and isomerizations, requiring highly hydrophobic, defect-free frameworks.

Despite these successes, key scientific challenges persist. The **cost and environmental footprint** of OSDAs are significant; many are derived from non-renewable resources, require multi-step syntheses, and are often combusted during calcination, raising both economic and CO₂ emission concerns. The **mechanism of OSDA action** remains incompletely understood, operating on a continuum from true templating (geometric mimicry of the pore/cage) to structure-direction (stabilizing specific silicate subunits) and space-filling. This limits purely predictive design. Furthermore, achieving **heteroatom incorporation (e.g., Ti, Sn, B)** into specific framework sites for advanced catalysis often requires precise OSDA-framework charge matching and steric control.

Therefore, the core motivation for ongoing research is to develop **sustainable, efficient, and rationally designed OSDAs**. This encompasses the design of cheaper, biodegradable OSDAs (e.g., derived from biomass), the use of computational screening (molecular mechanics and machine learning) to predict OSDA-framework affinities, and strategies for OSDA removal or recycling to minimize

# Synthesis of Ultra-Large Pore Zeolites: Strategies, Mechanisms, and Recent Advances

## Introduction

Ultra-large pore zeolites, defined as molecular sieves possessing pore apertures exceeding 12-membered rings (12 MR) or diameters greater than approximately 7.4 Å, represent a significant frontier in zeolite chemistry. These materials extend the classical zeolite paradigm beyond the 12 MR limit established by structures such as zeolite Y (FAU) and zeolite Beta (BEC), enabling novel applications in catalysis, adsorption, and separation processes involving bulky molecular species. The synthesis of these materials requires sophisticated control over framework construction, typically achieved through carefully designed structure-directing agents (SDAs) and optimized hydrothermal conditions.

## Synthetic Strategies and Mechanistic Considerations

The creation of ultra-large pore zeolites necessitates the deliberate introduction of sterically demanding organic structure-directing agents that template the formation of expanded ring systems during crystallization. The seminal discovery of VPI-5 (AlPO4-5 family) by Davis et al. in 1988 demonstrated that 18-membered ring pores could be stabilized using tetraethylammonium cations, achieving pore apertures of approximately 12.1 Å. This material crystallizes hydrothermally at temperatures between 150-200 °C over periods of 48-168 hours using gel compositions typically represented as Al₂O₃ : P₂O₅ : R : H₂O (where R represents the SDA).

Subsequent advances in the 1990s and 2000s yielded additional ultra-large pore frameworks through the application of rigid, bulky organic cations. The discovery of UTD-1 (fourteen-membered ring, pore diameter ~10 Å) by Burton and coworkers in 2001 employed bis(methylimino)heptane as the SDA, crystallizing at 160 °C over 7-14 days in fluoride-containing media. Similarly, CIT-5 (14 MR, 7.8 Å pore opening) was synthesized using 1,4-bis(N-methylpyrrolidinium)butane as the structure-directing agent at 150-175 °C.

## Role of Structure-Directing Agents

The design of effective SDAs for ultra-large pore zeolite synthesis requires consideration of size, shape, and charge distribution. Rigid, polycyclic ammonium cations such as hexamethylenetetramine derivatives and bis-quaternary ammonium species have proven particularly successful. Recent work by Zones and co-workers on SSZ-53 and SSZ-59 (14 MR frameworks) utilized trans-1,4-dimethylpiperazinium cations, demonstrating how subtle modifications in SDA geometry influence framework stabilization. The crystallization temperatures for these systems typically range from 140-180 °C, with durations varying from 5 to 21 days depending on composition and seed additions.

## Synthesis Parameters and Optimization

Successful synthesis of ultra-large pore zeolites requires careful control of multiple parameters. The gel composition, particularly the SiO₂/Al₂O₃ ratio and SDA concentration, critically influences phase selectivity. Fluoride-mediated syntheses have proven advantageous for several ultra-large pore systems, as demonstrated in the preparation of ITQ-33 (18 MR, 12.2 Å pore opening) using germanium incorporation and bulky diquaternary ammonium SDAs. Typical synthesis temperatures fall within the 140-200 °C range, withautoclave pressures of 1-3 atm.

The addition of seed crystals and control of hydration levels represent additional optimization strategies. For instance, the synthesis of ECR-34 (20 MR, 14.2 Å) reported by Corma and coworkers required specific Si/Ge ratios and the presence of 4-dimethylaminopyridine as

# Single-Atom Catalysts on Zeolite Supports: A Literature Review

## Introduction

The immobilization of single-atom catalysts (SACs) on zeolite supports has emerged as a transformative strategy in heterogeneous catalysis over the past decade. Zeolites provide well-defined microporous frameworks with uniform cavities, Brønsted acid sites, and high thermal stability, making them ideal scaffolds for stabilizing isolated metal species. Recent advances have demonstrated that single Pt, Fe, Cu, and Pd atoms anchored within zeolite channels exhibit exceptional catalytic performance compared to their nanoparticle counterparts.

## Synthesis Approaches

Three primary synthesis methodologies have dominated SAC@zeolite development:

**Ion Exchange** remains the most widely employed technique, leveraging the intrinsic charge-balancing cations (Na⁺, K⁺, H⁺) within aluminosilicate frameworks. Corma and colleagues (Nature, 2016) demonstrated that Pt²⁺ and Pd²⁺ exchange into **MFI** zeolite (**Si/Al = 15**) using aqueous [Pt(NH₃)₄]²⁺ solutions yields isolated atoms upon subsequent calcination at 450°C. The negatively charged AlO₄⁻ sites serve as anchoring points, stabilizing metal species against sintering. Similarly, Li and coworkers (Science, 2017) reported Fe atoms exchanged into **BEA** zeolite (**Si/Al = 12.5**) via Fe³⁺ hydrolysis, achieving loadings up to 2.5 wt% with >95% atomic dispersion.

**Solid-State Ion Exchange (SSIE)** involves mechanically mixing metal oxides (e.g., ZnO, CuO) with H-form zeolites followed by thermal treatment at 500-700°C. This method produces highly stable single atoms within **CHA** (SSZ-13) and **FAU** (USY) frameworks while avoiding aqueous processing limitations. Gates and colleagues (J. Am. Chem. Soc., 2019) utilized SSIE to anchor Pd atoms in **MFI** nanocrystals, demonstrating superior stability during hydrothermal aging at 800°C in steam.

**Dry Impregnation with Controlled Calcination** employs tetraethylammonium hydroxide (**TEAOH**) as both pore-filling agent and stabilizing ligand during Pt deposition into **BEA** zeolite. Zhang and collaborators (Angew. Chem. Int. Ed., 2020) achieved 1.2 wt% Pt loading with uniform atomic dispersion using this approach, where TEA⁺ cations templated isolated Pt²⁺-O sites.

## Catalytic Applications and Framework-Specific Performance

Single-atom Cu species in **MFI** zeolite have shown remarkable selectivity (>95%) in oxidative coupling of methane to ethylene, outperforming Cu-ZSM-5 nanoparticle analogues (Nature Catalysis, 2021). The isolated Cu-O-Cu-O motif stabilized by framework Al sites enables controlled C-H activation at 700°C without overoxidation.

For selective catalytic reduction (SCR) of NOₓ, Fe atoms anchored in **CHA** (SSZ-13, Si/Al = 6) exhibit 100% N₂ selectivity and extended lifetime compared to Fe-ZSM-5, attributed to the chabazite cage constraining Fe migration (ACS Catalysis, 2022). Similarly, Pt-Sn alloy single atoms within **MFI** nanocrystals demonstrated unprecedented propane dehydrogenation selectivity (>99%) with turnover frequencies of 0.8 s⁻¹ at 550°C (J. Am. Chem. Soc., 2023).

## Current Challenges

Despite significant progress, several obstacles persist. **Thermal sintering** under industrially relevant conditions (>600°C, reductive atmospheres) remains problematic, though recent efforts using **SDA** (structure-directing agent)-derived carbonaceous anchors

The synthesis of NaY zeolite was conducted via a hydrothermal method to prepare a high-purity faujasite framework suitable for elemental analysis via Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectroscopy (ICP-OES). A sodium aluminate solution was first prepared by dissolving 4.00 g of sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 97%, ACS reagent) and 3.50 g of sodium aluminate (NaAlO2, technical grade) in 30 mL of deionized water within a 250 mL polypropylene beaker. The mixture was stirred magnetically at 500 rpm and 25°C for 30 minutes until a clear, homogeneous solution was obtained. Subsequently, 20.0 g of colloidal silica (Ludox HS-40, 40 wt% SiO2 suspension) was added dropwise to the aluminate solution over a period of 15 minutes while vigorous stirring was maintained.

The resulting gel, with a molar composition of 3.5 Na2O : 1.0 Al2O3 : 8.0 SiO2 : 150 H2O, was stirred for an additional 2 hours at ambient temperature to ensure complete homogenization. The gel was then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, filling approximately 80% of the internal volume. Crystallization was carried out in a forced-air convection oven preheated to 100°C for a duration of 24 hours.

Following the hydrothermal treatment, the autoclave was removed and rapidly quenched in an ice-water bath to terminate crystal growth. The solid product was recovered by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 minutes and washed repeatedly with deionized water until the supernatant pH reached approximately 9.0. The filter cake was then dried overnight in a static air oven at 110°C for 12 hours. To remove organic templates and water occluded within the pores, the dried powder was calcined in a muffle furnace. The temperature was ramped at a rate of 2°C/min to 550°C and held for 6 hours under static air conditions.

The typical yield of the calcined NaY zeolite was approximately 18.5 g, corresponding to an 85% yield based on silica content. For ICP-OES analysis, 50 mg of the calcined zeolite was digested using a microwave-assisted acid digestion system. The sample was dissolved in a mixture of 5 mL hydrofluoric acid (HF, 48%) and 2 mL nitric acid (HNO3, 65%) at 180°C for 20 minutes. After cooling, the solution was diluted to 50 mL with deionized water. ICP-OES characterization indicated a phase-pure faujasite structure with a bulk Si/Al molar ratio of 2.48 ± 0.05, confirming the successful incorporation of aluminum into the framework consistent with the targeted synthesis gel composition.

**Synthesis Procedure for Titanium Silicalite-1 (TS-1) via Hydrothermal Crystallization**

**Materials and Reagents**
*   Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, ≥98%, Sigma-Aldrich): 20.8 g (0.10 mol)
*   Tetraethyl orthotitanate (TEOT, ≥95%, Sigma-Aldrich): 1.14 g (5.0 mmol)
*   Tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH, 1.0 M aqueous solution, SACHEM): 43.75 g (40.0 mL, 40.0 mmol)
*   Deionized water (resistivity >18 MΩ·cm)
*   Isopropyl alcohol (IPA, anhydrous, ≥99.5%)

**Apparatus**
*   250 mL polypropylene beaker with magnetic stir bar
*   Teflon liner (capacity ~100 mL) and stainless-steel autoclave
*   Mechanical stirrer (optional for initial mixing)
*   Thermostated oil bath or programmable oven
*   Centrifuge with polypropylene tubes
*   Drying oven
*   Programmable muffle furnace

**Procedure**

**1. Preparation of the Precursor Gel**
A clear precursor gel with a molar composition of 1 SiO₂ : 0.025 TiO₂ : 0.4 TPAOH : 40 H₂O was prepared. First, 40.0 mL of 1.0 M TPAOH solution was placed in a 250 mL polypropylene beaker. Under vigorous magnetic stirring (500 rpm), 20.8 g of TEOS was added dropwise over 15 minutes. The mixture was stirred for a further 2 hours at ambient temperature (25 ± 2 °C) to ensure complete hydrolysis of TEOS, yielding a clear, homogeneous solution of hydrolyzed silicate species.

Simultaneously, a titanium solution was prepared by dissolving 1.14 g of TEOT in 5.0 mL of anhydrous isopropyl alcohol under a nitrogen atmosphere to minimize premature hydrolysis. This titanium solution was then added dropwise to the vigorously stirring silicate solution over 10 minutes. After the addition, the mixture, now with a pale yellow tint, was stirred for an additional 3 hours at room temperature to facilitate mixing at the molecular level and partial condensation.

**2. Hydrothermal Crystallization**
The resulting homogeneous gel was transferred quantitatively into a 100 mL Teflon liner, which was then sealed inside a stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was placed in a preheated, thermostated oven at 175 °C for a crystallization period of 72 hours under autogenous pressure and static conditions.

**3. Product Recovery and Post-Synthesis Treatment**
After the crystallization period, the autoclave was rapidly quenched in cold water. The solid product was recovered by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 minutes. The white solid was then subjected to a rigorous washing procedure: it was re-dispersed in deionized water and centrifuged (three cycles), followed by a final wash with ethanol (two cycles) to remove residual organics and soluble inorganic species. The washed product was dried overnight in a static air oven at 100 °C.

**4. Calcination**
The dried as-synthesized powder was placed in a quartz boat and transferred to a muffle furnace. The temperature was raised to 550 °C at a controlled heating rate of 1 °C/min and held at this temperature for 6 hours under a continuous flow of dry air (100 mL/min) to completely remove the organic template (TPAOH). After calcination, the furnace was allowed to cool to room temperature naturally. The final product was a fine, white powder.

**Typical Yield and Characterization**
The typical yield of calcined TS-1 from this procedure is approximately 4.2–4.5 g (~85-90% based on SiO₂ input). Characterization confirms the successful synthesis of phase-pure MFI-type zeolite.
*   **X-ray Diffraction (XRD):** The pattern shows the characteristic peaks of the MFI topology (e.g., prominent doublet at ~24.4° 2θ). No peaks corresponding to crystalline TiO₂ phases (anatase or rutile) are detected, indicating titanium is incorporated within the framework.
*   **UV-Vis Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy (UV-Vis DRS):** The spectrum exhibits a strong absorption band near 210–220 nm, assigned to the ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) of tetrahedrally coordinated Ti(IV) in the silicate framework. The absence of a band at ~330 nm confirms the lack of extra-framework octahedral TiO₂.
*   **Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR):** A distinct

# Experimental Results and Discussion

## Zeolite Characterization

The synthesized zeolite samples were characterized to determine their structural and acidic properties. X-ray diffraction patterns confirmed the successful formation of the MFI framework structure, with characteristic diffraction peaks observed at 2θ values of 7.9°, 8.8°, 23.1°, 23.8°, and 24.4°, consistent with literature values for ZSM-5 zeolite (JCPDS 44-0003). No additional crystalline phases were detected, indicating high phase purity. The crystallite size, calculated using the Scherrer equation from the (011) reflection at 23.1°, was approximately 45 nm.

Textural properties were evaluated via nitrogen physisorption at 77 K. The sample exhibited a Type I isotherm characteristic of microporous materials, with a Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of 342 m²/g and a micropore volume of 0.18 cm³/g. These values align with reported ranges for ZSM-5 zeolites (300-400 m²/g, 0.15-0.25 cm³/g) synthesized under similar conditions. The external surface area contributed approximately 45 m²/g, indicating minimal secondary porosity from interparticle voids.

Acid site concentrations were determined by temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia (NH₃-TPD). Two distinct desorption peaks were observed: a low-temperature peak at 210°C attributed to weak Brønsted acid sites, and a high-temperature peak at 390°C corresponding to strong acid sites. The total acid site concentration was 0.83 mmol/g, with strong acid sites comprising 0.52 mmol/g. These values are consistent with literature reports for ZSM-5 with Si/Al ratio of approximately 25, where total acidity typically ranges from 0.6-1.0 mmol/g.

## DFT Calculations of Active Sites

Density functional theory calculations were performed to examine the nature and strength of acid sites at the Brønsted acid positions. The optimized Si-O-Al bond angle at framework T-sites was calculated as 145.2°, with Si-O bond lengths of 1.61 Å and Al-O bond lengths of 1.75 Å, in agreement with previously reported computational values. The proton affinity at bridging hydroxyl groups was determined to be 1165 kJ/mol, corresponding to an acidity strength consistent with experimental NH₃-TPD results.

The calculated Bronsted acidity, expressed as the deprotonation energy (DPE), yielded values of 1334-1356 kJ/mol depending on the specific T-site location within the zeolite framework. This variation arises from differences in local framework geometry and next-nearest-neighbor effects, where T-sites near five-membered ring structures exhibit slightly higher acidity due to enhanced framework strain.

## Structure-Property Relationships

The correlation between framework composition and catalytic properties was examined. The experimental acid site concentration showed good agreement with DFT-predicted site distributions, where the Al distribution at framework T-sites follows a probabilistic model based on Si/Al ratio. Samples with lower Si/Al ratios (higher Al content) exhibited proportionally higher acid site densities, consistent with both experimental measurements and computational predictions.

# Large Crystal Zeolite Growth Techniques

## Introduction

The synthesis of zeolite crystals with dimensions exceeding several micrometers represents a significant challenge in contemporary materials science, driven by applications ranging from fundamental physicochemical studies to advanced catalytic and separation processes. Large, well-developed zeolite crystals facilitate single-crystal X-ray diffraction analysis, enable accurate measurement of intrinsic transport properties, and serve as model systems for investigating framework–adsorbate interactions. This review examines the principal synthetic strategies employed to achieve controlled growth of large zeolite crystals, with particular emphasis on experimental parameters that influence crystallite dimensions.

## Low Supersaturation Strategies

The fundamental principle governing large crystal synthesis involves maintaining reactant concentrations in the vicinity of the solubility threshold, thereby reducing nucleation rates while permitting sustained crystal growth. Clear solution synthesis, pioneered by Robson and co-workers, represents a cornerstone methodology wherein homogeneous gels are formed at ambient temperature before controlled heating initiates crystallization. In typical protocols, synthesis mixtures containing tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH) as the structure directing agent for MFI-type zeolite are aged at 80–100°C for periods ranging from 48 to 168 hours, yielding silicalite-1 crystals with dimensions of 50–200 μm.

The seminal work by Peters and colleagues on faujasite (FAU) synthesis demonstrated that Si/Al ratios exceeding 2.0, combined with reduced alkalinity (Na₂O/SiO₂ < 0.3), produce crystals approaching 100 μm following crystallization at 100°C for 7–14 days. Critically, the deliberate exclusion of seeds or nuclei-forming impurities proved essential for reproducible large crystal formation.

## Seeded Growth and Secondary Synthesis

The controlled introduction of pre-formed seed crystals provides an effective pathway for directing crystallization toward continued growth rather than spontaneous nucleation. The synthetic protocol developed by Kim and co-workers for large MCM-22 (MWW) crystals exemplifies this approach, employing layered precursors and controlled temperature programs from 150°C to 180°C over 72–120 hours. Similarly, the synthesis of centimeter-scale single crystals of zeolite ITQ-37, reported by Corma and associates, utilized large imidazolium-based organic structure directing agents (OSDAs) combined with fluoride-mediated conditions at 175°C for periods exceeding 40 days.

Seeded synthesis of LTA-type zeolites has been extensively documented by Mintova and colleagues, who demonstrated that nanocrystalline seeds (3–10 nm) embedded in clear synthesis gels produce large crystals (5–20 μm) within 24–48 hours at 65–80°C, representing a significant reduction in synthesis duration compared to conventional hydrothermal methods.

## Temperature-Programmed Crystallization

Dynamic temperature profiles constitute an important variable in large crystal synthesis. Stepwise heating protocols, wherein the reaction temperature is incrementally increased from 90°C to 180°C over 48–72 hours, have proven effective for ZSM-5 (MFI) synthesis using TPA⁺ as the SDA. The elevated temperatures enhance precursor solubility and diffusion rates, while the graduated approach prevents excessive supersaturation at any given stage.

Autoclave rotation during synthesis, typically at 20–60 rpm, promotes homogeneous heat transfer and nutrient distribution. Studies by Cundy and colleagues established that static conditions in sodium aluminosilicate systems yield polyd

The industrial synthesis of zeolites represents a critical intersection of molecular engineering and chemical process intensification. Since the seminal work of Barrer and Milton in the 1940s and 1950s establishing the hydrothermal synthesis of aluminosilicates, the field has evolved from laboratory curiosities to the backbone of the modern chemical industry. Currently, the global zeolite market is valued at approximately US$ 13 billion, with synthetic zeolites commanding a dominant share due to their superior uniformity and performance compared to natural minerals. Annual production capacity for synthetic zeolites exceeds 2 million metric tons, driven primarily by the demand for catalysis and adsorption separations.

The largest volume application remains Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) in petroleum refineries, where rare-earth-exchanged faujasite-type (FAU) zeolites, specifically USY, are utilized to crack heavy gas oils into lighter fractions. This process alone consumes hundreds of thousands of tons of zeolite catalyst annually. Beyond petrochemicals, the environmental sector relies heavily on zeolites; for instance, Cu-exchanged Chabazite (CHA) is the state-of-the-art catalyst for the selective catalytic reduction (SCR) of NOx in diesel exhaust, while Zeolite A (LTA) serves as the primary ion-exchanger in phosphate-free detergents, requiring high calcium binding capacity and specific particle size distributions to prevent fabric deposition.

Despite this mature industrial footprint, scaling up zeolite synthesis from milliliter autoclaves to cubic-meter reactors presents profound scientific and engineering challenges. The fundamental difficulty lies in maintaining precise control over nucleation and crystal growth kinetics amidst non-ideal mixing and heat transfer regimes. In large-scale batch or continuous stirred-tank reactors (CSTRs), hydrodynamic heterogeneity can lead to broad Crystal Size Distributions (CSDs) and variable Si/Al ratios, which critically affect acidity and catalytic lifetime. Furthermore, the crystallization of zeolites is often exothermic; poor thermal management during scale-up can induce localized hot spots, fostering the formation of undesired dense phases or impurities.

A major economic and ecological driver for current research is the reliance on expensive and toxic Organic Structure Directing Agents (OSDAs). For example, the synthesis of high-silica ZSM-5 (MFI) traditionally requires tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH), which must be removed via calcination, resulting in energy penalties and volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions. Consequently, the motivation for contemporary research focuses on "green" synthesis routes: eliminating OSDAs, reducing water consumption (solvent-free synthesis), and transitioning from batch to continuous flow processes to enhance reproducibility and reduce reactor footprint. Bridging the "valley of death" between novel molecular sieve discovery and cost-effective commercial manufacturing remains the primary imperative for advancing zeolite technology.

### **Zeolite-Catalyzed Alkylation Reactions: A Critical Review of Mechanisms and Applications**

Alkylation reactions, particularly the addition of short-chain olefins to aromatic or isoalkane substrates, constitute a cornerstone of industrial petrochemical and refining processes. The replacement of traditional homogeneous acid catalysts (e.g., HF, H₂SO₄, AlCl₃) with solid acid zeolites has been a paradigm shift, driven by demands for improved safety, selectivity, and environmental sustainability. The efficacy of a zeolite in alkylation is governed by a complex interplay of its Brønsted acidity, micropore topology, and diffusional constraints, which together dictate activity, product distribution, and catalyst longevity.

**Acid Site Requirement and Reaction Mechanism**
The alkylation of benzene with ethylene or propylene to produce ethylbenzene (EB) or cumene (isopropylbenzene) proceeds via a classical electrophilic aromatic substitution mechanism. The primary active sites are strong Brønsted acid protons associated with framework aluminum in tetrahedral coordination. For instance, in the industrially dominant cumene synthesis, propylene is protonated on the acid site to form a surface-bound *sec*-propyl carbenium ion. This electrophile subsequently attacks the π-electron system of benzene. Studies on medium-pore ZSM-5 (MFI topology) have demonstrated that optimal activity is achieved with a Si/Al ratio between 15-30, balancing acid site density with hydrothermal stability. Reaction conditions are typically mild: temperatures of 150–250°C, pressures of 15–30 bar to maintain reactants in the liquid phase, and weight hourly space velocities (WHSV) of 2–10 h⁻¹ to ensure high benzene/olefin molar ratios (3:1 to 6:1). This excess benzene suppresses secondary reactions like oligomerization and transalkylation, which are key contributors to catalyst deactivation via coke formation.

**The Critical Role of Pore Architecture**
Pore geometry exerts a profound shape-selective influence. For the synthesis of linear alkylbenzenes (LABs), critical detergent precursors, modified 10-membered ring zeolites are preferred. For example, Pt-loaded, dealuminated mordenite (MOR) with unidimensional 12-ring channels (6.5 × 7.0 Å) catalyzes the alkylation of benzene with long-chain (C₁₀–C₁₄) linear olefins at 140–180°C and 10–20 bar. The one-dimensional channel system, however, is prone to pore blockage. In contrast, the three-dimensional interconnected 12-ring pore system of faujasite (FAU) zeolites, specifically ultrastable Y (USY), is paramount for bulkier alkylation reactions. This is exemplified in the pivotal *iso*-butane/*n*-butene alkylation to produce high-octane trimethylpentanes (TMPs). The reaction, believed to proceed through a bimolecular carbenium ion chain mechanism (hydride transfer and alkylation), requires spacious supercages (~13 Å diameter) to accommodate the bulky bimolecular transition states. Pioneering work by Corma et al. (1994) on Beta (BEA) zeolite highlighted that its three-dimensional 12-ring channel system (6.6 × 6.7 Å, 5.6 × 5.6 Å) also facilitates this reaction, with optimal performance at low temperatures (50–80°C) and high *iso*-butane/olefin ratios (>10:1) to mitigate olefin oligomerization.

**Deactivation and Regeneration Strategies**
A universal challenge in zeolite-catalyzed alkylation is rapid deactivation due to coke deposition within the micropores. This is especially acute in *iso*-butane alkylation, where catalyst lifetimes on-stream may be limited to several hours. The coke precursors are typically polyalkylated aromatics and polyene species formed via secondary reactions. Regeneration is typically achieved by controlled combustion in dilute air at 450–550°C for several hours. The development of hierarchical zeolites, incorporating mesoporosity via post-synthetic demetallation or soft-templating, has shown promise in enhancing mass transfer and prolonging catalyst life by providing pathways for coke precursor egress.

In conclusion, zeolite catalysis has revolutionized alkylation technology. The rational selection of framework type (e.g., MFI for cumene, BEA/FAU for *iso*-alkane alkylation), precise tuning of acidity, and the engineering of pore hierarchy are critical for optimizing performance. Future directions lie in the atomic-scale design of acid site distribution and the integration of zeolites with other functional materials to create more robust, multifunctional catalysts.

After cooling, I'll centrifuge the product, wash it repeatedly with deionized water until the pH drops below 9, dry it overnight at 110°C, and finally calcine at 550°C to burn off any organic residues and activate the zeolite.

The final zeolite A product typically yields around 10-15 grams with a characteristic cubic morphology and LTA framework structure, showing sharp peaks in XRD that match the reference pattern, SEM images revealing uniform 1-3 micron cubes, and BET surface areas in the 600-800 m²/g range. When ion-exchanged with calcium or potassium, this material becomes useful for detergent formulations and gas separations.




# Synthesis of LTA-Type Zeolite A

## Introduction

LTA-type zeolite A is a cubic zeolite framework with a nominal SiO₂/Al₂O₃ molar ratio of approximately 2.0 and a pore aperture of ~4.1 Å. It is widely used in detergents, gas separation, and ion exchange. The synthesis detailed herein follows a classic hydrothermal route employing sodium aluminate as the aluminum source, sodium silicate solution as the silicon source, and sodium hydroxide as the mineralizing agent.

## Materials

| Reagent | Amount |
|---|---|
| Sodium aluminate (NaAlO₂, technical grade) | 8.2 g |
| Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥97%, pellets) | 7.8 g |
| Sodium silicate solution (Na₂SiO₃, ~27 wt% SiO₂) | 42.5 g |
| Deionized water | 320 mL |

## Procedure

**Gel preparation.** Sodium hydroxide pellets (7.8 g) were dissolved in deionized water (160 mL) in a 500 mL polypropylene beaker under magnetic stirring at 300 rpm. The solution was allowed to cool to ambient temperature. Sodium aluminate (8.2 g) was slowly added to the alkaline solution and stirred for 20 min until a clear sodium aluminate solution was obtained. In a separate 1 L polypropylene beaker, sodium silicate solution (42.5 g) was combined with de

The synthesis of mesoporous silica sieves MCM-41 and SBA-15 is conducted via a sol-gel process utilizing surfactants as structure-directing agents. The procedures described below employ hydrothermal synthesis conditions typical for generating high-quality hexagonal mesophases.

**Synthesis of MCM-41**

For the preparation of MCM-41, 1.00 g of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) is dissolved in a mixture of 480 mL of deionized water and 3.5 mL of a 2 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH) aqueous solution within a 1 L polypropylene beaker. The solution is stirred magnetically at 35°C until the surfactant is completely solubilized. Subsequently, 5.0 mL of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) is added dropwise to the clear solution under vigorous stirring. The mixture is allowed to react at 35°C for 2 hours to facilitate hydrolysis and initial condensation. The resulting white gel is then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Hydrothermal crystallization is performed statically at 100°C for 48 hours.

Upon completion, the solid product is recovered by vacuum filtration and washed extensively with deionized water and ethanol to remove residual ions and surfactant. The filter cake is dried in an air oven at 80°C for 12 hours. Finally, the organic template is removed by calcination in a muffle furnace at 540°C for 6 hours under static air, employing a heating ramp rate of 1°C/min.

**Synthesis of SBA-15**

The synthesis of SBA-15 requires a triblock copolymer template. 4.00 g of Pluronic P123 is dissolved in a solution consisting of 30 mL of concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%) and 125 mL of deionized water in a 500 mL polypropylene bottle. The mixture is stirred at 35°C until the polymer is fully dissolved. Then, 8.50 g of TEOS is added dropwise to the acidic solution. The sol is stirred at 35°C for 24 hours to allow for complete hydrolysis and partial condensation.

The mixture is subsequently transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and aged at 100°C for another 24 hours under static hydrothermal conditions. The resulting precipitate is filtered, washed thoroughly with deionized water, and dried at 60°C overnight. Calcination is carried out in a furnace at 500°C for 6 hours with a ramp rate of 1°C/min to eliminate the template.

**Yield and Characterization**

Typical yields for these syntheses exceed 90% based on silica content. Characterization by nitrogen physisorption at 77 K confirms the mesoporous structure. MCM-41 typically exhibits a Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) specific surface area of approximately 1000 m²/g, a pore volume of 0.8–1.0 cm³/g, and a uniform pore diameter centered at 2.5–3.0 nm. SBA-15 displays a slightly lower BET surface area of 600–800 m²/g but features larger pores ranging from 6 to 10 nm and thicker silica walls. Low-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns for both materials show a well-resolved main (100) reflection along with higher-order (110) and (200) peaks, indicative of a highly ordered 2D hexagonal (p6mm) symmetry.

### **A Comprehensive Literature Review: Natural Zeolite Deposits, Mineralogy, and Advances in Synthesis (Past Decade)**

#### **1. Natural Zeolite Deposits and Mineralogy**
Natural zeolites are crystalline, hydrated aluminosilicates of alkali and alkaline earth cations, formed primarily by the alteration of volcanic glass in saline, alkaline lacustrine or marine environments over geological timescales. Their formation is governed by factors such as precursor composition, pH, temperature, pressure, and activity of aqueous species. Major global deposits are dominated by low-silica frameworks, including **clinoptilolite (HEU-type)**, **mordenite (MOR-type)**, **phillipsite (PHI-type)**, and **chabazite (CHA-type)**. These minerals are extensively mined in countries like the United States, Japan, Turkey, and Hungary.

Recent mineralogical studies (e.g., Čobić et al., 2020) have employed advanced characterization techniques—including high-resolution scanning electron microscopy (HR-SEM), synchrotron-based X-ray diffraction (XRD), and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy—to resolve complex intergrowths and compositional zoning within natural specimens. A key finding is the widespread occurrence of **mixed-phase assemblages** (e.g., clinoptilolite coexisting with mordenite or quartz), which complicates their direct industrial application. Furthermore, the presence of extra-framework cations (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) and inherent impurities (clays, Fe-oxides) necessitates rigorous beneficiation (e.g., acid washing, thermal treatment, magnetic separation) to achieve consistent performance, particularly in catalysis and ion-exchange.

#### **2. Advances in Synthesis: Mimicking and Transcending Nature**
The past decade has seen significant progress in synthesizing zeolites that either replicate natural frameworks or create entirely novel structures. Synthesis strategies are broadly categorized as **hydrothermal**, **solvothermal**, and **green or alternative** routes. The critical parameters—**SiO₂/Al₂O₃ ratio**, **alkalinity (OH⁻ concentration)**, **temperature (80–180°C)**, **time (hours to days)**, and the use of **structure-directing agents (SDAs)**—dictate the phase purity, crystal size, and morphology.

*   **Hydrothermal Synthesis with Organic SDAs:** This remains the primary method for producing high-silica and pure-phase zeolites. For instance:
    *   **MFI-type (ZSM-5)** is reliably synthesized using **tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH)** as the SDA, with recent optimizations yielding nanocrystalline (<100 nm) forms for enhanced mass transfer.
    *   **BEA-type (Beta)** synthesis often employs **tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH)**, with studies showing that the interplay between TEA⁺ and Na⁺ ions influences polymorph intergrowth (polymorphs A, B, C).
    *   **CHA-type (SSZ-13)** is typically prepared using *N,N,N-trimethyl-1-adamantammonium hydroxide* as the SDA, prized for its small-pore structure in methanol-to-olefins (MTO) and NOx reduction (SCR) catalysis.

*   **Green Synthesis and SDA-Free Routes:** Driven by sustainability goals, research has focused on reducing environmental impact. Notable advances include:
    *   **Ionothermal synthesis** using ionic liquids as both solvent and SDA.
    *   **SDA-free synthesis** of **FAU-type (zeolite X and Y)** and **LTA-type (zeolite A)** using seeded growth or extreme reactant concentrations, though control over Si/Al ratio remains challenging.
    *   The use of **alternative silica/alumina sources** (e.g., rice husk ash, coal fly ash) to synthesize **LTA**, **FAU**, and **SOD** frameworks, valorizing industrial waste.

A comparative analysis reveals that while organic SDA-directed syntheses offer superior control and access to high-silica frameworks, they incur higher costs and require calcination (≥550°C) for SDA removal, posing environmental concerns. Conversely, SDA-free and green routes are more economical and sustainable but often produce lower-silica zeolites with limited framework diversity and potential impurity phases.

#### **3. Current Challenges and Future Directions**
Despite advances, significant challenges persist:
1.  **Scalability and Cost:** The translation of novel synthesis methods (e.g., dry-gel conversion, interzeolite transformation) to industrial-scale production remains non-trivial, particularly concerning the recycling of expensive SDAs and solvents.
2.  **Precise Defect Engineering:** Controlling the nature and density of framework defects (e

## Results and Discussion

### Catalyst Characterization

The synthesized Cu-ZnO/H-ZSM-5 catalysts were characterized by multiple techniques to establish structure-property relationships relevant to CO2 hydrogenation. X-ray diffractogram of the optimized Cu10Zn5/H-ZSM-5 catalyst exhibited characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ = 7.9°, 8.8°, 23.2°, 23.9°, and 24.4°, confirming the preservation of MFI framework structure following metal impregnation. The Cu(111) reflection at 2θ = 43.3° and Cu(200) at 50.4° indicated successful incorporation of metallic copper nanoparticles, with crystallite size calculated at 12.3 nm using the Scherrer equation.

Nitrogen physisorption measurements revealed a BET surface area of 342 m²/g for the zeolite support, decreasing to 287 m²/g after metal loading due to partial pore blockage. The microporous volume, determined by the t-plot method, decreased from 0.18 cm³/g (pristine H-ZSM-5) to 0.14 cm³/g upon Cu-ZnO deposition, while the total pore volume remained relatively unchanged at 0.21–0.23 cm³/g. These values align with reported literature for similar zeolite-supported metal catalysts, where surface areas in the range 250–380 m²/g have been documented for H-ZSM-5 impregnated with transition metals.

Temperature-programmed ammonia desorption (NH₃-TPD) quantification revealed moderate acid site concentrations of 1.24 mmol/g for the parent zeolite, distributed between weak (0.41 mmol/g) and strong (0.83 mmol/g) acid sites. Upon metal introduction, the total acid capacity decreased to 0.89 mmol/g, attributable to partial occupation of Brønsted acid sites by metal oxide clusters. This reduction in accessible acid sites is consistent with findings by Al-Fatesh et al. (2020), who reported acid site densities of 0.95–1.15 mmol/g for metal-loaded zeolites and correlated these with altered product distributions in CO2 conversion.

Catalytic evaluation under reaction conditions of 250–350 °C, 30 bar, and H₂/CO₂ = 3:1 demonstrated that the Cu10Zn5/H-ZSM-5 catalyst achieved a CO₂ conversion of 67.3% at 300 °C, with methanol selectivity of 58.2% and dimethyl ether (DME) selectivity of 24.1%. Notably, the zeolite-supported catalyst exhibited superior methanol yield compared to the unsupported Cu-ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst (48.6% conversion, 41.3% methanol selectivity under identical conditions), validating the synergistic effect of acid-base bifunctionality

# Zeolite Catalysis in Petroleum Refining: A Critical Review of Contemporary Applications and Mechanistic Insights

## Abstract

Zeolite-based catalysis represents a cornerstone of modern petroleum refining operations, enabling the efficient conversion of crude oil fractions into high-value transportation fuels and petrochemical feedstocks. This contribution examines the principal catalytic transformations facilitated by microporous aluminosilicate materials, with particular emphasis on structure-property relationships governing catalytic performance in fluid catalytic cracking, hydrocracking, and allied processes.

## Introduction

The advent of zeolite catalysis in the 1960s fundamentally transformed petroleum refining economics, permitting selective carbonium ion-mediated transformations at severity levels unachievable with conventional amorphous silica-alumina catalysts. The distinctive acid site distribution, uniform pore architecture, and hydrothermal stability of synthetic zeolites collectively enable extraordinary selectivity in C-C bond scission and skeletal rearrangement reactions essential to fuel production.

## Fluid Catalytic Cracking: The Dominant Application

Fluid catalytic cracking remains the single largest application of zeolite catalysts, processing approximately 14 million barrels of vacuum gas oil daily worldwide. **Ultrastable Y (USY) zeolite**, derived from ammonium-exchanged Y zeolite through controlled dealumination, serves as the primary active component in contemporary FCC catalysts. Typical operating conditions encompass temperatures of 520-560°C with catalyst residence times of 2-5 seconds in the riser reactor, followed by regeneration at 680-720°C in the regenerator dense phase.

The bifunctional nature of FCC catalysts—combining Brønsted acid sites within the 7.4 Å supercages of FAU framework with transferable hydrogen from metal contaminants—facilitates complex reaction networks including protolytic cracking, β-scission, and hydrogen transfer. Recent investigations by researchers at the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Zhang et al., 2021) demonstrated that USY zeolites with unit cell sizes of 24.40-24.50 Å and Si/Al ratios of 6-12 exhibit optimal gasoline selectivity with minimized coke yields. The incorporation of **ZSM-5 zeolite** as a secondary additive component (3-8 wt%) selectively cracks linear C₁₁-C₁₂ olefins into propylene and light olefins, enhancing octane number through shape-selective aromatics formation.

## Hydrocracking and Hydroisomerization

Hydrocracking processes utilizing **NiMo/zeolite** or **NiW/zeolite** bifunctional catalysts convert heavy atmospheric and vacuum gas oils into middle distillates at 350-420°C and hydrogen pressures of 80-150 bar. The zeolite component, typically **beta zeolite** (BEA framework) or **Y zeolite** with Si/Al ratios of 15-40, provides acid site density approximately one order of magnitude greater than amorphous silica-alumina, enabling substantially lower reaction temperatures.

Studies by Satterfield and coworkers established the fundamental correlation between acid site proximity and hydrocracking selectivity, demonstrating that zeolites with moderate acid strength promote selective paraffins hydrocracking while suppressing overcracking to light gases. The **pore mouth catalysis** concept, wherein reactant molecules access acid sites preferentially at crystal external surfaces, has gained substantial experimental support through sophisticated diffusion studies employing pulsed field gradient NMR techniques.

## Alkylation and Isomerization Reactions

Solid phosphoric acid and **H-Beta zeolite** catalyze light olefin (C₂-C₄) alkylation with isobutane to produce

### **Atomic Layer Deposition on Zeolite Surfaces: A Comprehensive Literature Review**

Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD) has emerged in the past decade as a transformative tool for the precise engineering of zeolite surfaces and pore architectures. Unlike conventional impregnation or ion-exchange methods, ALD offers sub-nanometer control over the deposition of metal oxides, metals, and sulfides, enabling the creation of tailored active sites at the external surface, within mesopores, or at the pore mouths of zeolites. This review synthesizes recent advances, compares synthesis approaches, and outlines future challenges.

**Synthesis Approaches and Framework-Specific Outcomes**
ALD on zeolites typically employs alternating, self-limiting exposures to a metal precursor (e.g., trimethylaluminum (TMA), titanium tetrachloride (TiCl₄), diethyl zinc (DEZ)) and a co-reactant (e.g., H₂O, O₃) at moderate temperatures (150–300°C). The critical challenge is achieving selective deposition without pore blockage, governed by precursor molecular diameter relative to zeolite pore aperture.

*   **External Surface and Pore-Mouth Modification:** For small-pore zeolites (e.g., CHA with 0.38 nm pores, SAPO-34), precursors like TMA (kinetic diameter ~0.55 nm) cannot penetrate the framework. Studies show that 1-5 ALD cycles of Al₂O₃ from TMA/H₂O selectively functionalize only the external surface and pore entrances of CHA crystals. This approach has been used to poison external acid sites, significantly improving shape selectivity in methanol-to-olefins (MTO) reactions by suppressing undesired hydride transfer reactions at the surface.
*   **Intra-crystalline Deposition in Medium/Large Pores:** In frameworks with larger apertures (MFI: 0.51 x 0.55 nm, BEA: 0.66 x 0.67 nm, FAU: 0.74 nm), careful precursor selection allows for controlled deposition within the pore system. For instance, using the smaller TiCl₄ precursor (~0.46 nm), 10-20 ALD cycles of TiO₂ can be deposited inside the channels of ZSM-5 (MFI) synthesized with TPAOH as the structure-directing agent (SDA). This creates isolated Ti-O-Si sites, enhancing catalytic activity in oxidation reactions like the epoxidation of cyclohexene. Conversely, using bulky precursors like tetraethyl orthotitanate (TEOT, >0.8 nm) results in exclusive external deposition even on large-pore BEA.
*   **Core-Shell and Overcoating Strategies:** A significant advance involves using ALD to create metal oxide overcoats (e.g., Al₂O₃, TiO₂, ZrO₂) that stabilize zeolite surfaces against dealumination or hydrolysis. For example, 2-5 ALD cycles of Al₂O₃ on HY (FAU) have been shown to anchor extra-framework aluminum species, dramatically improving hydrothermal stability in fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) conditions. Similarly, ALD of SiO₂ using SiCl₄/H₂O has been employed to create ultrathin, porous silica shells on MFI nanoplates, enhancing diffusion and coking resistance.

**Comparison of Approaches and Key Findings**
The outcomes are highly sensitive to the zeolite's acidity, pore topology, and the SDA used during synthesis. Zeolites synthesized with organic SDAs (e.g., TPAOH for MFI, TEAOH for BEA) often retain trace organic residues that can alter ALD nucleation kinetics. *In situ* IR spectroscopy studies reveal that initial TMA doses preferentially react with Brønsted acid sites (Si-OH-Al) within accessible pores, forming Al-O-Si bonds, before growing as Al₂O₃ clusters. Quantitative analysis shows that ALD on H-ZSM-5 achieves near-monolayer coverage of external acid sites (~0.5 Al atoms/nm²) after just 1-2 cycles, whereas Na-form zeolites exhibit slower nucleation.

**Current Challenges and Future Directions**
Despite progress, significant challenges remain:
1.  **Diffusion Limitations:** Even for kinetically allowed precursors, slow intra-crystalline diffusion can lead to non-uniform deposition, with heavy coating at pore mouths and minimal deposition in the crystal core.
2.  **Precursor Design:** A lack of thermally stable, highly reactive precursors with tailored molecular diameters (<0.4 nm) limits functionalization of small-pore zeolites like SSZ-13 (CHA).
3.  **Characterization Complexity:** Differentiating between deposited overlayers, grafted single sites, and modified framework atoms requires advanced *operando* techniques like synchrotron X-ray absorption spectroscopy and tomographic diffusion studies

# Natural Zeolite Deposits and Mineralogy: A Comprehensive Review

## Introduction

Natural zeolites are hydrated aluminosilicate minerals that crystallize from volcanic ash in alkaline lacustrine and marine environments, representing one of the most important groups of microporous materials found in nature. Over 250 naturally occurring zeolite species have been identified, though approximately 20 species occur in economically significant quantities. These materials have attracted sustained scientific interest due to their distinctive porous frameworks, ion-exchange capabilities, and catalytic properties, which parallel many synthetic zeolites employed in industrial applications.

## Geological Formation and Deposit Distribution

Natural zeolites form through the hydrothermal alteration of volcanic glass (rhyolitic or andesitic composition) at temperatures typically ranging from 25°C to 200°C, with optimal crystallization occurring between 80°C and 150°C over extended periods spanning thousands to millions of years. The transformation requires alkaline conditions (pH > 9) and the presence of cations such as Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, and Mg²⁺ in the aqueous medium.

Major commercial deposits occur predominantly in Tertiary and Quaternary volcanic provinces. The largest and most extensively studied deposits include those in sedimentary formations of the western United States (California, Nevada, Oregon), particularly the extensive clinoptilolite-rich beds in the John Day Formation of Oregon, where deposits reach thicknesses exceeding 30 meters. Significant occurrences have also been documented in Italy (Sardinia and Sicily), Greece (Milos island), Turkey (Bigadic district), Jordan, Iran, and China (Jiangxi and Zhejiang provinces). The Szob deposit in Hungary and the Nadvornaya deposit in Ukraine represent notable European occurrences.

## Mineralogical Characteristics and Framework Types

Natural zeolites exhibit diverse framework architectures derived from TO₄ tetrahedra (where T = Si or Al). The Si/Al ratio constitutes a fundamental parameter influencing stability, cation exchange capacity, and adsorption properties. Clinoptilolite, the most abundant natural zeolite, typically displays Si/Al ratios between 4.0 and 5.5, while mordenite exhibits higher ratios of 5.0 to 10.0. The lower silica varieties (Si/Al < 4) include chabazite and phillipsite, the latter being particularly abundant in marine palagonitic tuffs.

The cation exchange capacity (CEC) of natural zeolites varies substantially according to framework type and chemical composition. Clinoptilolite demonstrates CEC values ranging from 1.3 to 2.8 meq/g, whereas mordenite typically exhibits lower capacities of 1.0 to 1.5 meq/g. These values correspond to exchangeable cations predominantly consisting of Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, and Mg²⁺, with occasional Sr²⁺ and Ba²⁺ incorporation.

## Structural Diversity and Characterization

The major natural zeolite species may be categorized according to framework topology. The heulandite group (including clinoptilolite and heulandite) possesses a layered structure with 10-membered ring channels, while mordenite exhibits unidimensional 12-membered channel systems. Chabazite forms a cubic framework with large cavities accessible through 8-membered rings, and phillipsite presents an intergrowth structure with both 8- and 12-membered ring channels.

Characterization methodologies applied to natural zeolites include X-ray powder diffraction for phase identification, scanning electron microscopy for morphological analysis, and chemical analysis via energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy. Thermal analysis reveals dehydroxylation events typically occurring between 200°C and 400°C, with structural collapse observed at temperatures exceeding 700°C depending on silica content and framework type.

## Applications and Economic Significance
