# Metal-Organic Frameworks versus Zeolites: A Comparative Review of Synthesis Advances and Structural Characteristics

## Introduction

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and zeolites represent two distinct classes of porous crystalline materials that have garnered substantial research attention over the past decade. While zeolites are aluminosilicate or aluminophosphate materials with well-established industrial applications, MOFs offer unprecedented structural diversity through metal cluster nodes and organic linkers. This review synthesizes recent advances in synthesis methodologies and identifies critical challenges facing both fields.

## Synthesis Approaches and Mechanistic Insights

Zeolite synthesis typically employs hydrothermal routes using alkali-based structure-directing agents (SDAs). The **MFI** framework (Mobil Thirty-Five, ZSM-5) is commonly synthesized using tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH) as the SDA, with typical Si/Al ratios ranging from 10 to infinity. The **BEA** framework (Beta zeolite) utilizes tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH), while **FAU** (faujasite) and **CHA** (chabazite) structures require specific crown ethers or organic cations for directing pore architecture. Recent advances have explored seed-assisted synthesis, reducing SDA concentration by up to 40% while maintaining product crystallinity.

MOF synthesis has diversified substantially since 2014, with solvothermal methods dominating for materials such as UiO-66, ZIF-8, and MIL-101. The modulator-assisted synthesis approach, introducing monocarboxylic acids during crystallization, has proven particularly effective for controlling particle size and defect density. Studies demonstrate that formic acid modulation in UiO-66 synthesis increases BET surface areas from 800 to over 1400 m²/g. Additionally, the advent of **MOF-5** analogues and **PCN-** series materials has expanded accessible surface areas beyond 6,000 m²/g.

## Structural Comparison and Performance Metrics

| Parameter | Zeolites | MOFs |
|-----------|----------|------|
| BET Surface Area | 300-900 m²/g | 1,000-7,000 m²/g |
| Thermal Stability | Up to 1,300°C | Typically <500°C |
| Pore Size | 0.3-1.2 nm | 0.5-5.0 nm |
| Water Stability | Excellent | Variable |

Zeolites demonstrate superior hydrothermal stability, making them indispensable for catalytic cracking and adsorption processes. The **LTA** framework exhibits exceptional moisture resistance, retaining 95% crystallinity after steam treatment at 700°C. Conversely, MOFs such as ZIF-8 maintain structural integrity in water due to strong metal-imidazolate bonds, while MIL-101(Cr) degrades rapidly under aqueous conditions.

## Current Challenges

Zeolite synthesis faces persistent challenges in controlling aluminum distribution and reducing framework defects. The precise location of acid sites within **MFI** channels remains controversial, with recent advanced spectroscopy revealing heterogeneous distributions affecting catalytic selectivity. For MOFs, water stability constitutes the primary limitation—only approximately 15% of reported MOFs demonstrate stability exceeding 24 hours in liquid water. Postsynthetic modification strategies have emerged as promising solutions, with MIL-101(Cr) exhibiting enhanced stability following hydrothermal treatment in phosphoric acid solutions.

## Future Directions

Future research directions include the development of hierarchical pore structures combining microporous and mesoporous characteristics within both material classes. Metal-azolate frameworks (MAFs) represent an emerging subclass combining MOF flexibility with enhanced water stability. For zeolites, computationally-guided SDA design using machine learning algorithms has reduced synthesis screening time by 60%. The integration of MOFs and zeolites into composite materials for tandem catalysis represents a promising frontier, with preliminary studies on

# Cu-SSZ-13 for Selective Catalytic Reduction of NOx: A Literature Review of Synthesis Advances

## Introduction

Copper-exchanged SSZ-13, possessing the chabazite (CHA) framework topology, has emerged as the state-of-the-art catalyst for NH3-assisted selective catalytic reduction (NH3-SCR) of nitrogen oxides in diesel exhaust treatment. The past decade has witnessed substantial advances in synthesis methodologies, active site characterization, and performance optimization for this material.

## Synthesis Approaches

### Conventional Hydrothermal Synthesis

Traditional Cu-SSZ-13 synthesis employs aluminosilicate gels with N,N,N-trimethyl-1-adamantammonium hydroxide (TMAdaOH) as the structure-directing agent. Typical gel compositions involve Si/Al2 ratios of 10-30, with crystallization occurring under autogenous pressure at 140-160°C for 3-7 days. This method produces highly crystalline materials but requires substantial quantities of the expensive SDA.

### Seed-Assisted Synthesis

Seed-assisted approaches, gaining prominence since 2015, substantially reduce SDA consumption while maintaining product quality. Studies by Martin et al. demonstrated that adding 1-5 wt% CHA seeds permits SDA reduction by 40-60% without compromising crystallinity. This method accelerates nucleation and provides more reproducible synthesis conditions.

### One-Pot Direct Synthesis

Direct Cu incorporation during synthesis has shown promise. Research by Jang et al. (2019) reported one-pot methods using Cu-tetraethylenepentamine complexes, achieving Cu loadings of 1.5-3.5 wt% with uniform distribution. However, this approach often results in lower surface areas compared to post-synthetic ion exchange methods.

### Comparative Framework Analysis

The CHA framework demonstrates superior performance compared to conventional frameworks. Cu-SSZ-13 (CHA) exhibits 20-30% higher NOx conversion at 200-300°C versus Cu-ZSM-5 (MFI) and Cu-BEA (BEA), while maintaining significantly better hydrothermal stability. The eight-membered ring (8-MR) pore windows in CHA (7.4 Å) create shape-selectivity favorable

**Experimental Results and Discussion**

The hydrothermal synthesis of TNU-9 and TNU-10 was successfully conducted using 1,4-bis($N$-methylpyrrolidinium)butyl dihydroxide as the structure-directing agent (SDA) under rotating autoclave conditions at 160 °C for 5 days. Following calcination at 600 °C in air to remove the organic templates, the phase purity and textural properties of the resulting zeolites were rigorously characterized.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis confirmed the formation of highly crystalline materials with distinct topologies. The diffraction pattern of TNU-9 exhibited characteristic reflections at $2\theta$ values of 7.82°, 8.62°, 23.15°, and 23.86° (Cu $K\alpha$ radiation), which are indexed to the *TUN* framework structure. In contrast, TNU-10 displayed prominent peaks at 7.58°, 9.42°, 22.38°, and 24.05°, corresponding to the *STI* (stilbite) topology. These peak positions and relative intensities align closely with the reference patterns reported by Hong et al., indicating minimal impurity phases.

Nitrogen physisorption isotherms measured at 77 K revealed Type I isotherms characteristic of microporous solids. The BET specific surface area for TNU-9 was determined to be 525 $m^2/g$, with a corresponding micropore volume of 0.19 $cm^3/g$ derived via the $t$-plot method. TNU-10 demonstrated a slightly lower surface area of 410 $m^2/g$ and a micropore volume of 0.16 $cm^3/g$. These values are consistent with literature data for high-silica analogues, where TNU-9 typically exhibits higher porosity due to its multidimensional channel system compared to the intersecting 10- and 8-ring channels of the TNU-10 structure.

Acidity characterization via $NH_3$-temperature programmed desorption ($NH_3$-TPD) and pyridine-IR spectroscopy provided insight into the active sites. TNU-9 possessed a total acid site concentration of 0.28 mmol/g, comprising 0.22 mmol/g Brønsted acid sites and 0.06 mmol/g Lewis acid sites. TNU-10 showed a total concentration of 0.24 mmol/g (0.19 mmol/g Brønsted, 0.05 mmol/g Lewis). The slightly higher acid site density in TNU-9 correlates with its lower Si/Al ratio of approximately 25, compared to 32 for TNU-10, as determined by ICP-OES.

The structure-property relationships established here highlight the influence of framework topology on catalytic potential. The superior surface area and micropore volume of TNU-9, coupled with its multidimensional 10-ring channel system (5.6 $\times$ 5.6 Å and 5.1 $\times$ 5.5 Å), suggest enhanced mass transport characteristics compared to TNU-10. While the acid site concentrations are comparable, the constrained pore environment of TNU-10 (containing 8-ring channels) may impose diffusion limitations on bulky substrates. Consequently, TNU-9 is anticipated to exhibit superior performance in acid-catalyzed reactions involving larger molecules, such as the cracking of polyaromatics or alkylation processes, whereas TNU-10 may offer distinct shape selectivity for smaller reactants.

### **Experimental Results and Discussion**

The systematic variation of the inorganic cation (M) in the reaction mixture with molar composition 1.0 M₂O : 1.0 Al₂O₃ : 10 SiO₂ : 200 H₂O : 1.5 Structure-Directing Agent (SDA) yielded distinct zeolitic phases after hydrothermal treatment at 150°C for 72 hours, underscoring the profound influence of the cation on nucleation kinetics and framework stabilization.

When M = Na⁺, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis confirmed the formation of a highly crystalline ZSM-5 (MFI-type) structure. The pattern exhibited characteristic doublets at 2θ = 7.9° and 8.8°, alongside strong peaks at 23.1°, 23.9°, and 24.4° (Cu Kα radiation). The material possessed a high BET surface area of 420 m² g⁻¹, with a micropore volume of 0.18 cm³ g⁻¹ and an external surface area of 40 m² g⁻¹. Ammonia temperature-programmed desorption (NH₃-TPD) quantified the total acid site concentration at 0.48 mmol g⁻¹, with a distinct high-temperature desorption peak (Tmax ≈ 400°C) indicative of strong Brønsted acidity. These values align closely with literature reports for high-silica ZSM-5 synthesized under similar alkaline conditions, where Na⁺ effectively balances framework aluminum while moderating silicate polymerization rates to favor the MFI topology.

Substitution with K⁺ (M = K⁺) resulted in a significant shift in product selectivity. The XRD pattern was indexed to the MER framework type (zeolite W), featuring a dominant peak at 2θ = 12.5° and a series of lower-intensity peaks at 17.8°, 21.4°, and 28.2°. This structure possesses a two-dimensional pore system with smaller apertures than MFI. Correspondingly, the BET surface area was lower at 310 m² g⁻¹, with a micropore volume of 0.14 cm³ g⁻¹. The total acidity was also reduced to 0.31 mmol g⁻¹, and the high-temperature desorption peak was less pronounced, suggesting a weaker acid strength distribution. This can be attributed to the larger ionic radius of K⁺ (1.38 Å vs. 1.02 Å for Na⁺), which alters the electrostatic interactions within the growing aluminosilicate gel. Potassium cations preferentially stabilize denser, more charge-balanced frameworks like MER, as their lower hydration energy and poorer structure-breaking capability slow silicate dissolution and favor phases with higher aluminum content in the initial gel.

The most dramatic effect was observed with Cs⁺ (M = Cs⁺). The XRD pattern indicated an amorphous halo with only a faint, broad feature around 2θ = 22-25°, signifying a largely disordered aluminosilicate. The BET surface area was 220 m² g⁻¹, predominantly mesoporous, with a negligible micropore volume (<0.05 cm³ g⁻¹). Acid site concentration was minimal at 0.08 mmol g⁻¹. The large, weakly hydrated Cs⁺ ion (ionic radius 1.67 Å) severely inhibits the reorganization of silicate and aluminate species necessary for long-range crystalline order. It acts primarily as a mineralizer without providing specific templating or space-filling effects for known microporous frameworks under these conditions, leading to polymerization into dense, amorphous phases.

These results establish a clear structure-directing property relationship governed by cation size and alkalinity. Smaller, more highly hydrated cations like Na⁺ facilitate the formation of high-surface-area, strongly acidic frameworks with substantial micropore volume (e.g., MFI). Larger cations like K⁺ direct synthesis towards alternative, often more charge-dense structures with moderately reduced porosity and acidity. The extremely large Cs⁺ cation effectively suppresses crystallization under standard hydrothermal conditions. This trend correlates with the decreasing hydrated ionic radius and increasing preference for ion-pair formation, which directly modulates the solubility of silica, the kinetics of nucleation, and the thermodynamic stability of the resulting zeolite framework.

# Zeolite-Encapsulated Biomimetic Catalysts: Synthesis Procedure

## Introduction

The synthesis of zeolite-encapsulated enzymes and biomimetic catalysts represents a sophisticated approach to combining the molecular sieving properties of zeolites with the selective reactivity of biological or bio-inspired active sites. This procedure describes the hydrothermal synthesis of zeolite Y (FAU structure) containing encapsulated cytochrome c or its iron-porphyrin biomimetic analogue within the zeolite framework. The resulting material serves as a heterogeneous biomimetic catalyst for oxidation reactions.

## Materials and Reagents

The following reagents of analytical grade are required: sodium silicate solution (Na₂SiO₃, 27 wt% SiO₂, 8.5 wt% Na₂O) (12.5 g), aluminum hydroxide gel (Al(OH)₃, 54 wt% Al₂O₃) (1.85 g), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 97 wt%) (2.10 g), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99 wt%) (3.50 g), deionized water (85 mL), iron(III) meso-tetraphenylporphyrin chloride (FeTPPCl, 95 wt%) (0.045 g) as the biomimetic catalyst precursor, and template-free zeolite NaY precursor gel prepared from colloidal silica (Ludox AS-40, 40 wt% SiO₂) (8.0 g).

A 100 mL polypropylene reaction vessel with PTFE lining, a programmable muffle furnace, a shaking water bath, a centrifuge (5000 rpm capacity), and a vacuum drying oven (120 °C) are required.

## Step-by-Step Synthesis Procedure

**Step 1: Gel Preparation.** The sodium silicate solution (12.5 g) was diluted with deionized water (45 mL) in a 250 mL PTFE beaker under magnetic stirring (400 rpm) at 25 °C. The sodium hydroxide (2.10 g) was dissolved separately in deionized water (15 mL) at 80 °C, then added dropwise to the silicate solution over 10 minutes. The aluminum hydroxide gel (1.85 g) was subsequently introduced, and the mixture was stirred continuously for 30 minutes until a homogeneous gel (Si/Al ratio = 2.5, Na₂O/SiO₂ = 1.2, H₂O/Na₂O = 35) was formed.

**Step 2: Biomimetic Catalyst Incorporation.** The iron(III) meso-tetraphenylporphyrin chloride (0.045 g) was dissolved in ethanol (5 mL) at 40 °C, then added slowly to the zeolite gel under vigorous stirring (600 rpm). The mixture was stirred for an additional 15 minutes to ensure homogeneous distribution of the metalloporphyrin within the gel matrix. The final composition of the synthesis gel was: 0.45 Na₂O : Al₂O₃ : 2.5 SiO₂ : 100 H₂O.

**Step 3: Hydrothermal Crystallization.** The gel was transferred to the polypropylene reaction vessel, sealed, and placed in a shaking water bath. Hydrothermal treatment was conducted at 100 °C for 48 hours under static conditions with intermittent shaking (30 rpm, 5

# Synthesis and Characterization of BEA-Type Zeolite (Zeolite Beta)

## Introduction

Zeolite Beta (BEA-type) is a high-silica zeolite characterized by a three-dimensional pore system with 12-membered ring apertures (approximately 6.6 × 6.4 Å and 5.6 × 5.6 Å). It serves as a versatile catalyst in petrochemical processes, hydrocarbon conversion, and environmental applications. The following procedure describes the synthesis of nanocrystalline BEA zeolite with a Si/Al ratio of approximately 25, employing tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH) as the structure-directing agent.

## Materials

The following reagents were used as received: fumed silica (Sigma-Aldrich, ≥99.0%, surface area ~380 m²/g), sodium aluminate (Riedel-de Haën, 50-56% Al₂O₃, 37-45% Na₂O), tetraethylammonium hydroxide solution (Alfa Aesar, 35 wt.% in H₂O), sodium hydroxide pellets (Merck, ≥98.0%), and deionized water (18.2 MΩ·cm).

## Synthesis Procedure

**Gel preparation.** A sodium aluminate solution was first prepared by dissolving sodium hydroxide (4.12 g, 103 mmol) in deionized water (42.0 mL) under magnetic stirring at ambient temperature. To this solution, sodium aluminate (0.68 g, 8.5 mmol) was added gradually with continuous stirring (400 rpm). The mixture was stirred for 30 minutes until a clear solution was obtained.

**Silica incorporation.** Fumed silica (12.0 g, 200 mmol SiO₂) was slowly added in small portions to the aluminate solution under vigorous stirring (500 rpm). The white suspension was stirred for an additional 2 hours at room temperature to ensure homogeneous gel formation.

**Structure-directing agent addition.** The TEAOH solution (43.6 g, 35 wt.%, approximately 84 mmol) was added dropwise to the gel over 15 minutes. The final mixture exhibited a milky white appearance with the molar composition: 1.0 SiO₂ : 0.042 Al₂O₃ : 0.52 Na₂O : 0.42 TEAOH : 19 H₂O.

**Crystallization.** The gel was transferred to a 125 mL PTFE-lined stainless steel autoclave (Parr Instrument Company). The sealed autoclave was placed in a forced-air convection oven (Memmert) and heated statically at 140°C for 96 hours under autogenous pressure.

**Product recovery.** After the crystallization period, the autoclave was cooled naturally to room temperature over 4 hours. The white solid product was separated by vacuum filtration and washed thoroughly with deionized water (approximately 2 L) until the filtrate reached pH 8-9. The washed product was dried at 110°C for 12 hours in a static air oven.

**Calcination.** The as-synthes

**1. Introduction and Background**

SSZ-13 is a high-silica aluminosilicate zeolite possessing the chabazite (CHA) framework topology, distinguished by its three-dimensional pore system composed of intersecting 8-membered rings (8MR) with a nominal pore aperture of approximately 3.8 Å. First synthesized by Zones et al. at Chevron in the mid-1980s, the material was historically produced using the expensive, rigid organic structure-directing agent (OSDA) N,N,N-trimethyl-1-adamantammonium hydroxide (TMAdaOH). This specific OSDA was critical for nucleating the CHA structure over competing phases such as SSZ-16 (AFX) or SSZ-39 (AEI). The structural integrity of SSZ-13, characterized by high Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas exceeding 600 m²/g and exceptional hydrothermal stability, positions it as a superior material in heterogeneous catalysis compared to its small-pore predecessors.

The industrial relevance of SSZ-13 became unequivocally established following the commercialization of Cu-exchanged SSZ-13 (Cu-SSZ-13) for ammonia selective catalytic reduction ($NH_3$-SCR) of nitrogen oxides ($NO_x$) in diesel engine exhaust. Prior to 2010, the industry relied on medium-pore zeolites like ZSM-5 and Beta; however, these materials suffered from severe hydrothermal degradation above 600°C. Cu-SSZ-13 demonstrated remarkable stability at temperatures exceeding 800°C, effectively meeting the stringent Euro VI and EPA Tier 3 emission standards. The global market for automotive catalysts, driven by SCR technology, is valued at approximately $25–30 billion USD, with Cu-SSZ-13 constituting the dominant active component in modern heavy-duty and light-duty diesel systems. Furthermore, the zeolite is a pivotal candidate in the Methanol-to-Olefins (MTO) process, where its small pore size favors the formation of light olefins (ethylene and propylene) while suppressing polyaromatic hydrocarbon formation, offering a distinct selectivity advantage over SAPO-34 in specific feedstock conditions.

Despite its commercial success, the synthesis of SSZ-13 presents significant scientific and economic challenges that drive ongoing research. The primary economic barrier is the high cost of TMAdaOH, which can account for up to 50% of the raw material expenses. Consequently, substantial effort is directed toward "cost-down" strategies, including the use of alternative, cheaper OSDAs (e.g., benzyltrimethylammonium), OSDA-free synthesis routes utilizing seed crystals, and recycling protocols for the organic template. Additionally, precise control over the Si/Al ratio (typically ranging from 5 to 30 for SCR applications) is crucial; lower Si/Al ratios favor high Cu loading and activity but compromise hydrothermal stability, whereas higher ratios improve durability but reduce active site density. Managing the spatial distribution of Cu ions (isolated $Cu^{2+}$ vs. $CuO_x$ clusters) to maximize $NO_x$ conversion while minimizing unwanted $N_2O$ byproduct formation remains a focal point of mechanistic study. The motivation for this research is to optimize the cost-performance ratio of the catalyst, enabling wider adoption in emerging markets and facilitating compliance with future "real-world driving emission" (RDE) regulations.

### **Ionothermal Synthesis of Zeolites: Introduction and Background**

Zeolites, microporous crystalline aluminosilicates with well-defined pore architectures, are cornerstone materials in modern industrial catalysis, separation, and ion-exchange processes. Their global market, valued at approximately **USD 14.5 billion in 2023**, is projected to exceed **USD 20 billion by 2030**, driven by demand in fluid catalytic cracking (FCC), petrochemical production, and environmental remediation. Traditional hydrothermal synthesis, developed in the mid-20th century, utilizes water as the solvent and structure-directing agent (SDA), operating at autogenous pressure within sealed autoclaves at temperatures typically between **80°C and 200°C**. While this method has yielded over **250 distinct zeolite frameworks**, it faces inherent limitations: the high volatility of water necessitates pressure vessels, the use of organic SDAs often requires post-synthetic calcination (at **~550°C**) to remove templates, and the synthesis of certain aluminophosphate (AlPO) and metalloaluminophosphate (MeAPO) zeotypes can be constrained by hydrolysis.

The pursuit of alternative synthetic paradigms led to the groundbreaking development of **ionothermal synthesis** by Cooper and colleagues in 2004. This methodology employs ionic liquids (ILs) or eutectic mixtures as both the solvent and the SDA. Ionic liquids are salts with melting points below **100°C**, characterized by low vapor pressures, high thermal stability (often >**300°C**), and tunable physicochemical properties through cation-anion selection. In a typical ionothermal reaction, a gel precursor containing silica, alumina, and phosphate sources (for AlPOs) is combined with the ionic liquid (e.g., 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide, [EMIM]Br) and crystallized in open vessels at ambient pressure and temperatures ranging from **150°C to 200°C**. The ionic liquid fulfills a dual role: its ions act as templates around which the zeolite framework condenses, while its ionic nature provides the reaction medium.

The historical motivation for ionothermal research is rooted in addressing key scientific challenges of hydrothermal synthesis. First, the **near-negligible vapor pressure** of ILs eliminates the safety and engineering constraints of high-pressure systems, allowing for simpler reactor design and *in situ* characterization. Second, the **templating effect** of the IL ions can lead to novel framework topologies inaccessible via conventional routes, such as the discovery of **SIZ-1 to SIZ-7** (St Andrews Ionothermal Zeolites). Third, the **enhanced solvating power and structural diversity** of ILs facilitate the incorporation of heteroatoms (e.g., Mg, Co, Zn, Li) into zeotype frameworks, creating active sites for specialized catalysis. A critical challenge in conventional synthesis—the competition between framework formation and template decomposition at high temperatures—is mitigated due to the superior thermal stability of ILs.

Industrial applications impose stringent requirements on zeolite materials, including **high hydrothermal stability (>800°C steam for FCC catalysts), precise acidity (controlled Si/Al ratio), and hierarchical porosity for diffusion-limited reactions**. Ionothermally synthesized materials show particular promise in niche applications. For instance, **MeAPO-34 (CHA framework)**, synthesized ionothermally with cobalt or manganese, is a superior catalyst for the methanol-to-olefins (MTO) process due to its optimized acid site distribution and reduced coke formation. Its requirement is a **high ethylene/propylene selectivity (>80%) and extended catalyst lifetime**. Similarly, large-pore AlPOs like **JDF-2** are investigated for the separation of linear from branched alkanes, a process critical in petroleum refining requiring molecular sieving with sub-angstrom precision.

The motivation for ongoing research in ionothermal synthesis is thus multifaceted: to exploit its unique chemistry for **discovering novel frameworks**, to improve the **sustainability and energy efficiency** of zeolite manufacturing by avoiding high-pressure systems and calcination steps, and to tailor materials for next-generation applications in **CO₂ capture, hydrocarbon processing, and fine chemical synthesis**. This synthesis route represents a significant paradigm shift, moving from aqueous, pressure-dependent systems towards designer ionic media that offer unparalleled control over crystallization kinetics and ultimate framework architecture.

# In-Situ and Operando Spectroscopy of Zeolite Catalysis: A Literature Review

## Introduction

The characterization of zeolite catalysts under working conditions has become essential for understanding structure-performance relationships. Over the past decade, in-situ and operando spectroscopy have emerged as transformative tools, enabling researchers to monitor active sites, reaction intermediates, and deactivation mechanisms in real time.

## Advances in Spectroscopic Techniques

**UV-Vis spectroscopy** has proven particularly valuable for probing transition metal sites in metallo-zeolites. operando UV-Vis has enabled direct observation of Cu species evolution during NH3-SCR catalysis in CHA-type catalysts (SSZ-13), revealing dynamic redox cycling between Cu²⁺ and Cu⁺ that governs NOₓ conversion efficiency. For MFI catalysts in methanol-to-olefins (MTO) reactions, UV-Vis has tracked the formation and evolution of aromatic coke precursors, correlating spectral features with catalyst lifetime.

**Infrared spectroscopy** (DRIFTS) remains foundational for acid site characterization. Recent advances in time-resolved DRIFTS have allowed quantification of Brønsted acid site strength distribution in BEA zeolites synthesized with TEAOH as the structure-directing agent, demonstrating that Si/Al ratios directly influence v(OH) band frequencies. Operando DRIFTS has also resolved hydrocarbon pool species in MFI zeolites during MTO, distinguishing between methylbenzene cycle intermediates and alkene cycle products.

**Raman spectroscopy** has matured as a tool for framework characterization, with in-situ Raman identifying phase transitions during zeolite synthesis from gels containing TPAOH. Operando Raman studies of FAU-type catalysts during fluid catalytic cracking have detected coke formation onset through characteristic G-band evolution.

**X-ray absorption spectroscopy** (XAS) provides element-specific information on metal coordination. operando XAS has been applied to Fe-ZSM-5 and Cu-SAPO-34 catalysts, revealing how framework composition affects metal speciation under reaction conditions. Extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) analysis quantifies metal-cluster size changes during alkane activation.

## Synthesis-Structure Relationships

# Faujasite Zeolite Synthesis with Variable Si/Al Ratios: A Literature Review

## Abstract

Faujasite (FAU) zeolites, characterized by their three-dimensional pore system with 7.4 Å apertures and large supercages (≈13 Å), remain among the most industrially significant molecular sieves. The Si/Al ratio fundamentally governs their acidic, hydrothermal, and catalytic properties. This review synthesizes recent advances in FAU synthesis methodologies that achieve controlled Si/Al ratios and identifies persistent challenges in the field.

## Introduction

The faujasite framework (FAU) comprises tetrahedral SiO₄ and AlO₄ units arranged in a cubic close-packed lattice. Low-silica FAU (Si/Al ≈ 1–2.5, type X) exhibits high cation exchange capacity suitable for adsorption and ion exchange, while high-silica FAU (Si/Al > 2.5, type Y) possesses superior thermal stability and Brønsted acidity critical for catalytic cracking. The ability to precisely control Si/Al ratios during synthesis directly determines application suitability.

## Synthesis Approaches and Recent Advances

### Conventional Hydrothermal Synthesis

Traditional FAU synthesis employs alkaline media with silica and alumina sources under autogenous pressure at 70–100 °C for 8–72 hours. Recent advances have refined this methodology significantly. Research published in *Chemistry of Materials* (2018–2022) demonstrated that adjusting NaOH concentration from 0.5 M to 3.0 M while maintaining crystallization temperature at 90 °C enables Si/Al tuning from 1.1 to 4.5 in the product. The presence of Na⁺ cations serves dual roles as mineralizer and charge-balancing species, with optimal Na₂O/SiO₂ ratios of 0.15–0.25 achieving maximum crystallinity.

Structure-directing agents (SDAs) have expanded synthesis flexibility considerably. Tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH) has proven particularly effective for synthesizing FAU with Si/Al ratios of 2.0–6.0, as demonstrated by researchers at Delft University of Technology (2020). TEA⁺ cations preferentially stabilize larger supercages while permitting higher silica incorporation. comparative studies by Corma and colleagues showed that substituting TPAOH (tetrapropylammonium) for TEAOH in seed-assisted systems shifts product selectivity from **BEA** toward **FAU**, highlighting the critical role of SDA size-matching with the target framework.

### Seed-Assisted and Refined Methods

Seed-assisted synthesis, introduced systematically around 2015, has revolutionized FAU production by enabling faster crystallization (4–12 hours) and better Si/Al control. Studies in *Journal of Materials Chemistry A* (2021) reported that colloidal FAU seeds (50–200 nm) added at 0.5–2.0 wt% serve as nucleation sites, reducing induction periods and suppressing competing phases such as **ANA** (analcime) or **GIS** (garronite).

Research by Zhang et al. (2019) demonstrated microwave-assisted FAU synthesis achieving Si/Al = 2.8 at 150 °C within 2 hours, compared to 24–48 hours under conventional heating. Microwave energy creates localized superheating that accelerates both nucleation and crystal growth kinetics while maintaining product Si/Al uniformity.

### Geopolymer and Dry-Gel Conversion Methods

Dry-gel conversion (DGC) techniques, particularly steam-assisted conversion (SAC), have emerged as powerful approaches for producing high-silica FAU. Investigations published in *Microporous and Mesoporous Materials* (2020) achieved Si/Al ratios of 3.0–15.0 using fumed silica and pseudo-boehmite in DGC systems at 180 °C. The method produces FAU with superior thermal stability (≥1000 °C) compared to conventional hydrothermal products, attributed to denser framework polymerization during conversion.

## Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | Hydrothermal | Seed-Assisted | DGC/SAC |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|---------|
| Typical Si/Al | 1.1–4.5 | 1.5–8.0 | 3.0–15.0 |
| Crystallization Time | 24–72 h | 4–12 h | 2–6 h |
| Crystal Size | 5–50 μm | 0.5–5 μm | 1–20 μm |
| Phase Purity | Moderate | High |

**3.2. Characterization of FER-type Zeolite**

The hydrothermal synthesis yielded a highly crystalline FER-type zeolite, as evidenced by the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern shown in Figure S1. The diffractogram exhibited distinct reflections characteristic of the ferrierite topology (space group *Immm*), with prominent peaks indexed at 2$\theta$ values of 9.4°, 13.4°, 20.4°, 22.7°, 25.7°, and 35.9°, corresponding to the (020), (200), (400), (330), (501), and (002) crystallographic planes, respectively. The absence of extraneous peaks indicates a phase-pure product without detectable amorphous silica or competing zeolitic phases (e.g., mordenite). The relative crystallinity, calculated using a commercial FER reference as the baseline (100%), was determined to be 98%, suggesting optimal nucleation and crystal growth conditions during the 72-hour hydrothermal treatment at 448 K.

Textural properties were evaluated via $N_2$ physisorption at 77 K. The isotherm (Figure S2) displayed a Type I profile consistent with microporous materials, featuring a steep uptake at low relative pressures ($P/P_0 < 0.1$) and a distinct H4-type hysteresis loop at $P/P_0 > 0.45$, indicative of interparticle mesoporosity. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) specific surface area was calculated to be 368 $m^2/g$, with a t-plot micropore volume of 0.145 $cm^3/g$ and a total pore volume of 0.192 $cm^3/g$. These values are in excellent agreement with literature data for FER zeolites with similar Si/Al ratios (Si/Al $\approx$ 10), where typical surface areas range from 350 to 380 $m^2/g$ and micropore volumes approach 0.15 $cm^3/g$. The slight deviation in total pore volume is attributed to the presence of textural mesopores arising from the nanocrystal aggregate morphology observed in SEM analysis.

Acidic properties were quantified using temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia ($NH_3$-TPD). The profile revealed a bimodal distribution of acid strength. The low-temperature desorption peak, centered at 460 K, corresponds to weak acid sites, while the high-temperature peak at 680 K is attributed to strong Brønsted acid sites. The total concentration of acid sites was determined to be 0.68 mmol/g, with strong acid sites constituting 0.42 mmol/g of the total. This high density of strong acid sites results from the efficient isomorphous substitution of Al atoms into the tetrahedral framework during synthesis.

The structure-property relationships elucidated from this data are critical for understanding the catalytic potential of the synthesized FER. The FER framework possesses a unique bidirectional pore system consisting of intersecting 10-membered ring (10-MR) channels (0.42 $\times$ 0.54 nm) and 8-MR channels (0.35 $\times$ 0.48 nm). The combination of these constrained pore dimensions with the measured high external surface area and strong acidity (0.42 mmol/g) creates an optimal environment for shape-selective catalysis. Specifically, the high density of strong Brønsted sites facilitates protonation of reactants, while the 8-MR channels impose significant diffusional constraints on transition states. This balance renders the material particularly effective for reactions such as the skeletal isomerization of $n$-butene to isobutene, where the suppression of bulky by-products is essential for high selectivity.

### **Experimental Results and Discussion**

A series of zeolite X (FAU-type) samples were successfully synthesized via hydrothermal crystallization at 95°C for 24 hours using non-conventional silica precursors: rice husk ash (RHA, Sample **Z-RHA**), waste glass cullet (Sample **Z-GL**), and diatomaceous earth (Sample **Z-DE**). For comparison, a reference zeolite X was synthesized from reagent-grade sodium silicate (Sample **Z-Ref**). The products were characterized to evaluate the impact of silica source impurity profiles on crystallinity, textural properties, and acidity.

**Crystallinity and Phase Purity:** Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis confirmed the formation of the FAU framework for all samples (Figure 1). The three characteristic peaks for zeolite X were observed at 2θ = 6.2° (111), 10.1° (220), and 15.6° (331), in excellent agreement with reference patterns (ICDD 01-089-3738). Quantitative analysis using the relative crystallinity index (RCI) method, with **Z-Ref** as 100% crystalline, revealed values of 98% for **Z-RHA**, 92% for **Z-GL**, and 85% for **Z-DE**. The minor reduction in crystallinity for **Z-DE** is attributed to the presence of residual crystalline impurities (notably quartz, indicated by a small peak at 2θ = 26.6°) and higher concentrations of alkali earth metals (Mg, Ca) which can interfere with nucleation. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the peak at 2θ = 6.2° was nearly identical for **Z-Ref**, **Z-RHA**, and **Z-GL** (0.12°), suggesting comparable crystal size and quality, while it broadened slightly to 0.15° for **Z-DE**.

**Textural Properties:** N₂ physisorption at 77 K yielded type-I isotherms, confirming microporosity. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas were 720 m²/g (**Z-Ref**), 695 m²/g (**Z-RHA**), 680 m²/g (**Z-GL**), and 625 m²/g (**Z-DE**). The corresponding total pore volumes, determined at P/P₀ = 0.99, were 0.35, 0.33, 0.32, and 0.30 cm³/g, respectively. This suggests that residual cations (e.g., Ca²⁺) in **Z-DE** may partially neutralize the strongest framework protons, reducing both the number and strength of Brønsted acid sites. # Ionothermal Synthesis of Zeolites: Principles and Recent Advances

## Introduction

Ionothermal synthesis represents a paradigm shift in the preparation of zeolitic materials, employing ionic liquids (ILs) as both solvent and structure-directing agent simultaneously. Unlike conventional hydrothermal or solvothermal methods where organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs) are dissolved in water or alcohol solvents, ionothermal synthesis utilizes fused salts—typically molten salts with melting points below 300°C—as the reaction medium. This approach hasenabled access to novel zeolite frameworks and metastable phases unattainable through traditional routes.

## Mechanistic Considerations

The fundamental distinction of ionothermal synthesis lies in the mechanism of framework assembly. In conventional hydrothermal systems, hydration spheres around cations create localized concentration gradients that guide aluminosilicate polymerization. In contrast, ionothermal media exhibit negligible vapor pressure and unique hydrogen-bonding networks that fundamentally alter crystallization pathways. The ionic liquid cations—such as imidazolium ([Cnmim]⁺, where n = 2–8), pyridinium, or phosphonium derivatives—function simultaneously as solvents with exceptionally high dielectric constants (ε ≈ 10–50) and as structuredirecting agents that template pore systems through steric complementarity.

Critical parameters for successful ionothermal synthesis include: reaction temperatures typically ranging from 140–200°C, durations of 24–168 hours, and initial凝胶凝胶 molar ratios of SiO₂/Al₂O₃ between 10–200. The water content must be meticulously controlled—typically below 5 wt%—as excess water promotes conventional zeolite crystallization pathways and may hydrolyze the ionic liquid.

## Representative Synthetic Systems

Han et al. (2005) demonstrated the first systematic ionothermal synthesis of aluminosilicates using 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ([emim]Br) as solvent and SDA. This work afforded the zincosilicate ZIP-O and several novel framework types including the 8-ring zeolite ES (Exponential Space lattice). Subsequent studies by Parnham and Morris (2006–2007) established that even apparently "non-structure-directing" anions such as Cl⁻, Br⁻, and BF₄⁻ could effectively template zeolite formation when paired with appropriate cations, contradicting the prevailing assumption that both cation and anion must participate in templating.

Particularly noteworthy is the synthesis of alPO₄-d frameworks and zeotypes using ionic liquids such as 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([bmim]BF₄). Ma et al. (2012) reported the preparation of a novel 12-ring zeolite with 1D channels at 180°C for 72 hours using [emim]Cl:NaF eutectic mixtures. The addition of fluoride (0.5–2.0 mol per mole SiO₂) dramatically accelerates crystallization and enables synthesis ofsilicarich frameworks including all-silica SOD and RUT types.

## Advantages and Applications

Ionothermal synthesis offers several distinct advantages: (1) negligible solvent volatility enabling reactions in sealed autoclaves without pressure concerns; (2) tunable cation geometry permitting rational targeting of specific framework types; (3) in situ generation of hierarchical porosity due to ionic liquid decomposition products; and (4) exceptional framework stability post-synthesis calcination at 550°C removes organic residues without collapse.

Recent applications extend to_extra-large pore zeolites, metalloaluminosilicates with transition metals (Fe, Co, Cu) isolated in framework positions, and oriented crystal growth on substrates for membrane applications.

## Conclusion

Ionothermal synthesis has emerged as a versatile tool for accessing novel zeolitic materials. Further development of task-specific ionic liquids combining cation geometry optimization with functional anions promises continued expansion of zeolite science into previously inaccessible compositional and structural space.

# Experimental Results and Discussion: Zeolite-Catalyzed Cascade Reactions

## Characterization of Synthesized Catalysts

The crystallinity and phase purity of the synthesized zeolite catalysts were evaluated by powder X-ray diffraction analysis. The XRD pattern of the H-Beta zeolite (Si/Al = 15) exhibited characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ = 7.8° and 22.5°, corresponding to the (100) and (102) reflections of the BEA framework structure, respectively. Additional peaks at 2θ = 13.5°, 14.4°, and 26.9° confirmed the presence of the pure zeolite phase without detectable crystalline impurities. The H-ZSM-5 sample (Si/Al = 30) displayed the expected MFI framework reflections at 2θ = 7.9°, 8.8°, 23.1°, 23.3°, and 23.9°, in excellent agreement with literature values (JCPDS Card No. 44-0003).

Nitrogen physisorption measurements revealed that the Beta zeolite possessed a Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of 628 m²/g with a total pore volume of 0.38 cm³/g, while the microporous volume accounted for 0.24 cm³/g. The ZSM-5 catalyst exhibited a BET surface area of 412 m²/g and total pore volume of 0.29 cm³/g. These values align with reported literature data for conventionally synthesized zeolites in this Si/Al range (BET surface areas: 550-700 m²/g for Beta; 350-450 m²/g for ZSM-5).

The acid site distribution was quantified by temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia (NH₃-TPD). The H-Beta catalyst displayed weak acid sites at 0.42 mmol/g and strong acid sites at 0.38 mmol/g, yielding a total acidity of 0.80 mmol/g. The H-ZSM-5 sample exhibited lower total acidity (0.51 mmol/g) with 0.29 mmol/g of weak sites and 0.22 mmol/g of strong sites, consistent with its higher Si/Al ratio. These acidity values fall within the established range for commercial zeolite catalysts prepared by ammonium exchange and calcination procedures.

## Cascade Reaction Performance

The consecutive Friedel-Crafts alkylation of benzene with propylene to form cumene, followed by transalkylation, was employed as a model cascade reaction. The bifunctional nature of the H-Beta catalyst enabled sequential reaction pathways, achieving 47

The BEA (*Beta*) framework, characterized by a three-dimensional 12-membered ring pore system with intersecting channels of approximately $6.6 \times 6.7$ Å and $5.6 \times 5.6$ Å, remains a cornerstone of industrial catalysis due to its strong Brønsted acidity and hydrothermal stability. Traditional synthesis relies on hydrothermal treatment at 140–170°C using tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH) as a structure-directing agent (SDA). However, the high cost and environmental impact of quaternary ammonium SDAs have driven significant research over the last decade toward sustainable and cost-effective routes.

A major recent advance is the development of the "interzeolite conversion" (IZC) method. This approach utilizes the structural similarity between parent and target zeolites to lower the kinetic barrier for nucleation. For instance, the recrystallization of FAU-type zeolites into BEA has been successfully achieved using minimal amounts of TEAOH or even TPAOH (tetrapropylammonium hydroxide), which is typically associated with MFI synthesis. Studies indicate that using FAU seeds in a TEA$_2$O-SiO$_2$-Al$_2$O$_3$-H$_2$O system significantly accelerates crystallization, yielding pure BEA phases within 24–48 hours at 140°C, compared to several days for conventional gel synthesis. This method not only reduces SDA consumption by up to 60% but also facilitates the incorporation of heteroatoms.

Concurrently, the synthesis of hierarchical BEA zeolites has advanced through desilication and surfactant-templating strategies. While purely microporous materials like CHA offer superior shape selectivity, they suffer from severe diffusion limitations with bulky molecules. Post-synthetic treatment of BEA with dilute NaOH or NH$_4$F introduces intracrystalline mesoporosity (2–10 nm), drastically improving mass transfer in alkylation and isomerization reactions. Furthermore, the "dry gel conversion" technique has enabled the formation of nanosized BEA aggregates (<50 nm), increasing external surface area and accessibility.

In terms of applications, the functionalization of BEA framework sites has shifted focus toward biomass conversion. The incorporation of tin (Sn) into the BEA framework (Sn-Beta) has established a new paradigm for Lewis acid catalysis. Synthesized via hydrothermal treatment in fluoride media or post-synthetic solid-state incorporation, Sn-Beta exhibits exceptional activity in the Baeyer-Villiger oxidation of lactones to esters and the isomerization of glucose to fructose. The tetrahedral coordination of Sn$^{4+}$ within the siliceous lattice is critical, as isolated sites prevent side reactions.

Despite these advances, challenges persist. The control of Al distribution in the BEA framework remains difficult; the random occupancy of T-sites leads to inhomogeneous acid strength distribution compared to more constrained frameworks like MFI. Furthermore, the removal of organic templates still necessitates high-temperature calcination (>540°C), which poses risks of dealumination and framework collapse. Future directions are pivoting toward the " OSDA-free" synthesis of BEA using only inorganic cations and seeds, as well as the precise tuning of active site environments using machine learning-aided synthesis protocols to optimize catalytic performance in renewable chemical production.

### **Literature Review: Zeolite Synthesis from Coal Fly Ash and Siliceous-Aluminous Wastes**

#### **Introduction and Rationale**
The synthesis of zeolites from coal fly ash (CFA) and other industrial aluminosilicate wastes (e.g., rice husk ash, slag, metakaolin) represents a cornerstone of sustainable materials chemistry. This approach aligns with circular economy principles, converting a globally abundant byproduct (annual CFA production exceeds 1 billion tons) into high-value microporous materials. The primary drivers are the reduction of synthesis costs—by replacing commercial silica and alumina sources—and the valorization of hazardous waste. The inherent chemical composition of CFA (40-60% SiO₂, 20-30% Al₂O₃, plus Fe₂O₃, CaO, and unburned carbon) makes it a viable, albeit complex, precursor for zeolite formation.

#### **Synthesis Methodologies and Recent Advances (2014-2024)**
Over the past decade, research has focused on optimizing traditional hydrothermal methods and developing innovative, energy-efficient pathways.

1.  **Conventional Alkaline Hydrothermal Synthesis:** This remains the most prevalent method. CFA is typically subjected to a pre-treatment (fusion with NaOH at 550°C or direct acid leaching with HCl/HNO₃) to enhance Si/Al reactivity and remove inhibitory Fe and Ca ions. Subsequent hydrothermal treatment in alkaline media (2-5 M NaOH/KOH) at 90-150°C for 6-48 hours yields low-to-medium silica zeolites. Recent optimizations using two-stage processes (aging followed by crystallization) have improved phase purity. For instance, **Na-P1 (GIS-type)** and **Na-X (FAU-type, Si/Al ~1.2)** are commonly obtained from direct hydrothermal conversion, with yields of 60-80% reported.

2.  **Two-Step "Fusion-Assisted" Hydrothermal Synthesis:** This method, significantly advanced in the last decade, involves dry fusion of CFA with NaOH at 450-600°C followed by hydrothermal treatment. It dramatically increases the yield and enables the formation of higher-value frameworks. Studies have successfully produced **zeolite A (LTA-type)**, **sodalite (SOD)**, and **faujasite (FAU)** with high crystallinity (>90%). A key finding is that controlling the fusion temperature and alkali/ash ratio (typically 1.2:1 w/w) is critical to prevent the formation of competing phases like nepheline.

3.  **Template-Assisted Synthesis for High-Silica Zeolites:** The most significant recent breakthrough is the incorporation of organic **Structure-Directing Agents (SDAs)** to direct the crystallization of high-silica zeolites from CFA-derived extracts. Silicate and aluminate solutions are first extracted from pretreated CFA, then combined with SDAs under hydrothermal conditions.
    *   **Tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH)** has been successfully used to direct the formation of **ZSM-5 (MFI-type, Si/Al >20)** at 170-180°C for 24-72 hours.
    *   **Tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH)** and more complex SDAs have enabled the synthesis of **beta zeolite (BEA-type)** and, more recently, **SSZ-13 (CHA-type)**, a catalyst critical for NOx reduction (SCR).
    The challenge lies in the purity of the extract; residual impurities can poison the SDA or lead to phase impurities.

#### **Comparative Analysis of Outcomes**
The choice of synthesis path dictates the final zeolite's properties and application:
*   **Direct Hydrothermal:** Produces low-cost, high-Al zeolites (LTA, FAU-X) suitable for water softening (ion exchange) and heavy metal adsorption. Cation Exchange Capacity (CEC) can reach 4-5 meq/g.
*   **Fusion-Assisted:** Yields more crystalline and pure phases, enhancing adsorption capacity for gases (CO₂) and cations. Energy consumption is higher.
*   **SDA-Assisted:** Creates high-silica, hydrophobic, and acid-stable catalysts (MFI, BEA, CHA) for petrochemical cracking or environmental catalysis. This route adds cost but maximizes value from waste.

#### **Current Challenges and Future Directions**
Despite progress, significant hurdles remain:
1.  **Impurity Management:** Inherent Fe, Ca, and unburned carbon in CFA can act as nucleation poisons, limiting phase selectivity and reducing crystallinity. Advanced pre-treatment and purification of extracts are essential but increase process complexity.
2.  **Phase Selectivity & Reproducibility:** The heterogeneous composition of CFA batches leads to variability in product consistency. Machine learning models are being explored to predict optimal synthesis parameters for a given ash

# Results and Discussion

## Characterization of Zeolite-Based FCC Catalysts

The synthesized zeolite Y catalysts were characterized using multiple analytical techniques to establish structure-property relationships relevant to fluid catalytic cracking performance. X-ray diffraction analysis confirmed the successful formation of the FAU-type framework structure. The diffraction patterns exhibited characteristic reflections at 2θ values of 6.2°, 10.1°, 11.9°, 15.6°, 18.7°, 20.4°, 23.7°, 26.9°, and 31.4°, corresponding to the (111), (220), (311), (331), (422), (440), (533), (642), and (751) planes of the cubic Fd-3m space group. These results are consistent with the expected Faujasite topology and match previously reported patterns for zeolite Y synthesized under similar hydrothermal conditions (Corma et al., 1994). The relative crystallinity, calculated from the integrated peak areas relative to a commercial zeolite Y reference, was determined to be 94 ± 3%, indicating high phase purity without detectable amorphous contamination.

The textural properties were evaluated via nitrogen physisorption at 77 K. The BET surface area of the calcined zeolite Y was measured at 723 m²/g, which falls within the typical range of 600–800 m²/g reported for zeolite Y with Si/Al ratios between 2.5 and 6.0. This value exceeds the 510 m²/g reported by Du et al. (2004) for conventional zeolite Y prepared without optimization of synthesis凝胶化学, suggesting enhanced microporosity in the present material. The total pore volume was determined to be 0.42 cm³/g, with a micropore volume of 0.31 cm³/g obtained from t-plot analysis. These parameters indicate a well-developed porous framework conducive to molecular diffusion during catalytic cracking reactions.

The acidic properties were quantified through temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia (NH₃-TPD) and FTIR spectroscopy using pyridine as a probe molecule. The NH₃-TPD profiles displayed two distinct desorption maxima at approximately 210°C and 380°C, attributed to weak/medium and strong acid sites, respectively. The total acid site concentration, calculated by integrating the desorption peaks, was 1.24 mmol/g, which compares favorably with the range of 0.8–1.5 mmol/g reported in the literature for zeolite Y catalysts with similar Si/Al ratios. Pyridine-FTIR measurements revealed Brønsted acid site density of 0.68 mmol/g and Lewis acid site density of 0.31 mmol/g, yielding a Brønsted/Lewis ratio of 2.2. # High-Throughput Screening of Zeolite Synthesis Conditions

## Procedure for Systematic Optimization of MFI Zeolite Crystallization

### Gel Preparation and Precursor Solution Preparation

Aluminum sulfate (0.456 g, analytical grade) was dissolved in deionized water (45 mL) in a 100-mL polypropylene bottle under magnetic stirring at 300 rpm. Sodium hydroxide (4.8 g, ≥97% purity) was added gradually to the solution, and the mixture was stirred for 30 minutes at 25°C until complete dissolution was achieved. Tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS, 28.4 g, 98% purity) was added dropwise to the clear aluminate solution while maintaining vigorous stirring at 400 rpm. The molar ratio of the final gel was adjusted to SiO₂:Al₂O₃:Na₂O:H₂O = 1:0.02:0.4:40, and stirring was continued for 4 hours at ambient temperature to ensure homogeneous hydrolysis and condensation of silicate species.

### High-Throughput Gel Dispensing

The clear synthesis gel was transferred to a 250-mL HDPE reservoir bottle and cooled to 4°C for 30 minutes to suppress premature crystallization. Using a robotic liquid handling system (e.g., Chemspeed SWING), aliquots of 8.0 ± 0.1 g of the gel were dispensed into 24 individual 15-mL PTFE-lined stainless steel autoclave reactors arranged in a high-throughput array block. The gel was distributed across the array with systematic variations in synthesis parameters, including aging time (0-48 hours at 25°C), crystallization temperature (120°C, 140°C, 160°C, or 180°C), and crystallization duration (6, 12, 24, 48, or 72 hours).

### Hydrothermal Crystallization

The sealed autoclave array was placed in a forced-air convection oven preheated to the designated crystallization temperature with ±1°C accuracy. The reactors were held statically for the predetermined duration without agitation. After the crystallization period was completed, the oven was allowed to cool naturally to 60°C over 2 hours, and the reactors were subsequently removed and quenched to 25°C in a water bath for 15 minutes.

### Product Isolation and Washing

The solid product was separated from the mother liquor by vacuum filtration using a 0.45-μm nylon membrane filter. Each filter cake was washed with deionized water (50 mL × 3 portions) until the filtrate pH reached 8.5 ± 0.3. The washed solids were examined for crystallinity, and representative samples from each reactor were analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction to confirm phase purity.

### Drying and Calcination

The filter cakes were transferred to ceramic crucibles and dried in a muffle furnace at 80°C for 12 hours under static air conditions to remove physisorbed water. Subsequently, the temperature was ramped at 1°C/min to 550°C and held for 6 hours to decompose the organic structure-directing agent (if applicable) or to remove template molecules. The furnace was cooled to 150°C at 2°C/min, and the calcined zeolite was removed and stored in a desiccator over anhydrous CaSO₄.

### Characterization and Typical Results

Crystallization products were analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction (PANalytical X'Pert Pro, Cu Kα radiation, λ = 1.5406 Å) over the 2θ range of 5-50°. Representative diffraction patterns for successfully synthesized MFI zeolite exhibited characteristic reflections at 2θ = 7.9°, 8.8°, 23.1°, 23.3°, and 24.0° corresponding to the

Steam-assisted crystallization (SAC), classified within the broader family of dry-gel conversion (DGC) techniques, has emerged as a sophisticated methodology for the synthesis of zeolitic materials, offering significant advantages over conventional hydrothermal synthesis. This technique is characterized by the conversion of a dry aluminosilicate gel into a crystalline microporous framework via exposure to steam vapor, rather than immersion in a liquid solvent phase. The process typically begins with the preparation of a precursor gel comprising a silica source, such as tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) or colloidal silica, an aluminum source like sodium aluminate, and an organic structure-directing agent (SDA), for instance, tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH). This mixture is dried at elevated temperatures (e.g., 353 K) to form a solid xerogel, which is subsequently placed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave above a reservoir of deionized water, ensuring no physical contact between the solid and liquid phases.

During the SAC process, the autoclave is heated to temperatures typically ranging from 423 K to 473 K (150°C to 200°C) for durations varying between 24 and 120 hours, depending on the target topology. The water generates steam, creating a saturated vapor phase that permeates the dry gel. This steam serves a dual function: it acts as a mineralizing agent, facilitating the hydrolysis and condensation reactions necessary for framework assembly, and provides the medium for mass transport of SDA and inorganic species. A critical parameter in SAC is the steam-to-gel ratio, which dictates the chemical potential of water within the system. Excessive steam can lead to the leaching of active species, whereas insufficient steam may result in incomplete crystallization or the persistence of amorphous material.

Scientific investigations have elucidated the unique capabilities of SAC in controlling crystal morphology and defect density. For example, the synthesis of MFI-type zeolites (ZSM-5/silicalite-1) via SAC has been reported to yield highly crystalline phases with reduced organic template content compared to hydrothermal routes. Research indicates that the absence of bulk liquid solvent minimizes Ostwald ripening, thereby favoring the formation of nanocrystals or uniform sub-micron aggregates. Notably, studies have demonstrated the efficacy of SAC in producing hierarchical zeolites; by manipulating the drying conditions of the precursor gel or introducing pore-swelling agents, researchers can introduce mesoporosity into the final product. This is particularly advantageous for catalytic applications involving bulky molecules, as the hierarchical structure improves diffusivity while retaining shape selectivity.

Furthermore, SAC allows for the synthesis of compositions sensitive to dissolution, such as high-silica or germanium-containing zeolites. The vapor-phase transport mechanism mitigates the loss of heteroatoms into a mother liquor, ensuring high product yields with precise Si/Al ratios. Consequently, steam-assisted crystallization stands as a versatile and environmentally benign strategy for the tailored fabrication of advanced microporous materials.

### Literature Review: Steam Stability of Zeolite Catalysts

#### Introduction
Zeolite catalysts are indispensable in petrochemical and environmental catalysis, but their hydrothermal stability remains a critical performance limitation. Under steam-rich conditions (e.g., fluid catalytic cracking, methanol-to-olefins, automotive exhaust treatment), dealumination and structural collapse degrade acidity and porosity. This review synthesizes advances from 2014–2024 in enhancing steam stability through synthesis engineering, with emphasis on framework-dependent mechanisms.

#### Synthesis Approaches and Framework-Specific Outcomes
Recent strategies prioritize **isomorphous substitution, hierarchical structuring, and post-synthetic modification** to mitigate steam-induced degradation. Key findings include:

1. **Aluminum Zoning and Mesoporosity**: For **MFI (ZSM-5)** zeolites synthesized with **TPAOH** (tetrapropylammonium hydroxide), gradient aluminum distributions achieved via controlled temperature ramping (e.g., 100→180°C over 48 h) reduce dealumination rates. Studies show that **intracrystalline mesopores (2–10 nm)** introduced via soft templating (e.g., using amphiphilic polymers) lower steam-induced stress, preserving >85% crystallinity after 20 h at 800°C with 100% steam, versus <50% for conventional analogues.

2. **Stabilization via Heteroatom Incorporation**: In **BEA (Beta)** zeolites, **TEAOH** (tetraethylammonium hydroxide)-directed syntheses yield frameworks where **isomorphous substitution of Al with Sn or Zr** enhances stability. For instance, Sn-BEA treated at 750°C under 30% steam retains 95% of its Lewis acidity, attributed to stronger M–O bonds (M = Sn, Zr) resisting hydrolysis.

3. **Small-Pore Zeolites for Severe Conditions**: **CHA (SSZ-13)** frameworks, synthesized with **TMAdaOH** (N,N,N-trimethyl-1-adamantammonium hydroxide) as an SDA, exhibit exceptional stability due to their double-six-ring (D6R) units and low Al content. Optimal Si/Al ratios of 15–25 maintain >90% crystallinity after aging at 850°C in 10% steam for 16 h, crucial for diesel exhaust SCR-DeNOx applications.

4. **FAU (Y) Zeolite Enhancements**: Ultra-stable Y (USY) zeolites, prepared via combined **dealumination and lanthanum exchange**, show synergistic effects. Sequential steaming (600°C) and acid leaching remove extra-framework Al while La³⁺ ions migrate into hexagonal prisms, stabilizing the sodalite cages. This yields catalysts with 40% higher micropore retention after harsh hydrothermal aging (760°C, 100% steam).

5. **Emerging Framework Engineering**: **CON-type (SSZ-55)** and **AEI-type (SSZ-39)** zeolites synthesized with bulky, rigid SDAs (e.g., 1,4-diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane derivatives) demonstrate resilience due to their interlinked cages and reduced defect densities. In-situ XRD studies reveal that these frameworks tolerate 900°C steam exposure with <10% unit-cell contraction.

#### Mechanistic Insights
Advanced characterization techniques (e.g., *in-situ* NMR, synchrotron XRD, and TEM) have elucidated degradation pathways:
- **Steam attack initiates at framework defects** (silanols, grain boundaries), propagating via hydrolysis of Si–O–Al bonds.
- **Proximity of Al sites** accelerates dealumination; isolated Al in low-density frameworks (e.g., CHA) resists hydrolysis better.
- **Cationic SDAs** that template dense, defect-free regions (e.g., **TEA⁺** in BEA) yield more hydrothermally robust crystals.

#### Challenges and Future Directions
Despite progress, key challenges persist:
- **Scalability of advanced syntheses**: Multi-step templating and heteroatom incorporation increase costs.
- **Long-term stability under cyclic conditions**: Few studies address combined thermal, steam, and chemical stressors (e.g., SOx, alkali metals).
- **Predictive modeling**: Relating SDA chemistry to stability outcomes remains empirical.

Future research should focus on:
1. **Machine learning-guided SDA design** to target frameworks with intrinsic hydrothermal resilience.
2. **Defect engineering** via dry-gel conversion or fluoride-mediated synthesis to minimize silanol nests.
3. **Composite architectures** (e.g., zeolite@mesoporous oxide core-shell) for diffusion-limited stability enhancement.

#### Conclusion
The past decade has transformed zeolite steam stability from a materials limitation to a tunable property. By integrating framework-specific synthesis with mechanistic understanding,

# NU-87 Zeolite: Synthesis, Structure, and Pore Architecture

## Introduction

NU-87, along with itsUiO-87 designation in some literature, represents a significant member of the high-silica zeolite family discovered during the extensive molecular sieve exploration programs conducted by Mobil Research and Development Corporation in the late 1980s and early 1990s. This zeolite distinguishes itself through a remarkable two-dimensional pore system that incorporates both ten-membered ring (10MR) and twelve-membered ring (12MR) channels, rendering it particularly valuable for shape-selective catalytic and separation applications involving medium-sized hydrocarbon molecules.

## Framework Structure and Pore Architecture

NU-87 crystallizes in the orthorhombic space group **Cmcm** with unit cell parameters approximately a = 20.1 Å, b = 14.0 Å, and c = 7.5 Å. The framework exhibits a complex three-dimensional channel system characterized by **linear 10-membered ring channels** running parallel to the [001] direction with effective pore dimensions of approximately 5.5 × 5.5 Å. These intersect with ** sinusoidal 12-membered ring channels** oriented along the [010] direction, presenting pore openings of roughly 6.5 × 7.5 Å. This dual-pore architecture creates a unique interconnected network wherein molecules can access both channel systems through intersections, facilitating diffusion pathways that differ substantially from conventional single-pore zeolites such as ZSM-5 (MFI) or beta zeolite (BEA).

The framework possesses a SiO₂ composition with aluminum substitution occurring at tetrahedral sites, yielding Si/Al ratios typically exceeding 20, though ratios as low as 12 have been reported under specific synthesis conditions. This high silica content contributes to the framework's notable hydrothermal stability and hydrophobic character.

## Synthesis Methodology

The synthesis of NU-87 employs **hydrothermal crystallization** techniques utilizing organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs). The most effective templates include **benzyltriethylammonium chloride (BTEAC)**, **tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH)**, and various benzyl-substituted quaternary ammonium species. The general synthesis gel composition encompasses:

- **SiO₂**: 1.0
- **Al₂O₃**: 0.005–0.05 (corresponding to Si/Al ratios of 20–200)
- **Na₂O**: 0.05–0.30
- **OSDA**: 0.10–0.40
- **H₂O

# Results and Discussion

## X-ray Diffraction Analysis of Zeolite Catalysts

### Crystallographic Characterization

X-ray diffraction analysis confirmed the successful synthesis of all zeolite samples with high crystallinity and phase purity. The XRD pattern of the as-synthesized ZSM-5 zeolite (Si/Al = 25) exhibited characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ = 7.96°, 8.82°, 23.18°, 23.98°, and 24.50°, corresponding to the orthorhombic MFI framework structure according to JCPDS card No. 44-0003. The peak at 2θ = 23.18° exhibited the highest intensity, consistent with the (501) crystallographic plane of the MFI topology. No diffraction peaks corresponding to amorphous silica or competing crystalline phases were observed, indicating phase purity exceeding 95%.

The Beta zeolite sample displayed characteristic broad diffraction peaks at 2θ = 7.80° and 22.50°, indicative of the intergrowth of polymorph A and polymorph B in the BEA framework structure. The relatively broad peak widths suggested crystallite sizes in the range of 15-25 nm, calculated using the Scherrer equation from the full width at half-maximum (FWHM) of the 2θ = 22.50° reflection.

### Textural Properties

N₂ physisorption measurements revealed distinct Type I isotherms characteristic of microporous materials. The parent ZSM-5 zeolite exhibited a Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of 425 m²/g and a micropore volume of 0.19 cm³/g, values consistent with reported literature values of 400-450 m²/g for MFI-type materials. Following ammonium exchange and calcination to the protonic form, the BET surface area increased marginally to 438 m²/g, attributed to the removal of template molecules and cations from the pore system.

The hierarchical Beta-S support displayed a combined microporous and mesoporous character, with BET surface area of 612 m²/g and total pore volume of 0.48 cm³/g. The external surface area of 145 m²/g, determined by the t-plot method, was substantially higher than that of conventional microporous zeolites (20-40 m²/g), confirming the successful introduction of mesoporosity through the desilication treatment employed during synthesis.

### Acid Site Characterization

NH₃-temperature-programmed desorption (NH₃-TPD) quantified the total acid site concentration and strength distribution across all samples. The ZSM-5 zeolite exhibited total acid site density of 0.84 mmol/g, with approximately 35% classified as strong acid sites (desorption temperature > 350°C) and 65% as medium-strength sites. These values align with published reports for zeolites with similar Si/Al ratios, where total acidity typically ranges from 0.70-1.00 mmol/g.

The introduction of metal species via ion exchange reduced the total acid site concentration to 0.71 mmol/g while modifying the acid strength distribution, with strong acid sites comprising 42% of total acidity. This modification influenced the catalytic performance in subsequent transformation reactions.

### Structure-Property Relationships

The correlation between framework composition and catalytic properties merits discussion. The decrease in Si/Al ratio from 50 to 15 in the ZSM-5 series produced a linear increase in total acid site concentration from 0.52 to 1.15 mmol/g, consistent with the established relationship wherein each aluminum site generates one Brønsted acid site. However, the micropore volume remained relatively constant (0.18-0.20 cm³/g) across all compositions, indicating that the microporous structure remained intact regardless of aluminum content.

Over the past decade, the dealumination of zeolites has evolved from a bulk desilication technique into a precise tool for tailoring acidity and mesoporosity. The primary objective remains the removal of framework aluminum to increase the Si/Al ratio, thereby enhancing hydrothermal stability and modulating Brønsted acid site (BAS) density. Recent advances focus on "top-down" post-synthetic modifications that preserve crystallinity while introducing hierarchical porosity.

In the modification of MFI (ZSM-5) frameworks, the role of the organic structure-directing agent (OSDA) in the parent synthesis has proven critical. Studies indicate that ZSM-5 synthesized using TPAOH exhibits a homogeneous Al distribution, whereas TEAOH often yields Al zoning. This zoning dictates dealumination behavior; recent work employing mild chelating agents, such as oxalic acid or EDTA, demonstrates that homogeneous MFI frameworks are more resistant to amorphization. For instance, treatment with 0.1 M oxalic acid at 80°C can selectively extract Al, generating secondary mesopores (2–10 nm) without significant microporous loss, a vital parameter for biomass catalysis.

For large-pore frameworks like FAU (Y and USY), the traditional steaming-acid leaching sequence has been refined. Hydrothermal treatment at 500–700°C creates mesopores via the extraction of EFAL (extra-framework aluminum), but often results in severe structural degradation. Recent literature highlights the use of fluorinated acids (e.g., NH4HF2) as a superior alternative. This method selectively removes Al at lower temperatures (<100°C), yielding USY with superior surface areas (>700 m²/g) and optimized acid site distribution for fluid catalytic cracking (FCC).

The dealumination of small-pore CHA (SSZ-13) has garnered significant attention for selective catalytic reduction (SCR) applications. In Cu-CHA catalysts, hydrothermal aging induces dealumination, leading to Cu ion migration and deactivation. To counteract this, researchers are exploring post-synthetic "mild steaming" at 550–650°C to pre-stabilize the framework before Cu ion exchange. This creates a more robust Si-rich framework that resists further dealumination during operation. However, a key challenge identified in recent years is the preferential removal of Al from specific crystallographic sites (e.g., the 4-membered rings in CHA), leading to heterogeneous acid site strength.

Current challenges include the precise control of Al siting during dealumination and the mitigation of EFAL blockage, which can obscure active sites. Future directions are pivoting towards computational modeling combined with in-situ spectroscopy to predict Al extraction kinetics. Additionally, the integration of dealumination with desilication (combinatorial demetallation) offers a pathway to engineer zeolites with bimodal pore architectures, essential for processing bulky molecules in petrochemical and fine chemical synthesis.

**Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectrometry (ICP-OES) for the Quantitative Elemental Analysis of Zeolitic Materials**

**Introduction**

Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectrometry (ICP-OES) has established itself as an indispensable analytical technique for the precise quantification of elemental composition in zeolite science. This capability is critical for determining framework (e.g., Si, Al, P, B, Ga, Ge) and extra-framework (e.g., Na, K, Ca, transition metals) constituents, thereby defining the material's acidity, ion-exchange capacity, catalytic activity, and stability. Unlike bulk techniques such as X-ray fluorescence (XRF), ICP-OES requires complete sample digestion, providing a solution-based analysis that is both highly sensitive and capable of multi-elemental determination with detection limits typically in the parts-per-billion (ppb) range.

**Sample Digestion: A Critical Pre-Analytical Step**

The refractory nature of aluminosilicate zeolites necessitates rigorous, high-temperature acid digestion to achieve complete dissolution. A widely adopted and robust protocol involves the use of a combination of hydrofluoric acid (HF) and a strong mineral acid, such as nitric (HNO₃) or perchloric (HClO₄) acid, often in a pressurized microwave-assisted digestion system to enhance efficiency and safety.

A standard procedure for a ~0.1 g zeolite sample (e.g., ZSM-5, zeolite Y, or SAPO-34) is as follows: The accurately weighed solid is transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) digestion vessel. Subsequently, 3 mL of concentrated HNO₃ (69% w/w) and 2 mL of concentrated HF (48% w/w) are added. The sealed vessels are then placed in a microwave digestion system. A typical temperature program ramps to 200°C over 15 minutes, holds at 200°C for 30 minutes under pressure (e.g., 40 bar), and then cools. The resulting digestate must be treated to remove residual HF, often by evaporation to near-dryness with HNO₃ or complexation with saturated boric acid (H₃BO₃), before final dilution to a known volume (e.g., 50 mL) with deionized water and acidification with 2% HNO₃ to stabilize the analyte ions in solution.

**ICP-OES Instrumental Analysis and Calibration**

The prepared solution is introduced into the ICP-OES via a pneumatic nebulizer (e.g., a concentric glass nebulizer with a cyclonic spray chamber). The plasma, sustained by argon gas and a radiofrequency (RF) generator operating at 27.12 or 40.68 MHz, reaches excitation temperatures of 6000-8000 K, effectively atomizing and exciting the constituent elements. The characteristic emission lines are then resolved by an echelle grating spectrometer and detected by a charge-coupled device (CCD).

Quantitative analysis is performed using external calibration with matrix-matched standards. For silicate-based zeolites, it is crucial that calibration standards contain a similar concentration of silicon and acid matrix to compensate for potential interferences, such as slight variations in nebulization efficiency or ionization suppression. Key analytical emission lines are selected to avoid spectral overlaps; for instance, Al is commonly measured at 396.152 nm, Si at 251.611 nm, and P at 213.618 nm. The ratio of intensities for these lines directly yields the molar composition (e.g., Si/Al ratio), a fundamental parameter in zeolite characterization. For example, Fickel et al. (2011) utilized ICP-OES to precisely determine the decreasing Si/Al ratio in a series of dealuminated HY zeolites, directly correlating it with the loss of Brønsted acid sites measured by pyridine FT-IR spectroscopy.

**Applications and Data Interpretation**

ICP-OES data is foundational for confirming zeolite synthesis outcomes, monitoring post-synthetic modifications, and characterizing spent catalysts. In the synthesis of metal-substituted zeolites, such as Fe-ZSM-5 or Co-SAPO-34, ICP-OES provides the definitive metal loading and framework incorporation efficiency. For instance, in the study of Cu-SSZ-13 catalysts for the selective catalytic reduction (SCR) of NOx, Deka et al. (2013) relied on ICP-OES to quantify copper content, distinguishing between ion-exchanged and occluded copper oxide species when combined with spectroscopic techniques.

Furthermore, analysis of trace impurities (e.g., Fe, Ca, Mg) from raw materials or reactor corrosion can be pivotal in explaining variations in catalytic performance or hydrothermal stability. The technique's precision, typically with relative standard deviations (RSD) below 2% for major elements, allows for the detection of subtle compositional gradients within crystallites or between batches.

**Conclusion**

ICP-OES remains a cornerstone of
