# Results and Discussion

## Characterization of Zeolite-Supported Single-Atom Catalysts

The X-ray diffraction patterns of the synthesized Pt single-atom catalysts supported on HZSM-5 zeolite (Pt-SAC/HZSM-5) and the bare HZSM-5 support are presented in Figure 1. The diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 7.9°, 8.8°, 23.1°, 23.8°, and 24.4° correspond to the characteristic MFI framework structure, confirming the preservation of zeolite crystallinity following Pt incorporation. Notably, no distinct Pt metallic diffraction peaks were observed at 2θ = 39.8° or 46.2°, indicating the successful isolation of Pt atoms within the zeolite framework rather than nanoparticle formation. The slight reduction in peak intensity (approximately 8%) observed for Pt-SAC/HZSM-5 relative to the parent zeolite suggests minor framework distortions induced by Pt atom incorporation, consistent with previous reports by researchers who documented similar observations for transition metal single-atom catalysts on microporous supports.

The textural properties of the catalysts were evaluated through nitrogen physisorption measurements, and the results are summarized in Table 1. The parent HZSM-5 zeolite exhibited a BET surface area of 362 m²/g with a micropore volume of 0.18 cm³/g, values consistent with reported literature values for conventionally synthesized ZSM-5 materials (350–380 m²/g; 0.15–0.20 cm³/g). Following Pt single-atom loading (0.5 wt%), the BET surface area decreased marginally to 348 m²/g, representing a 3.9% reduction. Similarly, the micropore volume decreased from 0.18 to 0.16 cm³/g. These modest reductions are attributable to the occupation of some micropore volume by Pt single atoms dispersed within the zeolite channels, rather than surface blockage, as the external surface area remained essentially unchanged (42 m²/g versus 44 m²/g).

**Table 1. Textural and acidic properties of HZSM-5 and Pt-SAC/HZSM-5**

| Sample | BET Surface Area (m²/g) | Micropore Volume (cm³/g) | External Surface Area (m²/g) | Acid Site Concentration (mmol/g) |
|--------|-------------------------|--------------------------|------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| HZSM-5 | 362                     | 0.18                     | 44                           | 1.12                              |
| Pt-SAC/HZSM-5 | 348              | 0.16                     | 42                           | 0.87                              |

The acid site concentrations, determined by temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia (NH₃-TPD), revealed a significant modification of the acidic properties upon Pt single-atom incorporation. The parent HZSM-5 zeolite exhibited a total acid site concentration of 1.12 mmol/g, comprising both Brønsted and Lewis acid sites. Following Pt single-atom loading, the acid site concentration decreased to 0.87 mmol/g, representing a 22% reduction. This observation suggests that Pt single atoms partially occupy or neutralize some of the zeolite acid sites, likely through electronic interactions with framework aluminum atoms. This finding aligns with the work of Zhang et al., who reported similar acid site reductions (18–25%) upon loading transition metal single atoms onto zeolitic supports and attributed this phenomenon to the formation of metal–acid site adducts that modify the protonic acidity.

# Synthesis of FER-Type Zeolite Ferrierite: Recent Advances and Methodological Approaches

## 1. Introduction

Ferrierite, characterized by the three-letter code **FER**, represents one of the most structurally well-defined medium-pore zeolites, featuring a distinctive two-dimensional channel system comprising 8-membered ring (8-MR) pores (4.3 × 5.5 Å) intersected by 10-membered ring (10-MR) channels (4.2 × 5.4 Å) (Vaughan, 1966; Artioli, 1994). The FER framework exhibits remarkable thermal stability and has garnered significant attention for applications in hydrocarbon isomerization, acetylation, and selective catalytic reduction of nitrogen oxides. The successful synthesis of pure-phase FER zeolite remains challenging due to the metastable nature of this framework and the competitive crystallization of undesired phases such as MFI, TON, and GIS.

## 2. Conventional Hydrothermal Synthesis

The classical synthesis of ferrierite employs alkali-metal-containing gel systems at elevated temperatures. The archetypal procedure, as established by **Mobius and coworkers** in the 1980s, utilizes initial gels with molar compositions typically ranging from 6-12 Na₂O : 0.5-2 K₂O : 1 Al₂O₃ : 30-60 SiO₂ : 400-800 H₂O, with pyrrolidine or ethylenediamine serving as structure-directing agents (SDAs). **Coudurier and coworkers** (1985) demonstrated that the simultaneous presence of Na⁺ and K⁺ cations proves essential for pure FER crystallization, whereas gels containing only Na⁺ typically yield GIS or SOD phases.

Crystallization proceeds under **autogenous pressure** at temperatures between **150°C and 180°C** for durations of **3 to 14 days**. Studies by **Perego and colleagues** (1996) established that optimal crystallization occurs at 160°C over 7 days, yielding phase-pure materials with Si/Al ratios between 5 and 20. Lower temperatures (140°C) extend crystallization times to 10-14 days, while temperatures exceeding 200°C promote the formation of intergrowth structures and framework defects.

## 3. Template-Directed Synthesis and Clear Solution Methods

The development of **clear solution synthesis** methodologies, pioneered by **Kumar and coworkers** (1990), represented a significant advancement in FER synthesis. These approaches employ homogeneous aluminosilicate solutions wherein nucleation and crystal growth occur in the absence of gel particles. The seminal work by **Kerr and Kokotailo** (1961) on ferrierite synthesis using pyrrolidine established the foundation for modern organic template-directed routes.

Clear solution protocols typically employ gels with compositions approximating **1.0 Al₂O₃ : 30-40 SiO₂ : 2.0-4.0 SDA : 400-800 H₂O**, where SDAs include **pyrrolidine**, **ethylenediamine**, **piperidine**, or **cyclohexylamine**. **Chen and coworkers** (1996) systematically investigated the SDA structure-activity relationship, demonstrating that cyclic amines with appropriate molecular dimensions preferentially direct FER crystallization. The replacement of pyrrolidine with N-methyl-1,3-diaminopropane, as reported by **Pophale and coworkers** (2013), enables synthesis of FER with enhanced Si/Al ratios approaching 50.

## 4. Seed-Directed Synthesis and Boron-Containing Systems

The introduction of **seed-directed synthesis** has emerged as a powerful strategy for phase-selective FER

The fabrication of oriented zeolite films and membranes represents a pivotal advancement in molecular sieve technology, primarily because controlled crystallographic orientation minimizes the tortuosity of diffusion pathways and mitigates the deleterious effects of intercrystalline grain boundaries. Among the various frameworks, the MFI-type zeolites (Silicalite-1 and ZSM-5) have been the subject of extensive scrutiny due to their sinusoidal and straight channel system, which is ideally suited for size-selective separations of hydrocarbon isomers.

The secondary growth method, pioneered by researchers such as Tsapatsis and co-workers, remains the benchmark for synthesizing highly oriented membranes. This two-step process involves the deposition of a monolayer of colloidal seeds onto a porous support, typically $\alpha$-alumina or stainless steel, followed by hydrothermal recrystallization. To achieve $b$-oriented MFI films—where the straight channels run perpendicular to the support surface—researchers utilize seed suspensions synthesized from clear solutions with a molar composition of $SiO_2$ : $TPAOH$ : $H_2O$ = 1 : 0.1 : 100, treated at 80°C for 24 hours to yield nanocrystals of approximately 50–100 nm. The seeding technique is critical; the "rubbing" method or dip-coating at controlled withdrawal rates (e.g., 1 cm/min) ensures a dense, uniform monolayer. Subsequent hydrothermal growth is conducted in a synthesis gel often containing structure-directing agents (SDAs) like tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH) at 175°C for 4 to 8 hours.

To further enhance orientation and suppress random nucleation, growth modifiers such as diquaternary ammonium salts (e.g., C$_6$-diquat) are frequently introduced into the synthesis gel. These modifiers preferentially adsorb onto specific crystal faces, inhibiting growth along the $a$-axis and thereby promoting the elongation of crystals along the $b$-axis. For instance, the addition of 0.1 M C$_6$-diquat has been shown to increase the intensity ratio of the (020) XRD peak relative to the (501) peak by an order of magnitude, confirming superior orientation.

Alternatively, in-situ hydrothermal synthesis on functionalized substrates offers a one-step route to orientation. Modifying the support surface with cationic polymers, such as poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDADMA), or self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) of chlorosilanes creates a charged interface that directs the nucleation of specific zeolite faces. Furthermore, fluoride-mediated synthesis has emerged as a method to produce defect-free films. In this system, NH$_4$F replaces hydroxide as the mineralizer, operating at near-neutral pH and elevated temperatures (e.g., 200°C for 48 hours). This approach significantly reduces the density of silanol defects, enhancing the hydrothermal stability of the membrane.

The performance benefits of orientation are quantified in separation applications. $b$-oriented MFI membranes have demonstrated separation factors ($\alpha$) for $p$-xylene/$o$-xylene mixtures exceeding 100 at temperatures above 150°C, vastly outperforming randomly oriented membranes which typically exhibit $\alpha$ values between 10 and 30 due to the prevalence of non-selective grain boundary defects. Consequently, the precise control of synthesis parameters—specifically SDA concentration, thermal profile, and surface chemistry—is essential for exploiting the anisotropic transport properties of zeolitic materials.

## **Supercritical Fluid Synthesis of Zeolites: A Novel Pathway to Hierarchical Architectures**

### **Introduction**
The synthesis of zeolites under supercritical conditions represents a frontier in the design of microporous and mesoporous materials, offering a unique physicochemical environment distinct from conventional hydrothermal or solvothermal methods. In this context, a supercritical fluid (SCF)—typically water (scH₂O) or carbon dioxide (scCO₂)—serves as a solvent, reaction medium, and sometimes a structure-directing agent (SDA) or porogen. The synthesis is conducted above the critical point (Tc, Pc) of the solvent, where it exhibits properties intermediate between a liquid and a gas: low viscosity, high diffusivity, near-zero surface tension, and tunable density and solvation power via modest changes in pressure and temperature. These properties can dramatically influence nucleation kinetics, crystal growth, and ultimate material morphology.

### **Experimental Paradigms and Conditions**
Two primary SCF systems dominate the literature: supercritical water and supercritical carbon dioxide, often used in conjunction with organic additives or ionic liquids.

**1. Supercritical Water Synthesis (Hydrothermal-SCF Hybrid):**
This approach typically employs reaction temperatures between 400–500 °C and pressures of 23–35 MPa, well above the critical point of water (Tc = 374 °C, Pc = 22.1 MPa). A seminal study by Tompsett et al. (2006) demonstrated the rapid crystallization of silicalite-1 (MFI type) from a clear solution of tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS) and tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH) in scH₂O at 400 °C and 27 MPa. Crystallization occurred within 30–120 minutes, orders of magnitude faster than under conventional hydrothermal conditions (often 24–72 hours). The low viscosity and high mass transfer in scH₂O promote rapid supersaturation and homogeneous nucleation, often yielding small, uniform crystals (50–200 nm). The absence of a liquid-vapor interface also minimizes capillary forces during mesopore formation when using soft templates.

**2. Supercritical CO₂-Mediated Synthesis:**
Here, scCO₂ (Tc = 31 °C, Pc = 7.38 MPa) acts as a co-solvent, porosity modifier, or reaction medium at milder temperatures (40–150 °C) and pressures of 10–30 MPa. A notable methodology, reported by García-Martínez et al. (2012), involves the post-synthetic treatment of conventional zeolites (e.g., ZSM-5, zeolite Y) with scCO₂ containing dissolved organosilanes (e.g., phenylaminopropyltrimethoxysilane). Conducted at 50 °C and 20 MPa for 2–4 hours, this process facilitates the diffusion of silane precursors into the zeolite micropores, where they condense to create intracrystalline mesopores (2–10 nm) upon calcination. This "desilication-recrystallization" mechanism under scCO₂ yields hierarchical zeolites with preserved crystallinity and acidity, demonstrating superior catalytic performance in reactions like fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) due to enhanced molecular transport.

### **Mechanistic Insights and Material Characteristics**
The supercritical environment alters fundamental crystallization pathways. The high diffusivity suppresses Ostwald ripening, leading to narrow particle size distributions. Furthermore, the tunable solvent power allows for the dissolution and re-deposition of silica species, facilitating the creation of interconnected micro-mesopore networks *in situ*. For instance, the synthesis of zeolite beta (*BEA*) in a scH₂O–ionic liquid ([C₄mim][BF₄]) system at 420 °C and 25 MPa for 6 hours produced crystals with intrinsic mesoporosity (5–15 nm) and exceptional thermal stability (>1000 °C), as characterized by N₂ physisorption and TEM (Wang et al., 2015).

### **Applications and Outlook**
Zeolites synthesized or modified under supercritical conditions exhibit superior properties for catalysis, adsorption, and separation. Their hierarchical porosity mitigates diffusion limitations in bulky molecule reactions (e.g., alkylation, isomerization). Challenges remain in scaling up the high-pressure processes and precisely controlling long-range order. Future research is directed toward exploring novel SCF-SDA combinations, continuous-flow reactors, and the synthesis of metastable or novel framework types inaccessible via conventional routes.

- García-Martínez, J., et al. (2012). *Science*, 336(6089), 1684-1687

# Zeolite-Based Fluid Catalytic Cracking: Recent Advances and Future Perspectives

Fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) remains the cornerstone of modern petroleum refining, converting heavy gas oil and vacuum gas oil into high-value gasoline, light olefins, and diesel. Zeolites serve as the active components driving these transformations, and the past decade has witnessed significant advances in zeolite synthesis, modification, and application within FCC catalysts.

**Advances in Framework Types and Synthesis**

The traditional FCC catalyst relies predominantly on ultrastable Y (USY) zeolite (FAU framework) due to its large pore aperture (7.4 Å) and high acidity. However, research from 2015-2024 has expanded the zeolite palette considerably. ZSM-5 (MFI framework) has gained prominence for its shape-selectivity toward light olefins, with studies by Liu et al. (2018) demonstrating that incorporating 15-25 wt% ZSM-5 into conventional Y-based catalysts increased propylene yield by 2-4 absolute percentage points. The use of CHA framework zeolites (chabazite) has emerged as a promising development, offering medium-pore selectivity (3.8 Å) that enhances gasoline octane by promoting branched aromatics formation (Zhang et al., 2020).

Synthesis approaches have evolved substantially. Hydrothermal synthesis using tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH) remains standard for MFI zeolites, but seed-assisted and/or template-free methods have reduced costs considerably. Corma and colleagues (2017) demonstrated that organotemplate-free synthesis of beta zeolite (BEA) achieves comparable catalytic performance to template-directed routes while eliminating organic waste. Interzeolite conversion methods, wherein metastable zeolites transform into target frameworks under controlled conditions, have enabled access to phases previously considered difficult to obtain. For instance, successful synthesis of SAPO-34 (CHA) from FAU precursors has been reported by Sun et al. (2019), opening routes to heteroatom-containing catalysts with tuned acidity.

**Structure-Directing Agents and Acidity Control**

The choice of structure-directing agent (SDA) profoundly influences zeolite properties. Traditional SDAs include TPAOH for MFI, TEAOH for BEA, and sodium hydroxide for FAU. Recent work has explored mixed-templates and combinatorial approaches. Wang et al. (2021) reported that dual-SDA systems combining TPAOH with cheaper quaternary ammonium salts yield zeolites with optimized acid site distribution and reduced synthesis times. Post-synthetic treatments, including steam calcination, dealumination, and isomorphic substitution, remain critical for balancing acidity and stability. Steam dealumination of USY, for example, creates mesoporosity that improves diffusion while maintaining sufficient Brønsted acidity (Chen et al., 2022).

**Current Challenges and Future Directions**

Despite progress, several challenges persist. Coke formation remains the primary deactivation mechanism, particularly for light olefins production where consecutive reactions consume desired products. Hierarchical zeolites—featuring microporous frameworks with integrated mesoporosity—offer a partial solution, and commercial implementations have increased recently. Thermal and hydrothermal stability under FCC regenerator conditions (650-750°C, steam-rich atmosphere) continues to limit framework options.

Future directions emphasize computational materials design, wherein machine learning models predict optimal SDA-framework combinations before experimental synthesis. The integration of multiple zeolite components (Y, ZSM-5, beta) within engineered catalyst particles to create cascade reaction pathways represents another frontier. Additionally, emerging frameworks such as EMM-12 and ITQ-55 remain under evaluation for specialized FCC applications. The convergence of advanced characterization

# Desilication of Zeolites: Recent Advances and Future Perspectives

## Introduction

Desilication—the selective removal of silicon from zeolite frameworks—has emerged as a powerful postsynthetic modification strategy for enhancing the catalytic performance of microporous materials. Originally developed as a complementary approach to dealumination, desilication creates mesoporosity within traditionally microporous structures, addressing diffusion limitations that constrain catalyst lifetime and selectivity in industrial processes such as fluid catalytic cracking, hydrocarbon conversion, and biomass valorization.

## Mechanism and Process Window

The selective dissolution of framework silicon in zeolites proceeds via nucleophilic attack of hydroxide ions at silicon sites, with mechanism and selectivity strongly dependent on the Si/Al ratio and the framework type. Groen and colleagues established the critical Si/Al framework ratio of approximately 25–50 as the optimal window for mesoporosity development via alkaline treatment, below which excessive aluminum content protects the framework and above which uncontrolled dissolution occurs. Within this framework composition range, the presence of aluminum atoms serves as a natural inhibitor, creating a self-limiting desilication process that preferentially removes silicon from aluminum-poor domains.

## Recent Advances by Framework Type

**MFI Zeolites**: The past decade has seen substantial refinement of MFI desilication protocols. Studies employing TPAOH (tetrapropylammonium hydroxide) as a structure-directing agent during recrystallization have demonstrated precise control over mesoporosity, with recent work reporting tailored pore architectures achieving hierarchical structures with intercrystalline mesopores of 5–15 nm while preserving >85% microporosity. The incorporation of fluoride media during desilication has proven particularly effective for ZSM-5, enabling more selective silicon removal with minimal framework degradation.

**BEA Zeolites**: Beta zeolite desilication presents unique challenges due to its faulting and lower framework stability. Recent advances utilizing TEAOH (tetraethylammonium hydroxide) in combination with controlled alkaline treatment have yielded hierarchical Beta materials with improved accessibility for bulky molecules. Research by Pérez-Ramírez and colleagues has shown that sequential desilication-dealumination treatments produce bimodal pore systems with enhanced selectivity in alkylation reactions.

**FAU Zeolites**: USY (ultrastable Y) zeolites have benefited significantly from mild desilication protocols, with optimal treatments using dilute NaOH solutions (0.02–0.1 M) at 65–75°C. These conditions preferentially remove framework silicon from the supercages while maintaining the critical FAU structure integrity, improving diffusion coefficients by factors of 10–100 for large hydrocarbon molecules.

**CHA Zeolites**: Recent work on chabazite desilication has demonstrated that controlled silicon removal can enhance propylene selectivity in methanol-to-olefin reactions, with optimal performance achieved at Si/Al ratios of 15–20 following alkaline treatment.

## Role of Structure-Directing Agents and Additives

The past decade has revealed that SDAs play critical roles beyond initial synthesis. TPAOH, TEAOH, and novel polyamine-based SDAs serve dual functions during desilication—acting as pore-directing agents during recrystallization and preventing excessive framework collapse. Studies have demonstrated that cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) as a co-additive creates additional mesoporosity through a soft-templating mechanism concurrent with desilication.

## Current Challenges

Significant challenges remain in achieving precise control over mesoporosity distribution and connectivity. Reproducibility across different synthesis batches, the environmental impact of high-pH waste streams, and maintaining long-term hydrothermal stability of created mesoporosity represent ongoing concerns. Furthermore, the relationship between mesoporosity architecture and catalytic performance remains incompletely understood, particularly for shape-selective reactions.

## Future Directions

Future research should focus on developing environmentally sustainable desilication protocols, including aqueous ammonia and organic base alternatives. In situ characterization techniques during desilication, combined with machine learning approaches for parameter optimization, offer promising avenues for achieving unprecedented control over hierarchical zeolite architectures. The integration of des

Recent advances in zeolite science have significantly elucidated the hydrothermal synthesis pathways of analcime (ANA) and its complex phase transformation behavior. Analcime, a small-pore tectosilicate with the ANA framework type (space group $Ia3d$), is frequently identified as the thermodynamic sink in many low-silica aluminosilicate systems. Over the past decade, research has shifted from viewing ANA merely as an undesirable byproduct in the synthesis of gismondine (GIS) or sodalite (SOD) to understanding its controlled crystallization kinetics and potential utility in nuclear waste management and ion exchange.

The synthesis of pure analcime typically requires hydrothermal conditions ranging from 150°C to 200°C under autogenous pressure. Experimental parameters indicate a strict dependency on the Si/Al molar ratio, generally optimized between 2.0 and 2.5, and high alkalinity (NaOH/SiO₂ > 1.5). Unlike the synthesis of high-silica frameworks such as MFI or BEA, which critically rely on organic structure-directing agents (SDAs) like tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH) or tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH) to stabilize the porous architecture, ANA formation is predominantly driven by inorganic cations (Na⁺). Comparative studies demonstrate that while TPAOH directs the formation of the 10-ring MFI structure, its absence in Na-rich aluminosilicate gels invariably leads to the densification of the structure into the 8-ring ANA topology.

A major focus of recent literature involves interzeolite transformation (IZT), particularly the conversion of FAU-type zeolites into ANA. This transformation adheres to the Ostwald step rule, where the metastable FAU framework dissolves and reorganizes into the more thermodynamically stable ANA phase. Recent findings quantify this transformation, noting that at 180°C, NaY zeolite fully converts to ANA within 48–72 hours. The mechanism is identified as a "solution-mediated" transport rather than a solid-state reconstructive transition, characterized by an activation energy typically ranging from 45 to 65 kJ/mol. Furthermore, researchers have observed that the presence of residual organic SDAs from the parent FAU synthesis can retard the nucleation of ANA, prolonging the induction period.

Despite these advances, challenges remain in the morphology control of ANA crystals, which tend to form massive, intergrown aggregates detrimental to diffusion kinetics. Additionally, synthesizing high-silica ANA (Si/Al > 4) is difficult without organic templates, as high alkalinity promotes the formation of dense, silica-poor phases. Future directions are concentrating on the "reverse" transformation of ANA into open frameworks, such as CHA or MFI, utilizing recyclable organic SDAs to valorize natural or waste zeolite resources, thereby closing the loop in zeolite lifecycle management.

**Introduction and Background: Molecular Simulation of Zeolite Adsorption**

Molecular simulation has emerged as an indispensable tool in the rational design and optimization of zeolites for adsorption-based processes. Zeolites, microporous crystalline aluminosilicates with well-defined pore architectures at the molecular scale (typically 0.3–2.0 nm), are among the most critical industrial adsorbents and catalysts. Their utility stems from properties such as high internal surface area (often exceeding 500 m²/g), uniform pore dimensions, tunable acidity via the Si/Al ratio, and exceptional thermal and hydrothermal stability. The precise prediction of molecular adsorption equilibria and kinetics within these confined environments is paramount for advancing separation and catalytic technologies.

The historical development of this field is closely tied to advances in both computational power and theoretical frameworks. Early adsorption models, such as the Langmuir and Dubinin-Polanyi theories, treated zeolites as homogeneous substrates, failing to capture the critical effects of pore geometry and specific guest-host interactions. The advent of Monte Carlo (MC) methods, particularly Grand Canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC) for simulating adsorption equilibria at a fixed chemical potential, and Molecular Dynamics (MD) for probing diffusional kinetics, revolutionized the field in the 1980s and 1990s. These atomistic simulations, coupled with accurate force fields (e.g., the TraPPE force field for hydrocarbons, the ClayFF or INTERFACE force fields for zeolite frameworks), allow for the *in silico* screening of adsorption isotherms, heats of adsorption, and diffusion coefficients with remarkable fidelity to experimental measurements.

The industrial relevance of these simulations is immense, driven by the scale of zeolite applications. The global zeolite market was valued at approximately **USD 33.5 billion in 2022**, with adsorbents and catalysts representing the largest segments. Key applications with stringent adsorption requirements include:
1.  **Air Separation:** The production of oxygen and nitrogen via Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA) or Vacuum Swing Adsorption (VSA) processes relies on zeolites like Li-LSX (Low Silica X, Si/Al ≈ 1.0) for N₂ adsorption via quadrupole interaction. These units require adsorbents with high N₂/O₂ selectivity (>5) and working capacity, producing over **100 million tons** of industrial gases annually.
2.  **Hydrocarbon Separations:** The separation of linear from branched or cyclic paraffins using 5A zeolite (Ca²⁺-exchanged A-type, pore aperture 0.5 nm) is a foundational petrochemical process for enhancing octane number in gasoline. Simulating the subtle differences in alkane-zeolite van der Waals interactions is crucial for optimizing performance.
3.  **Drying and CO₂ Capture:** 3A zeolite (K⁺-exchanged A-type, pore aperture 0.3 nm) is universally employed for deep drying of cracked gas streams in ethylene plants, requiring near-total exclusion of larger molecules. For post-combustion CO₂ capture, zeolites like 13X (Na⁺-exchanged X-type) are leading candidates, necessitating high CO₂ capacity (~2–3 mmol/g at 0.1 bar CO₂, 25°C) and stability in humid flue gas.
4.  **Nuclear Waste Remediation:** Cesium and strontium sequestration from high-level waste streams using titanosilicates (e.g., CST) or engineered phases like **IONIV™** requires simulations that accurately model ion-exchange in extreme chemical and radiation environments.

Despite their power, key scientific challenges persist in molecular simulation of zeolite adsorption. These include: (1) the accurate description of **framework flexibility and cation mobility**, particularly for low-silica zeolites where extra-framework cations (Na⁺, Ca²⁺, Li⁺) can migrate upon adsorption, significantly altering the potential energy landscape; (2) modeling **co-adsorption and competitive adsorption** in complex, multi-component process streams (e.g., humid CO₂/N₂ mixtures); and (3) bridging the vast **timescale gap** between MD simulations (typically nanoseconds to microseconds) and real-world adsorption/desorption cycle times (seconds to minutes). Furthermore, simulating reactive adsorption or the initial steps of catalysis remains a formidable challenge.

The primary motivation for ongoing research is to transition from a heuristic, trial-and-error-based material selection to a **predictive, physics-driven design paradigm**. By integrating high-throughput GCMC and MD screening with machine learning algorithms, researchers aim to identify novel zeolitic or zeolite-like frameworks tailored for next-generation separations, such as the energy-efficient separation of xenon/krypton, olefin/paraffin splits (e.g., C₂H₄/C₂H₆), or the removal of trace contaminants. # Post-Synthetic Modification of Zeolites: A Literature Review

## Introduction

Post-synthetic modification (PSM) has emerged as a powerful strategy to tailor zeolite properties beyond conventional synthesis, enabling precise control over acidity, porosity, and thermal stability. These modifications address inherent limitations of direct synthesis, such as restricted Si/Al ratios and inaccessible active sites, while opening new avenues for advanced applications in catalysis, adsorption, and separation processes.

## Dealumination Strategies

Acid leaching with mineral acids (HCl, HNO₃) represents the most established dealumination method, effectively removing framework aluminum to create silanol nests. Studies on MFI zeolites (Si/Al = 15-40) demonstrate that controlled HCl treatment (0.1-2.0 M, 333-363 K, 2-8 h) increases Si/Al ratios to 200-500 while preserving crystallinity. However, excessive dealumination leads to framework collapse, with critical stability thresholds observed at Si/Al > 300 for MFI.

Hydrothermal steaming (873-1123 K, 0.1-1.0 MPa, 1-10 h) induces more extensive dealumination through demetallation and subsequent vacancy formation. This approach has been extensively characterized for commercial catalysts, including USY (Ultra-Stable Y, FAU framework) where steaming at 1033 K for 3-5 hours produces Si/Al ratios of 15-80 while generating mesoporosity. Steaming also creates bridging OH groups (Si-OH-Al) with modified acidity, though excessive treatment leads to dealuminated debris affecting catalytic performance.

## Desilication and Combined Approaches

Alkaline treatment (NaOH, 0.1-1.0 M, 338-358 K) preferentially removes silicon atoms, creating mesopores (2-10 nm) within microporous frameworks. Systematic studies on MFI zeolites reveal optimal conditions: 0.2 M NaOH at 338 K for 30-60 minutes generates hierarchical porosity with surface areas maintained above 350 m²/g. Desilication has been extended to BEA (β-zeolite) and CHA (SAPO-34) frameworks, though framework stability varies significantly—CHA shows greater susceptibility to structural degradation under identical conditions.

Combined dealumination-desilication (sequential acid-base treatment) enables fine control over Si/Al ratios and pore architecture. Research demonstrates that MFI treated first with 0.2 M HCl (333 K, 4 h) followed by 0.2 M NaOH (338 K, 30 min) achieves Si/Al = 120-180 with bimodal micro-meso porosity, showing enhanced diffusion for bulky molecule conversion in methanol-to-olefins catalysis.

## Cation Exchange and Metal Insertion

Ion exchange with multivalent cations (La³⁺, Ce³⁺, Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺) modifies acidic and redox properties. Studies on H-BEA zeolites report complete exchange with Cu²⁺ (0.01 M Cu(NO₃)₂, 298 K, 24 h) yielding 1.5-2.0 wt% Cu loading, with isolated Cu²⁺ sites active for NH₃-SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction) of NOₓ below 573 K. Frameworks with larger pore openings (FAU, BEA) show higher exchange capacities due to enhanced diffusion.

Isomorphous substitution via liquid-phase grafting introduces heteroatoms (Ti, Sn, Zr, V) into dealuminated frameworks. Ti insertion into dealuminated MFI using Ti(OBu)₄ in anhydrous solvents (298-333 K) produces isolated Ti(IV) sites active for selective oxidation, with turnover frequencies of 150-200 h⁻¹ for cyclohexene epoxidation using tert-butyl hydro

# Comparative Analysis of Metal-Organic Frameworks and Zeolites: Structural Features, Synthesis Parameters, and Application Domains

## Abstract

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and zeolites represent the two principal classes of crystalline microporous materials extensively employed in adsorption, catalysis, and separation processes. Despite sharing architectural similarities in their porous architectures, these material classes exhibit fundamentally distinct chemical compositions, synthetic pathways, and performance characteristics. This contribution provides a critical comparison of these material families, examining their structural attributes, synthesis conditions, and application-relevant properties.

## Structural Characteristics and Compositional Diversity

Zeolites constitute aluminosilicate materials characterized by corner-sharing TO₄ tetrahedra (where T = Si or Al), forming three-dimensional framework structures with well-defined pore apertures typically ranging from 0.3 to 1.2 nm. The framework bears a net negative charge compensated by extra-framework cations (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, or H⁺), as exemplified in faujasite-type zeolite Y (Si/Al ratio 1.5–150) and pentasil zeolite ZSM-5 (MFI framework, ten-membered ring channels).

Metal-organic frameworks comprise metal clusters or ions interconnected by organic linkers, yielding exceptional surface areas and structural diversity. MOF-5 (Zn₄O(BDC)₃, where BDC = 1,4-benzenedicarboxylate) demonstrates a Langmuir surface area of approximately 2,900 m² g⁻¹, while MIL-101(Cr) achieves values exceeding 5,900 m² g⁻¹. Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs), particularly ZIF-8 with sodalite topology, exhibitzeolite-like apertures of 0.34 nm despite their organic composition. The UiO-66 family (Zr₆O₄(OH)₄(BDC)₆) demonstrates exceptional thermal stability up to 540°C in Ar atmosphere, approaching conventional zeolite stability parameters.

## Synthesis Methodologies and Processing Conditions

Conventional zeolite synthesis typically employs hydrothermal crystallization at temperatures between 80°C and 200°C for durations ranging from 6 hours to 14 days under autogenous pressure. The classical zeolite Y preparation utilizes a gel composition of Na₂O:Al₂O₃:SiO₂:H₂O in ratios approximating 14:1:10:1500, with crystallization occurring at 100°C for 24–72 hours. The presence of organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs) such as tetraethylammonium hydroxide or crown ethers has enabled synthesis of extra-large pore zeolites and complex framework types.

MOF synthesis encompasses solvothermal methods predominantly, wherein metal precursors (typically nitrates, chlorides, or acetates) react with organic linkers in polar solvents at temperatures from 85°C to 220°C for 12–72 hours. HKUST-1 (Cu₃(BTC)₂, where BTC = 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylate) synthesis proceeds in a water-ethanol mixture at 120°C for 18 hours. Modulated synthesis approaches, employing monocarboxylic acids as modulators, have proven effective for controlling crystal morphology and size distribution. Post-synthetic modification strategies, including linker exchange and metal node functionalization, provide additional synthetic flexibility absent in zeolite chemistry.

## Comparative Performance Assessment

The water and thermal stability hierarchy reveals that conventional zeolites generally outperform MOFs under demanding conditions. Zeolite Y maintains structural integrity in steam at 800°C, while most MOFs undergo degradation below 400°C in humid atmospheres. Exceptions include the hydrolytically stable Zr-based UiO-66 and MIL-140 series, as demonstrated by

Zeolites, crystalline microporous aluminosilicates with regular pore systems, constitute the backbone of modern heterogeneous catalysis, particularly in the petrochemical and refining sectors. Their industrial dominance stems from their exceptional shape selectivity, strong Brønsted acidity, and high ion-exchange capacity. However, the practical utility of zeolitic materials is frequently limited by their susceptibility to structural degradation under hydrothermal conditions. Steam stability—the ability of the zeolite framework to retain crystallinity, porosity, and acidic functionality after exposure to high-temperature water vapor—is a critical determinant of catalyst lifetime and economic viability.

The historical imperative for steam-stable catalysts emerged prominently in the 1960s with the introduction of zeolite Y in Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC). While zeolite X offered superior activity, its rapid dealumination in the regenerator environment rendered it commercially unviable. This spurred the development of Ultra-Stable Y (USY), produced via post-synthetic steam calcination and acid leaching to remove framework aluminum, thereby creating a silica-rich, hydrothermally robust lattice. Today, the global market for zeolite catalysts exceeds USD 4.5 billion, with FCC catalysts alone accounting for nearly 40% of this volume. A modern FCC unit processes upwards of 50,000 barrels per stream day, where catalyst turnover costs are substantial; therefore, enhancing steam stability directly correlates with significant operational savings.

The primary scientific challenge governing steam stability is the hydrolysis of the tetrahedral T-O-T bonds (specifically Si-OH-Al) under high-temperature steam. At temperatures exceeding $600^\circ\text{C}$, water molecules polarize the Si-O-Al bond, leading to the ejection of aluminum from the framework as extra-framework aluminum (EFAL). This dealumination results in unit cell contraction, the irreversible loss of Brønsted acid sites, and the eventual collapse of the microporous architecture into amorphous silica-alumina. This degradation necessitates rigorous research into framework stabilization, often through increasing the silica-to-alumina ratio (SAR), incorporating heteroatoms (e.g., phosphorus, lanthanum), or employing post-synthetic surface passivation.

Industrial applications demand rigorous hydrothermal endurance. In FCC, the equilibrium catalyst is continuously cycled between the reactor ($\sim 500^\circ\text{C}$) and the regenerator ($\sim 700-750^\circ\text{C}$) in the presence of $1-2\%$ steam. Similarly, in the selective catalytic reduction (SCR) of $\text{NO}_x$ from diesel exhaust, Cu-exchanged Chabazite (Cu-SSZ-13) must withstand hydrothermal aging at $700^\circ\text{C}$ to $800^\circ\text{C}$ in $10\%$ steam for over 24 hours to meet federal durability mandates. The motivation for ongoing research is thus twofold: to mitigate the economic burden of frequent catalyst replacement and to enable emerging processes, such as Methanol-to-Olefins (MTO), where the regeneration of SAPO-34 catalysts exposes the material to even more severe hydrothermal stress. Understanding the atomistic mechanisms of steam-induced degradation is essential for designing the next generation of resilient, high-performance molecular sieves.

### Geopolymer-to-Zeolite Conversion: An Introduction and Background

The synthesis of zeolites, microporous crystalline aluminosilicates with well-defined pore architectures, is a cornerstone of modern materials science due to their pivotal roles in catalysis, ion-exchange, and gas separation. Traditional hydrothermal synthesis, typically conducted at elevated temperatures (80–200 °C) under autogenous pressure over periods of hours to days, remains the dominant production method for frameworks such as FAU (zeolites X, Y), LTA (zeolite A), and MFI (ZSM-5). However, this conventional route is often energy-intensive, requires significant liquid water usage, and can generate alkaline wastewater. In parallel, the last four decades have seen the emergence of geopolymers—amorphous to semi-crystalline, three-dimensional aluminosilicate networks formed via the alkaline activation of solid aluminosilicate precursors (e.g., metakaolin, fly ash, blast furnace slag) at near-ambient temperatures. Geopolymers are celebrated as low-CO₂ construction materials but possess a disordered, meso- to macroporous structure with limited molecular sieving capability.

The strategic conversion of geopolymers into zeolites represents a convergent research frontier, aiming to synergize the scalable, low-energy fabrication of geopolymer monoliths with the superior functionality of crystalline zeolites. This process, often termed "zeolitization" or "hydrothermal conversion," involves subjecting a hardened geopolymer to a post-synthesis hydrothermal treatment, typically in a mother liquor or steam environment, which drives the dissolution of the amorphous gel and the subsequent nucleation and growth of zeolitic phases. Historically, the observation that aged alkaline-activated aluminosilicates could develop crystallinity dates to early studies on geological analogues and waste stabilization. However, directed research into controlled conversion for advanced materials began in earnest in the early 2000s, spurred by the dual needs of valorizing industrial by-products (like coal fly ash) and creating novel hierarchical porous solids.

The industrial relevance is substantial. The global zeolite market was valued at approximately USD 33.5 billion in 2022, with synthetic zeolites holding a dominant share for detergent builders, fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) catalysts, and adsorbents. The geopolymer-to-zeolite pathway offers a potential route to lower-cost, shaped catalyst supports or adsorbent monoliths, bypassing the need for binders and complex forming processes. For instance, converting fly-ash-based geopolymers into zeolite phases like Na-P1 (GIS type) or analcime (ANA type) can produce structured adsorbents for heavy metal (e.g., Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺) wastewater remediation, where requirements include mechanical stability for fixed-bed operation, ion-exchange capacities >2 meq/g, and attrition resistance. In catalysis, converting metakaolin geopolymers into chabazite (CHA type) or ZSM-5 composites presents a route to structured catalysts for selective catalytic reduction (SCR) of NOₓ or methanol-to-olefins (MTO) processes, where criteria encompass high crystallinity (>80%), controlled acidity (0.2–1.0 mmol NH₃/g), and preservation of macroporous diffusion pathways.

Key scientific challenges in this conversion process are multifaceted. The first is **phase selectivity and purity**: the geopolymer's composition (Si/Al ratio, cation type, presence of impurities) and the hydrothermal conditions (temperature, time, alkalinity) must be precisely controlled to avoid intergrown mixtures of non-target phases. For example, a low-Si/Al geopolymer may favor LTA or SOD types, while higher ratios are needed for MFI or MOR. The second is **morphological and hierarchical control**: the process must retain the macroscopic shape and desirable mesoporosity of the geopolymer precursor while developing microporous zeolitic crystals. Uncontrolled crystallization often leads to dense, intergrown films that block mass transport. The third challenge is **kinetic and mechanistic understanding**: the dissolution-reprecipitation process is complex, involving simultaneous reactions at the solid-liquid interface, with nucleation often heterogeneous. Parameters such as the molarity of the activating solution (e.g., 6–10 M NaOH), curing temperature of the geopolymer (20–80 °C), and hydrothermal treatment duration (6–72 h) are critical and interlinked variables.

The motivation for ongoing research is driven by the promise of a sustainable, one-pot fabrication strategy for hierarchical zeolitic materials. By embedding zeolitic functionality into a geopolymer-derived monolith, researchers aim to create advanced, multi-scale porous materials for applications requiring both high intrinsic activity (micropores) and efficient diffusion (macro-/mesopores), all while potentially utilizing low-cost, waste-derived feedstocks and reducing process energy inputs by up to 30-40% compared to conventional zeolite synthesis. # Synthesis of TS-1 Titanium Silicalite

## Reagents and Equipment

The following reagents of analytical grade were employed: tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98 wt%), titanium butoxide (Ti(OBu)₄, 97 wt%), tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH, 40 wt% aqueous solution), hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂, 30 wt%), and deionized water. A 100 mL stainless steel autoclave lined with PTFE was used as the crystallization vessel. Polypropylene containers (250 mL capacity) were employed for gel preparation, and a programmable muffle furnace was required for calcination.

## Procedure

### Gel Preparation

A clear solution was first prepared by combining 36.6 g of TPAOH (40 wt%) and 18.0 g of deionized water in a polypropylene container. The mixture was magnetically stirred at 300 rpm while the temperature was maintained at 25°C using a water bath. Subsequently, 22.8 g of TEOS was slowly added dropwise over a period of 30 minutes. The resulting solution was stirred for an additional 2 hours at 25°C to allow complete hydrolysis of TEOS. A separate solution was prepared by dissolving 0.38 g of Ti(OBu)₄ in 5.0 g of ethanol under nitrogen atmosphere. This titanium source was added to the silica-containing solution, and the combined mixture was stirred for 1 hour at 25°C. The final gel composition, expressed as molar ratios, was SiO₂ : 0.015 TiO₂ : 0.25 TPAOH : 25 H₂O : 0.4 EtOH.

### Crystallization

The gel was transferred into a PTFE-lined autoclave (100 mL capacity). The autoclave was sealed and placed in a convection oven. Crystallization was carried out under static conditions at 175°C for 72 hours. The autogenous pressure generated was approximately 12–15 bar. After crystallization was completed, the autoclave was cooled naturally to 25°C over a period of 4 hours.

### Washing and Drying

The solid product was recovered by vacuum filtration and washed repeatedly with deionized water until the filtrate reached pH 7–8. Washing was continued until no residual alkaline species were detected by qualitative analysis. The wet cake was then dried at 100°C in a convection oven for 12 hours to remove physically adsorbed water.

### Calcination

The dried sample was placed in a ceramic crucible and transferred to a muffle furnace. Calcination was performed in air flow (100 mL/min) using the following temperature program: ramp from 25°C to 120°C at 2°C/min and hold for 2 hours; ramp from 120°C to 550°C at 1°C/min and hold for 6 hours; finally, ramp from 550°C to 600°C at 1°C/min and hold for 1 hour. The sample was subsequently cooled to 25°C at 3°C/min. The total calcination duration was approximately 24 hours.

## Yield and Characterization Results

The typical product yield was 8.5–9.5 g of calcined TS-1 per 100 g of initial gel (SiO₂ basis). The product was obtained as a white crystalline powder with particle size ranging from 0.3 to 1.5 μm as determined by scanning electron microscopy. The BET surface area, measured by nitrogen adsorption at 77 K, was 380–420 m²/g. The titanium content, as determined by ICP-OES, was 0.8–1.2 wt% (TiO

# Delamination of UTL-Type Zeolite: A Detailed Synthesis Procedure

## Background

The UTL-type zeolite (IZA code: UTL) possesses a unique 3-dimensional framework featuring a dual-pore system with intersecting 12-membered ring (12MR) channels. The interlayer connectivity in UTL contains strained Si-O-Ge linkages that are susceptible to hydrolysis under mild acidic conditions, enabling controlled delamination via the ADOR (Assembly-Disassembly-Organization-Reassembly) approach. This procedure describes the synthesis of the parent UTL zeolite followed by its top-down delamination to produce 2D zeolite nanosheets with high surface area and exposed active sites.

---

## Part I: Synthesis of Parent UTL-Type Zeolite

### Reagents and Materials

| Component | Amount | Source/Notes |
|-----------|--------|--------------|
| Colloidal silica (Ludox AS-40, 40 wt%) | 6.20 g | Silicon source |
| Germanium dioxide (GeO₂) | 2.15 g | Germanium source |
| 1,3-bis(1-Adamantyl)imidazolium hydroxide (20 wt% aqueous) | 12.80 g | Organic structure-directing agent (OSDA) |
| Distilled water | 8.50 mL | Reaction medium |
| Hydrofluoric acid (48 wt%) | 1.65 g | Mineralizing agent |

### Synthesis Protocol

**Step 1: Gel Preparation**

The colloidal silica was weighed and transferred into a 100 mL polypropylene beaker. Germanium dioxide was added gradually to the silica suspension under continuous magnetic stirring at 400 rpm. The mixture was stirred for 15 minutes at ambient temperature (22 ± 2°C) until a homogeneous white suspension was obtained. The OSDA solution was added dropwise over 5 minutes while stirring was maintained. Distilled water was incorporated to adjust the final Si/Ge ratio to approximately 3.0.

**Step 2: Acid Addition and Gel Homogenization**

Hydrofluoric acid was carefully introduced using a polypropylene-coated magnetic stir bar at 200 rpm. The addition was performed in a fume hood with appropriate safety precautions. The mixture was stirred for 30 minutes until a thick, homogeneous gel formed. The final gel composition, expressed as molar ratios, was: SiO₂: 0.33 GeO₂: 0.25 OSDA: 15 H₂O: 0.50 HF.

**Step 3: Crystallization**

The clear gel was transferred into a 45 mL PTFE-lined stainless steel autoclave (Parr Instruments, Model 4749). The autoclave was sealed and placed in a convection oven preheated to 150°C. Crystallization was conducted under autogenous pressure with static conditions for 7 days (168 hours).

**Step 4: Product Isolation**

**Experimental Results and Discussion**

The successful synthesis of TNU-9 and TNU-10 zeolites was verified through comprehensive physicochemical characterization. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the calcined samples exhibited high crystallinity with sharp, well-defined peaks, indicating the effective removal of the organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs). For TNU-9, the diffraction profile matched the standard reference for the TUN framework, displaying distinctive reflections at $2\theta$ values of 7.9°, 8.8°, 20.6°, and 23.8° (Cu K$\alpha$ radiation, $\lambda = 1.5418$ Å). The absence of extraneous peaks in the 20–25° region confirmed the phase purity of the material. In comparison, the XRD pattern of TNU-10 corresponded to the high-silica form of stilbite (STI topology), characterized by intense peaks at 9.5°, 20.0°, and 22.6°. These experimental peak positions align precisely with literature data reported by Hong et al., validating the successful crystallization of the target phases under the applied hydrothermal conditions ($150\ ^\circ\mathrm{C}$, 144 h).

Textural properties were evaluated using N$_2$ physisorption at 77 K. Both materials displayed Type I isotherms, characteristic of microporous solids. TNU-9 exhibited a Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area of $485\ \mathrm{m^2/g}$ and a total pore volume of $0.19\ \mathrm{cm^3/g}$. These values are consistent with the theoretical surface area of the TUN framework, which features a complex, three-dimensional intersecting 10-ring channel system. TNU-10 demonstrated a slightly lower BET surface area of $425\ \mathrm{m^2/g}$ and a pore volume of $0.16\ \mathrm{cm^3/g}$. This reduction in porosity relative to TNU-9 is attributable to the STI topology, which consists of intersecting 8- and 10-ring channels; the presence of 8-ring apertures reduces the overall accessible volume and imposes steric constraints on N$_2$ adsorption at the micropore level compared to the uniform 10-ring pores of TNU-9.

Acid site concentrations were quantified using ammonia temperature-programmed desorption (NH$_3$-TPD). The TNU-9 sample showed a total acid site density of $0.52\ \mathrm{mmol/g}$, comprising $0.31\ \mathrm{mmol/g}$ of weak sites (desorption at $150\text{--}250\ ^\circ\mathrm{C}$) and $0.21\ \mathrm{mmol/g}$ of strong sites (desorption $>350\ ^\circ\mathrm{C}$). TNU-10 possessed a total acidity of $0.41\ \mathrm{mmol/g}$ ($0.25\ \mathrm{mmol/g}$ weak, $0.16\ \mathrm{mmol/g}$ strong). The higher acidity observed in TNU-9 is consistent with its slightly lower framework Si/Al ratio (approx. 15 vs. 20 for TNU-10) and the unique framework Al siting within the larger TUN unit cell. These results fall within the expected ranges reported in the literature for high-silica zeolites synthesized via similar OSDA-directed routes.

The structure-property relationship analysis underscores the influence of pore architecture on material performance. The superior surface area and higher acid site concentration of TNU-9, coupled with its uniform 3D 10-ring channel system, suggest enhanced diffusion characteristics and catalytic potential for bulky hydrocarbon transformations. In contrast, while TNU-10 possesses respectable acidity, its mixed 8- and 10-ring channel system likely restricts mass transport of larger substrates compared to the TUN topology. Consequently, TNU-9 is anticipated to exhibit higher activity in acid-catalyzed reactions involving sterically demanding molecules, whereas TNU-10 may demonstrate higher shape selectivity for smaller reactants.

**Sol-Gel Synthesis of Zeolite NaA (LTA Type) Precursors**

**Introduction**
The sol-gel synthesis of zeolite precursors involves the controlled hydrolysis and condensation of silica and alumina sources in an alkaline medium to form a homogeneous aluminosilicate hydrogel, which is subsequently crystallized. This procedure details the synthesis of Zeolite NaA (Linde Type A, LTA framework), a common model system.

**Materials and Experimental Parameters**
*   **Silica Source:** Sodium silicate solution (Na₂SiO₃, 27 wt% SiO₂, 8 wt% Na₂O, density ~1.39 g/mL).
*   **Alumina Source:** Sodium aluminate (NaAlO₂, technical grade, 50-56 wt% Al₂O₃, 40-45 wt% Na₂O).
*   **Structure-Directing Agent (SDA)/Mineralizer:** Sodium hydroxide pellets (NaOH, ≥98%).
*   **Solvent:** Deionized (DI) water.
*   **Equipment:** Polypropylene or Teflon beakers (500 mL and 1 L), mechanical overhead stirrer with Teflon paddle, polypropylene crystallization vessels (or Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclaves), thermostat-controlled oil bath or oven, vacuum filtration setup, drying oven, muffle furnace.

**Synthesis Procedure**

**1. Aluminate Solution Preparation (Solution A):**
In a 500 mL polypropylene beaker, 2.50 g of sodium hydroxide pellets were dissolved in 50.0 mL of DI water under moderate magnetic stirring (300 rpm) for 15 minutes until a clear solution was obtained. To this, 4.10 g of sodium aluminate were added gradually. The mixture was stirred vigorously (500 rpm) at ambient temperature (25 ± 2 °C) for 60 minutes to ensure complete dissolution, forming a clear aluminate solution.

**2. Silicate Solution Preparation (Solution B):**
In a separate 1 L polypropylene beaker, 3.30 g of sodium hydroxide pellets were dissolved in 80.0 mL of DI water. Subsequently, 16.70 mL of sodium silicate solution (equivalent to 6.50 g of SiO₂) were added dropwise over 10 minutes under continuous stirring (400 rpm). The mixture was stirred for an additional 30 minutes to homogenize.

**3. Gel Formation and Aging:**
Solution A (aluminate) was added dropwise (approx. 2 mL/min) into Solution B (silicate) under vigorous mechanical stirring (700 rpm). Immediately upon mixing, a thick, white aluminosilicate gel formed. The final molar composition of the gel was 2.0 Na₂O : 1.0 Al₂O₃ : 2.0 SiO₂ : 120 H₂O. Stirring was continued for 90 minutes at ambient temperature to ensure homogeneity. The gel was then transferred to a sealed polypropylene crystallization vessel and subjected to a static aging period of 24 hours at 40 °C in a thermostatted oven.

**4. Crystallization:**
The aged gel was transferred into a series of 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclaves, filling each to 70% capacity. The autoclaves were sealed and placed in a forced-air convection oven for hydrothermal crystallization at 90 ± 2 °C for 4 hours. The crystallization progress can be monitored by the gradual transformation of the opaque gel into a settled white crystalline powder.

**5. Product Recovery:**
After crystallization, the autoclaves were quenched in cold water to 30 °C. The solid product was separated from the mother liquor by vacuum filtration using a Büchner funnel with a 0.45 μm cellulose nitrate membrane. The filter cake was washed repeatedly with DI water (approximately 2 L total per batch) until the filtrate pH reached 9-10.

**6. Drying and Calcination:**
The washed product was transferred to a ceramic crucible and dried in a static air oven at 100 °C for 12 hours. The dried zeolite powder (typically containing occluded water and any residual organics, though minimal for NaA) was then calcined in a muffle furnace. The temperature was ramped at 2 °C/min to 550 °C, held at this temperature for 6 hours to remove any volatile species, and then cooled to room temperature at 1 °C/min.

**Yield and Characterization**
The typical yield of calcined Zeolite NaA from this procedure is 5.2 ± 0.3 g (∼85% yield based on SiO₂ input). Characterization via X-ray diffraction (XRD) reveals a pure LTA phase with high crystallinity, exhibiting characteristic peaks at 2θ = 7.2°, 10.2°,

# Introduction and Background

## Historical Development of LTA-Type Zeolites

Zeolite A, designated as LTA (Linde Type A) according to the framework type code established by the International Zeolite Association, represents one of the most significant synthetic zeolites in industrial applications. The first successful synthesis of zeolite A was achieved by Breck and co-workers at Union Carbide Corporation in the mid-1950s, marking a pivotal advancement in synthetic zeolite technology. This landmark development demonstrated that aluminosilicate zeolites could be systematically synthesized under controlled hydrothermal conditions, paving the way for widespread industrial implementation. The original synthesis procedure, documented in subsequent patents and publications, established the fundamental framework composition and synthesis methodology that remains the basis for contemporary production processes. The discovery of zeolite A preceded the systematic classification of zeolite frameworks and contributed substantially to the fundamental understanding of zeolite crystallization mechanisms.

## Industrial Significance and Market Context

The LTA-type zeolite has achieved unparalleled industrial prominence, representing the highest volume production among all synthetic zeolite varieties globally. Current estimates place the annual production of zeolite A at several hundred thousand metric tons, with the global zeolite market exceeding USD 7 billion and projected growth rates of 5-7% annually. This industrial dominance stems primarily from two major application domains: detergent builders and molecular sieve adsorbents. The detergent industry alone consumes approximately 60-70% of globally produced zeolite A, serving as a phosphate-freeBuilder to mitigate eutrophication concerns associated with traditional sodium tripolyphosphate formulations. Major producers include multinational corporations such as BASF, Clariant, and PQ Corporation, operating production facilities across North America, Europe, and Asia. The substantial economic scale of zeolite A production reflects its essential role in contemporary consumer and industrial products.

## Scientific Challenges in Synthesis

The synthesis of LTA-type zeolite presents several fundamental scientific challenges that continue to motivate research in this field. The crystallization process involves complex nucleation and growth mechanisms influenced by multiple parameters including gel composition, temperature, pH, aging conditions, and reactant source materials. Control of particle size distribution remains particularly challenging, as applications ranging from detergent formulations to advanced adsorbents require specific particle morphologies typically between 1-10 micrometers. The dependence of crystal size on synthesis conditions, including the critical role of seed addition and gel aging, necessitates precise optimization to achieve reproducible product quality. Furthermore, the synthesis of phase-pure LTA zeolite without contamination by competing phases such as sodalite or hydroxy sodalite requires careful control of the Si

## Results and Discussion

### Sample Characterization and Mineralogical Composition

X-ray diffraction analysis of the natural zeolite deposits revealed the predominant presence of clinoptilolite (heulandite-type framework) as the primary crystalline phase, with minor quantities of mordenite and quartz as accessory minerals. The characteristic diffraction peaks for the clinoptilolite phase were identified at 2θ values of 9.8°, 17.4°, 22.5°, 28.2°, and 30.6°, which correspond to the (020), (200), (150), (132), and (−402) lattice planes, respectively. These values align closely with the standard diffraction pattern for heulandite-type zeolites (JCPDS Card No. 38-0243). The mordenite contamination was evidenced by shoulders at 2θ values of 6.5° and 25.8°, while the quartz impurity manifested as a sharp peak at 2θ = 26.6°. Semi-quantitative Rietveld analysis indicated a mineralogical composition of approximately 78 wt% clinoptilolite, 14 wt% mordenite, and 8 wt% quartz.

### Textural Properties

Nitrogen physisorption measurements at 77 K yielded a Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of 342 m²/g for the as-received natural zeolite, with a Langmuir surface area of 487 m²/g. The total pore volume, determined at a relative pressure (P/P₀) of 0.95, was measured at 0.28 cm³/g. The micropore volume, calculated using the t-plot method, accounted for 0.18 cm³/g, representing approximately 64% of the total porosity. The Horvath-Kawazoe analysis indicated a bimodal pore size distribution with maxima centered at 0.52 nm and 0.78 nm, corresponding to the characteristic channel dimensions of clinoptilolite (7.5 × 7.5 Å) and mordenite (6.5 × 7.0 Å), respectively.

These textural parameters are consistent with values reported in the literature for natural clinoptilolite-rich tuffs. Vergara et al. (2019) reported BET surface areas ranging from 280–410 m²/g for untreated natural clinoptilolite, while Ming and Dixon (1987) documented pore volumes between 0.15–0.35 cm³/g for natural zeolites of similar geological origin. The observed values fall within the upper range of reported measurements, suggesting favorable retention of the intrinsic microporous structure during the sample processing and dehydration steps.

### Acid Site Characterization

Temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia (NH₃-TPD) measurements revealed moderate acid site concentrations typical of natural aluminosilicate zeolites. The total acid site density was quantified at 0.72 mmol/g, distributed between weak acid sites (0.41

The Dry Gel Conversion (DGC) method, bifurcated into Steam-Assisted Conversion (SAC) and Vapor-Phase Transport (VPT), has emerged as a sophisticated alternative to conventional hydrothermal synthesis for zeolite manufacturing. By isolating the dry aluminosilicate precursor gel from the bulk liquid phase, DGC utilizes a vapor phase—typically water or a mixture of water and volatile organic structure-directing agents (SDAs)—to mediate crystallization. This approach significantly reduces wastewater generation and offers unique control over crystal morphology and phase purity.

Over the past decade, research has focused on expanding DGC to complex, high-silica frameworks and optimizing interzeolite transformations. A significant advance involves the synthesis of **MFI** (ZSM-5) and **CHA** (SSZ-13) frameworks via the SAC route. For instance, using tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH) impregnated into a dry silica source, researchers have achieved nanocrystalline MFI at 150–180°C, exhibiting enhanced mesoporosity compared to hydrothermally derived counterparts. In the realm of **CHA**, critical for DeNOx applications, DGC using N,N,N-trimethyl-1-adamantammonium hydroxide (TMAdaOH) has enabled the production of zeolites with high Si/Al ratios (>50) and superior hydrothermal stability. The vapor-phase transport of TMAda+ allows for efficient SDA utilization, where the volatile amine diffuses into the gel, directing the assembly of the chabazite cage while minimizing organic waste.

Furthermore, the "dry gel interzeolite conversion" has gained traction. Recent studies demonstrate the conversion of **FAU** zeolite into **BEA** or **MFI**. In these protocols, a FAU-derived dry gel is exposed to steam containing tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH) or TPAOH at 175°C. The mechanism involves the dissolution of FAU building units (e.g., double 6-rings) and their reorganization into the target framework. This method often yields hierarchical structures beneficial for diffusion-limited catalysis. Comparatively, SAC (where the SDA is pre-mixed in the dry gel) generally yields faster crystallization kinetics than VPT (where SDA migrates from the liquid reservoir), as the SDA is already in proximity to the inorganic framework.

However, challenges persist in scaling DGC for industrial application. The heat and mass transfer within the autoclave can be heterogeneous, leading to uneven crystallization in large batches. Additionally, the precise control of steam pressure is critical; insufficient vapor leads to incomplete crystallization, while excess steam can cause condensation, reverting the system to hydrothermal conditions. Future directions are pivoting toward "green" DGC, emphasizing solvent-free extrusion of dry gels and the recycling of volatile SDAs. Moreover, the application of DGC to the synthesis of zeolite membranes and thin films remains a promising frontier, leveraging the method’s ability to form continuous, intergrown layers on porous supports without excess slurry.

**Ion Exchange Procedure for Preparation of Potassium-Exchanged Zeolite A from Sodium Zeolite A (LTA Framework)**

**Introduction**
Ion exchange in zeolites is a fundamental post-synthetic modification used to alter cation composition, thereby tuning properties such as adsorption capacity, catalytic activity, and stability. This procedure details the aqueous-phase exchange of Na⁺ ions in commercially sourced Linde Type A (LTA) zeolite (Na-A, Si/Al ≈ 1.0) with K⁺ ions using potassium chloride (KCl) as the exchange reagent.

**Materials**
*   Zeolite starting material: Sodium Zeolite A (Na₁₂Al₁₂Si₁₂O₈₈•27H₂O), 5.00 g.
*   Exchange solution: 1.0 M aqueous potassium chloride (KCl), 250 mL, prepared by dissolving 18.64 g of KCl (ACS reagent grade, ≥99.0%) in deionized water and diluting to 250 mL in a volumetric flask.
*   Deionized water (resistivity >18 MΩ·cm), 500 mL.
*   Equipment: 500 mL three-neck round-bottom flask, magnetic stirrer with heating plate, reflux condenser, thermometer, Büchner funnel with filter paper (Whatman Grade 1, 90 mm), vacuum filtration apparatus, drying oven, programmable muffle furnace.

**Experimental Procedure**

**1.  Exchange Reaction**
The zeolite (5.00 g) was added to the 500 mL round-bottom flask equipped with a reflux condenser. The 1.0 M KCl solution (250 mL) was introduced into the flask. The slurry was agitated continuously at 400 rpm using a magnetic stir bar and heated to 80 ± 2 °C using a thermostatically controlled oil bath. The exchange was conducted under reflux for 24 hours to ensure equilibration. The use of a large solution volume (50 mL/g zeolite) and a moderate temperature promotes high exchange efficiency by overcoming diffusion limitations within the zeolite micropores.

**2.  Solid-Liquid Separation and Washing**
Upon completion, the slurry was cooled to room temperature over 30 minutes. The solid product was isolated by vacuum filtration using the Büchner funnel. The exchanged zeolite cake was subsequently washed with five aliquots of 100 mL deionized water (room temperature) to remove occluded KCl and displaced NaCl. Washing was considered complete when a 1 mL aliquot of the filtrate showed no precipitate upon addition of 1 M AgNO₃ solution, indicating the absence of chloride ions.

**3.  Drying and Calcination**
The moist filter cake was carefully transferred to a ceramic crucible and dried in a static air oven at 120 °C for 12 hours. The dried powder was then subjected to calcination in a muffle furnace under a static air atmosphere. The temperature was ramped from ambient to 450 °C at a rate of 2 °C/min, held at 450 °C for 6 hours to remove residual water and any organic impurities, and then allowed to cool to room temperature inside the furnace.

**4.  Yield and Handling**
The final product, potassium-exchanged Zeolite A (K-A), was obtained as a fine white powder. A typical yield is 4.6–4.8 g (92–96% mass recovery). The mass loss is attributed to the exchange of heavier Na⁺ (22.99 g/mol) for lighter K⁺ (39.10 g/mol) and the complete dehydration of the framework.

**Characterization and Expected Results**
The success and extent of ion exchange are confirmed through standard characterization techniques.
*   **Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectroscopy (ICP-OES):** Quantitative elemental analysis typically shows a potassium content of 10–11 wt% and a corresponding decrease in sodium content to 1–2 wt%, indicating an exchange level of 80–90% of the original cation exchange capacity (CEC).
*   **X-ray Powder Diffraction (XRD):** The diffraction pattern confirms retention of the LTA crystal structure. # Experimental Results and Discussion: Organic Template Recycling from Zeolite Synthesis

## Template Recovery and Zeolite Characterization

The recovery of organic structure-directing agents (SDAs) from zeolite synthesis represents a critical consideration for both economic viability and environmental sustainability in industrial applications. This section presents characterization data for zeolites synthesized using recoveredtemplates and discusses observed structure-property relationships.

### Powder X-ray Diffraction Analysis

Crystallinity of the synthesized zeolites was confirmed via powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) measurements performed on a PANalytical X'Pert Pro diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). The diffraction patterns for all samples exhibited the characteristic reflections consistent with the expected FAU framework topology. The primary diffraction peaks were observed at 2θ values of approximately 6.3°, 10.2°, 11.9°, 15.7°, 18.8°, 20.4°, 23.7°, 27.1°, and 31.4°, corresponding to the (111), (220), (311), (331), (441), (511), (533), (642), and (751) crystallographic planes, respectively. These 2θ positions are consistent with literature values for zeolite Y (FAU) structure, where the unit cell parameter a ranges from approximately 24.3 to 24.7 Å. No additional peaks attributable to amorphous phases or crystalline impurities were detected, confirming phase purity.

### Textural Properties

Nitrogen physisorption isotherms were measured at 77 K using a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 instrument. Prior to analysis, samples were degassed under vacuum at 573 K for 4 hours to remove adsorbed moisture. The specific surface areas were calculated using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method in the relative pressure range of 0.05-0.20, where linear correlation coefficients exceeded 0.999. The micropore volume and external surface area were determined using the t-plot method.

For zeolite Y samples synthesized with fresh tetramethylammonium (TMA) hydroxide, the BET surface area was measured at 680 m²/g, with a total pore volume of 0.35 cm³/g and micropore volume of 0.32 cm³/g. Samples prepared with recovered template solutions exhibited slightly reduced surface areas in the range of 620-655 m²/g, representing a 4-9% decrease relative to the fresh template control. This reduction is consistent with literature reports where template recycling has been shown to result in minor decreases in textural properties, typically ranging from 5-15% depending on the recovery methodology and number of recycle cycles employed.

### Acid Site Characterization

The concentration and strength of acid sites were determined via temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia (NH3-TPD) using a Micromeritics Autochem II instrument. Samples were pretreated in helium flow at 773 K for 1 hour, followed by ammonia adsorption
