# High-Throughput Screening of Zeolite Synthesis Conditions: Results and Discussion

## Crystallographic Analysis

High-throughput screening of 48 distinct synthesis compositions within the Si/Al molar ratio range of 15–200 yielded crystalline products in 31 reactions. X-ray diffraction analysis confirmed the successful synthesis of MFI-type zeolite (ZSM-5) in 24 reaction vessels, with characteristic diffraction peaks observed at 2θ = 7.96°, 8.88°, 23.18°, 23.98°, and 24.50°, corresponding to the (011), (020), (501), (051), and (303) crystallographic planes, respectively. These reflections match reported literature values within ±0.02° 2θ deviation, confirming phase purity.

The crystallinity, calculated from the integrated peak area at 2θ = 23.18° relative to a reference sample synthesized under optimized conditions, ranged from 67% to 104% across successful reactions. Samples synthesized at Si/Al = 40 and crystallization temperatures of 175°C for 72 hours exhibited maximum crystallinity values exceeding 100%, suggesting enhanced crystal ordering compared to the reference material. Lower crystallinity values (67–82%) were obtained when the Al content exceeded Si/Al = 25, attributable to framework distortion arising from increased aluminum incorporation and associated charge-balancing cations.

## Textural Properties

Nitrogen physisorption measurements revealed BET surface areas between 342 and 418 m²/g for fully crystalline MFI samples, with the highest value (418 m²/g) achieved at Si/Al = 60. These values align with the theoretical surface area range of 400–450 m²/g reported for microporous MFI structures. Total pore volumes ranged from 0.18 to 0.24 cm³/g, with the majority (0.16–0.19 cm³/g) attributed to micropore volume as determined by t-plot analysis. Samples with Si/Al < 30 exhibited reduced surface areas (342–365 m²/g) and pore volumes (0.18–0.20 cm³/g), attributable to the presence of extra-framework aluminum species partially occluding microporous channels.

Comparison with literature values for conventionally synthesized ZSM-5 (surface area: 350–420 m²/g; pore volume: 0.17–0.22 cm³/g) confirms that the high-throughput protocol produces materials with comparable textural properties while reducing synthesis time by approximately 40%.

## Acidic Characteristics

Temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia (NH₃-TPD) quantified total acid site concentrations between 0.42 and 0.89 mmol/g, with strong acid site densities (desorption temperatures > 350°C) ranging from 0.18 to 0.41 mmol/g. As expected, acid site concentration demonstrated an inverse relationship with Si/Al ratio, increasing with greater aluminum content. Samples prepared at Si/Al = 40 exhibited total acid site concentrations of 0.89 mmol/g with a strong-to-weak acid site ratio of 0.85, values consistent with previous reports for ZSM-5 catalysts in this composition range.

Notably, strong acid site density increased from 0.18 mmol/g at Si/Al = 200 to 0.41 mmol/g at Si/Al = 40, while the desorption peak maximum shifted from 298°C to 367°C, indicating strengthened acid sites with higher aluminum content. This trend reflects the increased concentration of Brønsted acid sites generated by isomorphous substitution of Si⁴⁺ by Al³⁺ in the zeolite framework.

## Structure-Property Relationships

The correlation between synthesis conditions and final catalyst properties provides insight into optimal formulation strategies. Crystallization temperature exerted the most significant influence on phase purity,

The synthesis of ZSM-11, identified by the IZA structure code MEL, has garnered significant attention over the last decade due to its structural isomorphism with ZSM-5 (MFI). Both frameworks possess intersecting 10-membered ring (MR) channels, but MEL is distinguished by a straight-channel system lacking the sinusoidal pores characteristic of MFI. This subtle topological difference often results in distinct shape selectivity and diffusion properties, making MEL a superior catalyst for specific hydrocarbon isomerization and cracking reactions.

Historically, the synthesis of pure MEL topology relies heavily on tetrabutylammonium (TBA+) cations, typically introduced as tetrabutylammonium hydroxide (TBAOH) or bromide, acting as the Structure-Directing Agent (SDA). Recent literature highlights a concerted effort to optimize synthesis parameters to reduce the high cost associated with TBA+ while maintaining phase purity. Advances in seed-directed synthesis have been pivotal; for instance, studies demonstrate that adding as little as 1.0 wt% of MEL seeds to a TBA-deficient gel can successfully nucleate the MEL phase, preventing the competitive formation of the thermodynamically stable quartz phase or the MFI framework.

A critical area of investigation involves the structural interplay between MFI and MEL. Research utilizing tetrapropylammonium (TPAOH) as a co-SDA reveals that the Si/Al ratio of the gel dictates the final framework outcome. At lower Si/Al ratios (e.g., <50), TPAOH predominantly directs the MFI structure; however, at higher silica content (Si/Al > 100), the system favors the formation of MEL or MFI/MEL intergrowths. Recent findings utilizing Rietveld refinement of XRD data show that specific TBA+/TPA+ molar ratios can precisely tune the stacking fault probability, allowing for the tailored design of "coherent intergrowths" that combine the diffusion pathways of both topologies.

Furthermore, the creation of hierarchical ZSM-11 has emerged as a primary strategy to mitigate diffusion limitations. Recent approaches employ dual-templating strategies, combining microporous SDAs (TBAOH) with mesoporogens such as cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) or amphiphilic organosilanes. These syntheses, typically conducted under hydrothermal conditions at 150–180°C for 24–48 hours, yield materials with intracrystalline mesopores (2–10 nm) while preserving the intrinsic microporous acidity. Characterization via Ar physisorption and TEM confirms that these hierarchical MEL zeolites exhibit significantly improved lifetime in methanol-to-hydrocarbons (MTH) reactions compared to their conventional microporous counterparts.

Despite these advances, challenges remain. The complete elimination of organic templates (organotemplate-free synthesis) for pure MEL has proven more difficult than for MFI, often resulting in phase impurities or low crystallinity. Future directions are focused on the sustainable synthesis of MEL using low-cost, biomass-derived SDAs, and the precise atomic-scale engineering of active sites to enhance catalyst stability in biomass conversion. Understanding the nucleation kinetics of MEL relative to other framework types, such as BEA and FAU in interzeolite transformations, represents the frontier of this field.

### Synthesis of MFI-Type Zeolite Membranes for Gas Separation

#### 1. Substrate Preparation
A porous α-alumina disc (diameter: 25 mm, thickness: 2 mm, average pore size: 1 µm) was employed as the support. The substrate was sequentially sonicated in acetone, ethanol, and deionized water for 15 minutes each to remove impurities. It was then dried at 120°C for 2 hours in a convection oven.

#### 2. Synthesis Gel Preparation
A clear synthesis gel with a molar composition of 1.0 SiO₂ : 0.032 TPABr : 0.1 NaOH : 35 H₂O was prepared. In a 250 mL polypropylene beaker, 0.41 g of sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 97%, pellets) was dissolved in 45.0 g of deionized water under magnetic stirring at 300 rpm. To this solution, 1.55 g of tetrapropylammonium bromide (TPABr, 98%) was added and stirred until fully dissolved. Finally, 10.0 g of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98%) was added dropwise over 15 minutes. The resulting mixture was stirred continuously at room temperature (25°C) for 24 hours to allow for complete hydrolysis and aging. The final gel pH was approximately 11.5.

#### 3. Hydrothermal Synthesis and Membrane Formation
The aged gel was transferred into a 125 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The cleaned alumina support was positioned vertically within the autoclave using a Teflon holder, ensuring the gel contacted one face of the disc. The sealed autoclave was placed in a forced-air convection oven and heated to 180°C at a ramp rate of 1°C/min. The hydrothermal crystallization was conducted under autogenous pressure at this temperature for 48 hours under static conditions.

#### 4. Post-Synthesis Processing
After synthesis, the autoclave was quenched in cold water. The membrane was carefully removed and rinsed thoroughly with copious amounts of deionized water to remove any residual gel or loosely adhered crystals from the surface. It was then immersed in 100 mL of a 1.0 M ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃) solution at 80°C for 6 hours under gentle stirring (100 rpm) to exchange sodium ions. This ion-exchange step was repeated twice. Subsequently, the membrane was washed with deionized water and dried overnight at 80°C in an oven.

#### 5. Template Removal (Calcination)
To remove the occluded TPABr template, the dried membrane was placed in a quartz tube furnace. Calcination was performed under a flowing air atmosphere (100 mL/min). The temperature was increased from room temperature to 450°C at a controlled ramp rate of 0.5°C/min, held at 450°C for 8 hours, and then allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. This slow ramp is critical to avoid crack formation from thermal stress.

#### 6. Yield and Membrane Characteristics
The synthesis procedure typically yields one membrane disc per autoclave. The successful formation of a continuous, intergrown MFI (silicalite-1) zeolite layer is indicated by a uniform, glossy surface. The membrane thickness, as determined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), typically ranges from 5 to 15 µm. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) confirms a Si/Al ratio >500 (essentially aluminum-free silicalite-1). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the membrane surface show characteristic peaks corresponding to the MFI framework (e.g., 2θ = 7.9°, 8.8°, 23.1°, 23.9°) with no evidence of other crystalline phases.

#### 7. Typical Gas Separation Performance
Single-gas permeance tests at 25°C and 1 bar pressure difference are used for initial evaluation. A high-quality membrane exhibits a high permeance for small molecules (e.g., H₂: ~1-5 × 10⁻⁷ mol m⁻² s⁻¹ Pa⁻¹) and a low permeance for larger ones (e.g., n-C₄H₁₀: <1 × 10⁻⁹ mol m⁻² s⁻¹ Pa⁻¹). The ideal selectivity for gas pairs such as H₂/n-C₄H₁₀ or CO₂/N₂ often exceeds the corresponding Knudsen diffusion values (e.g., H₂/n-C₄H₁₀ selectivity >50), confirming the presence of a continuous, molecular-sieving zeolite layer with minimal non-zeolitic defects.

# Introduction and Background

## Historical Development of Zeolite Y Synthesis

Zeolite Y, a synthetic aluminosilicate belonging to the FAU framework type, represents one of the most significant advances in heterogeneous catalysis and adsorption technology. The material was first developed by researchers at Union Carbide Corporation in 1964 as an improved alternative to zeolite X, offering superior thermal and hydrothermal stability critical for demanding industrial processes. The discovery emerged from systematic investigation of the Na2O-Al2O3-SiO2-H2O quaternary system under hydrothermal conditions, establishing the foundational synthetic methodology that continues to inform contemporary production protocols.

The evolution of zeolite Y synthesis has proceeded through several distinct phases. Early preparative approaches employed conventional hydrothermal crystallization from gel compositions requiring elevated temperatures (100-150°C) and extended reaction times (24-72 hours). The 1970s and 1980s witnessed significant optimization of synthesis parameters, including the development of clear solution methods and the introduction of organic structure-directing agents. The seed-induced or seed-assisted synthesis methodology emerged as a pivotal advancement, wherein pre-formed zeolite Y crystals serve as nucleation sites, enabling improved control over particle size distribution, reduced crystallization times, and enhanced reproducibility. This approach has become increasingly important for manufacturing catalysts meeting stringent industrial specifications.

## Industrial Relevance and Market Context

Zeolite Y holds paramount industrial importance, particularly in petroleum refining, where it serves as the primary active component in fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) catalysts. The global FCC catalyst market exceeds 450,000 metric tons annually, with zeolite Y comprising 20-40 weight percent of commercial catalyst formulations. The worldwide market for zeolite Y and related FCC catalysts is valued at approximately USD 3.5-4.5 billion annually, reflecting the essential role of this material in producing over 90% of gasoline and a significant fraction of light olefins worldwide. Major producers including Albemarle Corporation, BASF, and Grace Chemical Company operate facilities with combined annual production capacities exceeding 500,000 tonnes of zeolite-containing catalyst products.

The industrial significance extends beyond petroleum refining to include hydrocracking, aromatics processing, and emerging applications in biomass conversion. FCC units processing approximately 15 million barrels per day of feedstock globally depend upon zeolite Y for their catalytic functionality. This demand creates substantial economic incentive for optimized synthesis methodologies that maximize product quality while minimizing production costs.

## Key Scientific Challenges and Research Motivation

Despite decades of industrial application, zeolite Y synthesis presents persistent scientific challenges requiring continued research attention. The relationship between synthesis gel composition, crystallization kinetics, and final product properties remains incompletely understood, complicating process optimization. Control of crystal size distribution proves particularly critical, as particle dimensions directly influence catalytic performance, diffusion characteristics, and catalyst formulation requirements. The seed-induced method addresses several challenges inherent to conventional synthesis, including reducing template dependence, controlling nucleation, and improving phase purity. However, fundamental questions regarding seed effectiveness mechanisms, optimal seed loading, and seed preparation protocols continue to motivate investigation.

Environmental and economic pressures further emphasize the need for advanced synthesis approaches. Regulatory constraints on catalyst composition, particularly regarding rare earth content, compel development of synthesis methods producing zeolite Y with intrinsic catalytic activity sufficient to reduce additive requirements. Simultaneously, energy efficiency imperatives drive research toward lower-temperature synthesis protocols and reduced processing times. The seed-induced method offers potential advantages in addressing these concerns by enabling more efficient utilization of raw materials and energy resources.

Research into seed-induced zeolite Y synthesis remains motivated by the

# Experimental Results and Discussion: Synthesis and Characterization of Zeolite L with One-Dimensional Channel Systems

## Synthesis and Crystallization

Zeolite L with the LTL framework type was successfully synthesized via hydrothermal crystallization at 443 K (170°C) for 72 hours using a gel composition of 5.0 SiO₂:Al₂O₃:3.5 K₂O:130 H₂O. The as-synthesized product exhibited high phase purity with characteristic hexagonal morphology and particle dimensions of 0.5–2.0 μm as observed by scanning electron microscopy. The crystallization kinetics indicated an induction period of approximately 8 hours, with full crystallization achieved by 72 hours, consistent with literature reports for LTL-type zeolite synthesis under similar conditions.

## Structural Characterization

Powder X-ray diffraction analysis (Cu Kα radiation, λ = 1.5406 Å) confirmed the successful formation of the LTL framework structure. The diffraction pattern displayed characteristic reflections at 2θ values of 5.6°, 11.5°, 19.5°, 22.8°, 25.6°, 28.1°, and 30.6°, corresponding to the (100), (110), (210), (300), (310), (320), and (410) planes, respectively. These positions align with reported values for zeolite L (JCPDS Card No. 40-1463) and confirm the hexagonal P6/mmm space group symmetry. The refined unit cell parameters (a = 18.4 Å, c = 7.5 Å) agree within experimental error (±0.1 Å) with literature values, indicating proper framework assembly without significant lattice distortions.

## Textural Properties

Nitrogen physisorption measurements at 77 K revealed a Type I isotherm characteristic of microporous materials. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was determined to be 287 m²/g, with a micropore surface area of 263 m²/g calculated via the t-plot method. The total pore volume was 0.19 cm³/g, and the micropore volume (Horvath-Kawazoe method) was 0.15 cm³/g. These values fall within the expected range for zeolite L (BET surface area: 250–350 m²/g; pore volume: 0.15–0.25 cm³/g) and confirm the preservation of the microporous structure following calcination at 823 K (550°C).

## Acidic Properties

Ammonia temperature-programmed desorption (NH₃-TPD) measurements indicated total acid site concentrations of 0.84 mmol/g, with weak acid sites (desorption maximum ~473 K) contributing 0.32 mmol/g and strong acid sites (desorption maximum ~623 K) contributing 0.52 mmol/g. Pyridine adsorption infrared spectroscopy

Brønsted acid sites (BAS) constitute the fundamental active centers in zeolite-based heterogeneous catalysis, arising from the isomorphous substitution of tetrahedral silicon ($Si^{4+}$) by trivalent aluminum ($Al^{3+}$) within the crystalline aluminosilicate framework. This substitution generates a net negative charge within the lattice, which is locally compensated by a proton, forming a bridging hydroxyl group denoted as Si–(OH)–Al. The unique catalytic prowess of these sites lies in their intrinsic strong acidity, characterized by a Hammett acidity function ($H_0$) often ranging between -5 and -12, combined with the shape-selective properties imposed by the uniform microporous topology (typically 3–8 Å pore diameters).

Historically, the discovery of synthetic zeolites, particularly the pioneering work by Barrer and Milton in the 1940s and 1950s, transitioned zeolites from mineral curiosities to industrial workhorses. The commercialization of Zeolite X and Y by Union Carbide in the late 1950s revolutionized fluid catalytic cracking (FCC), establishing zeolites as the backbone of the modern petrochemical industry. Today, the global zeolite market is valued at approximately USD 13–14 billion, with synthetic zeolites commanding a dominant share due to high-value catalytic applications. In the FCC sector alone, zeolite-based catalysts facilitate the processing of over 12 million barrels of crude oil per day globally. This process relies heavily on USY (Ultra-Stable Y) zeolites, where the BAS density and strength are meticulously tuned to maximize gasoline yield (typically 40–50 wt% of the feed) while minimizing coke formation.

Despite this ubiquity, the precise nature of Brønsted acidity remains a complex scientific challenge. The reactivity of a BAS is not uniform; it is modulated by the local framework geometry (specifically the T–O–T bond angles), the Si/Al ratio, and the spatial proximity of Al atoms (Loewenstein’s rule). A critical industrial requirement is hydrothermal stability. Under FCC regeneration conditions, which often exceed 700°C in the presence of 100% steam, dealumination occurs, leading to the migration of aluminum out of the framework and the formation of extra-framework aluminum (EFAL) species. This degradation irreversibly diminishes the number of BAS and generates Lewis acid sites that can catalyze undesirable side reactions. Furthermore, the strong confinement effect of the micropores, while essential for shape selectivity, imposes severe diffusion limitations on bulky reactants, leading to rapid catalyst deactivation via coking.

Consequently, contemporary research is motivated by the need to decouple acid strength from site density and to engineer hierarchical pore systems that mitigate diffusion constraints. Advanced characterization techniques, such as solid-state NMR spectroscopy ($^{27}Al$, $^{29}Si$) and FTIR spectroscopy of adsorbed probe molecules (e.g., pyridine or collidine), are employed to quantify the concentration and accessibility of BAS. Current investigations focus on tailoring BAS for emerging applications, such as the Methanol-to-Olefins (MTO) process utilizing SAPO-34, where a precise BAS density is required to balance the induction period and catalyst lifetime, or biomass conversion, where moderate acidity is necessary to prevent the over-dehydration of complex oxygenates. Understanding the proton transfer mechanisms at the atomic level is therefore essential for advancing catalyst design toward higher efficiency and sustainability.

**Comparative Analysis of Metal-Organic Frameworks and Zeolites: Synthesis, Stability, and Functional Performance**

The advent of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) has significantly expanded the landscape of porous materials, presenting both complementarity and competition to the well-established class of zeolitic molecular sieves. A rigorous comparison necessitates examination across the domains of synthesis, structural chemistry, physicochemical stability, and resultant application performance.

**Synthesis and Structural Chemistry**
Zeolites are crystalline, microporous aluminosilicates based on a fully inorganic, covalent tetrahedral framework (TO₄, T = Si, Al). Their synthesis typically occurs via hydrothermal crystallization from alkaline gels (e.g., NaOH, TPAOH) at elevated temperatures (80–180 °C) and autogenous pressure over extended periods (12 hours to several days). The process is governed by complex solution-mediated and solid-phase transformation mechanisms, often requiring strict control over the Si/Al ratio, hydroxide concentration, and template (structure-directing agent) to achieve phase purity, as exemplified in the synthesis of ZSM-5 (MFI) using tetrapropylammonium ions.

In contrast, MOFs are coordination polymers comprising metal-ion nodes (e.g., Zn²⁺, Cu²⁺, Zr⁴⁺, Cr³⁺) connected by multidentate organic linkers (e.g., terephthalic acid, 2-methylimidazole). Synthesis is often performed under milder solvothermal conditions (e.g., 65–130 °C for 24-72 hours in DMF or water/ethanol mixtures). The modular "node-and-linker" approach affords exceptional structural and compositional diversity, enabling deliberate pore engineering, as seen in the isoreticular series of IRMOF-n materials. However, this often comes at the expense of thermodynamic stability compared to the robust, fully oxide zeolite frameworks.

**Thermal and Chemical Stability**
This disparity in stability is a defining differentiator. High-silica zeolites (e.g., silicalite-1) and aluminophosphates (e.g., AlPO₄-5) exhibit exceptional thermal stability (>1000 °C in air) and resistance to harsh chemical environments, including steam and mineral acids (excluding HF). This robustness stems from strong, covalent T–O–T bonds. Many prototypical MOFs, such as MOF-5 (Zn₄O(BDC)₃), degrade under humid conditions or at moderate temperatures (<400 °C) due to the hydrolysis of coordinate bonds or linker oxidation. Notable exceptions include zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs, e.g., ZIF-8 with a decomposition temperature near 550 °C) and carboxylate-based frameworks with high-valence metals (e.g., UiO-66, Zr₆O₄(OH)₄(BDC)₆, stable to ~500 °C and in water).

**Porosity and Functional Performance**
Both families achieve record-setting porosities. MOFs currently hold the records for BET surface area (e.g., DUT-60, ~7800 m² g⁻¹) and pore volume, while zeolites typically possess lower but highly uniform microporosity (<2 nm) with precise shape selectivity. This difference dictates application niches.

In **catalysis**, zeolites excel in Brønsted acid-catalyzed reactions (e.g., fluid catalytic cracking, methanol-to-olefins) due to their strong, stable acid sites generated by framework aluminum. Their shape selectivity is unparalleled, as demonstrated in the selective production of *para*-xylene in ZSM-5. MOFs offer superior opportunities for leveraging unsaturated metal sites (e.g., Cu in HKUST-1) or functionalized organic linkers for Lewis acid, base, or redox catalysis, though stability under process conditions remains a key challenge.

For **gas separation and storage**, the high density of charge-balancing cations in zeolites (e.g., Na⁺ in 4A zeolite) creates strong electrostatic fields ideal for polar molecule separations (e.g., CO₂/N₂). MOFs offer more tunable host-guest interactions via linker functionalization. For instance, the open metal sites in M-MOF-74 (M = Mg, Ni) provide high enthalpy of adsorption for CO₂, while Fe₂(dobdc) demonstrates exceptional selectivity for olefin/paraffin separation.

**Conclusion**
Zeolites represent a class of materials optimized by nature and decades of refinement for high-stability, industrial-scale processes. # Text Mining Procedure for Zeolite Synthesis Knowledge Extraction

## Overview

A systematic text mining workflow was developed to extract zeolite synthesis parameters from scientific literature. This procedure enables automated extraction of reaction compositions, temperatures, durations, and other critical synthesis conditions from thousands of published sources.

## Literature Collection

Scientific publications were retrieved from Web of Science, Scopus, and Google Scholar databases using the search query: "zeolite AND synthesis" with date restrictions from 1990–2023. Approximately 15,000 publications were initially identified. After duplicate removal and abstract screening for synthesis-relevant content, a corpus of 8,200 full-text articles was compiled in PDF format. All documents were stored in structured folders organized by zeolite framework type (MFI, FAU, MOR, CHA, etc.).

## Text Preprocessing

PDF documents were converted to plain text using Adobe Acrobat Pro with encoding set to UTF-8. Text was tokenized and sentences were split using the Natural Language Toolkit (NLTK) library in Python 3.9. Approximately 2.3 million sentences were generated. Stop words were removed, and part-of-speech tagging was applied using the spaCy model "en_core_web_sm." Chemical entity recognition was performed using the ChemDataExtractor package, version 2.0.1, which identifies chemical formulas, IUPAC names, and InChI strings.

## Named Entity Recognition

A custom named entity recognition model was trained using the spaCy framework to identify synthesis-specific parameters. Training data were annotated manually for 500 representative sentences containing temperature values, durations, molar ratios, pH values, and container types. The BIO tagging scheme was applied. The model achieved an F1-score of 0.87 on a held-out test set of 150 sentences.

## Information Extraction

Synthesis parameters were extracted using pattern-matching rules combined with the trained NER model. Temperature values were identified by detecting numerical patterns followed by "°C," "K," or temperature unit abbreviations. Durations were extracted by recognizing patterns such as "X hours," "X days," or "X min." Molar ratios (Si/Al, Na/K, H2O/Si) were extracted by identifying ratios expressed in colon or slash notation. The extraction results were stored in a structured SQLite database with fields for zeolite type, SiO2 source, Al source, structure-directing agent, mineralizer, temperature, time, pH, container material, and yield.

## Validation

Extracted parameters were validated against manually curated synthesis recipes from 200 randomly selected papers. Precision

# Deep Eutectic Solvents as Templates for Zeolite Synthesis: A Review of Green Chemistry Approaches

## Introduction

The synthesis of microporous crystalline materials, particularly zeolites, has traditionally relied on organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs) such as quaternary ammonium salts and amines. While effective, these conventional templates present significant environmental and economic challenges due to their toxicity, high cost, and the necessity for calcination to remove templates. In recent years, deep eutectic solvents (DESs) have emerged as promising alternative templates, combining the templating efficiency of conventional OSDAs with the advantages of sustainable chemistry.

## DES Classification and Compositions

Deep eutectic solvents are formed when two or more components are combined at specific molar ratios, producing a liquid with a melting point substantially lower than either constituent. For zeolite synthesis, choline chloride (ChCl)-based DESs have demonstrated particular efficacy. Common systems include ChCl:urea (1:2 molar ratio, melting point 12°C), ChCl:ethylene glycol (1:2, mp 67°C), and ChCl:malonic acid (1:1, mp 110°C), as systematically characterized by Abbott and colleagues.

## Synthesis Parameters and Mechanisms

The synthesis of zeolites using DES templates typically proceeds under hydrothermal conditions at temperatures ranging from 120 to 180°C for durations of 24 hours to 7 days. Cooper and colleagues pioneered this approach, demonstrating that ChCl:urea DES could serve dual functions as both solvent and template for zeolite Na-SSZ-39 synthesis at 150°C over 72 hours. The DES components participate in zeolite crystallization through multiple mechanisms: the chloride ion acts as a mineralizing agent, while the hydrogen-bonding network between components creates structured nanoenvironments that direct framework assembly.

## Zeolite Framework Synthesis with DES Templates

Significant advances have been achieved in synthesizing various zeolite frameworks using DES systems. ZSM-5 (MFI topology) has been prepared using choline chloride-based DESs combined with tetramethylammonium hydroxide as a co-template. Silicalite-1 crystallized from ChCl:glycerol DES at 160°C for 48 hours exhibited enhanced hydrophobicity compared to conventionally templated materials. Furthermore, zeolite Beta has been obtained using imidazolium-based DESs at 150°C for 96 hours, achieving Si/Al ratios adjustable through synthesis composition modification.

## Structural and Catalytic Properties

DES-templated zeolites demonstrate distinctive properties arising from their synthesis environment. The incorporation of DES components during crystallization creates hierarchical porosity through partial decomposition, while the absence of surfactant-like molecules prevents pore blockage. Materials synthesized by Li and colleagues using ChCl:oxalic acid DES exhibited external surface areas up to 25% higher than conventional preparations. Catalytic evaluation in methanol-to-olefins reactions revealed enhanced selectivity for light olefins, attributed to reduced diffusion limitations and modified acid site distributions.

## Advantages and Future Perspectives

The DES approach offers compelling advantages: inexpensive, biodegradable components; simplified template removal through water washing; and tunable DES composition enabling precise control over crystal size and morphology. However, challenges remain regarding complete mechanistic understanding, scalability, and extension to aluminum-rich zeolites. Future research should focus on computational modeling of DES-zeolite interactions and development of designer DES systems for targeted framework synthesis.

**Preparation of Ammonium-Modified Natural Clinoptilolite for Slow-Release Fertilizer Applications**

To prepare a high-efficiency slow-release fertilizer carrier, natural clinoptilolite tuff is processed through alkaline activation followed by ammonium ion exchange. The procedure begins with the crushing and sieving of 50.0 g of raw natural zeolite to obtain a uniform particle size fraction of 50–100 µm. The fine powder is transferred to a ceramic crucible and calcined in a muffle furnace at 550 °C for 4 hours to remove volatile organic impurities and carbonates, thereby unblocking the microporous channels.

Following calcination, the activated zeolite is subjected to a hydrothermal recrystallization step to enhance phase purity. The calcined material is dispersed in 250 mL of a 2.0 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH) aqueous solution within a 500 mL polypropylene beaker under constant stirring at 300 rpm. The resulting suspension is transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The hydrothermal crystallization is conducted at 100 °C for 6 hours. After cooling to room temperature, the solid product is recovered by vacuum filtration and washed repeatedly with deionized water until the pH of the filtrate is neutral (pH 7.0).

To convert the sodium form into the agriculturally relevant ammonium form, the zeolite is ion-exchanged. The washed solid is added to 300 mL of a 1.0 M ammonium chloride ($NH_4Cl$) solution in a 500 mL round-bottom flask equipped with a reflux condenser. The mixture is heated to 80 °C and stirred magnetically for 24 hours to ensure complete cation exchange ($Na^+ \rightarrow NH_4^+$). The treated zeolite is then separated by filtration and washed with warm deionized water (approximately 60 °C) to remove excess chloride ions, verified by a negative silver nitrate ($AgNO_3$) test. Finally, the product is dried in a forced-air oven at 105 °C for 12 hours.

Typically, this procedure yields approximately 48.5 g of ammonium-modified clinoptilolite, corresponding to a 97% yield based on the starting mass. Characterization via X-ray diffraction (XRD) confirms the retention of the HEU-type framework structure with increased crystallinity. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) reveals a characteristic platy morphology with minimal surface deposition. The cation exchange capacity (CEC), measured via the ammonium acetate method, is observed to increase from an initial 120 meq/100 g (raw) to approximately 165 meq/100 g, confirming the successful enhancement of nutrient retention capabilities essential for agricultural use.

**A Comprehensive Review of Single-Atom Catalysts on Zeolite Supports: Advances, Synthesis, and Prospects**

**Introduction and Rationale**
Single-atom catalysts (SACs), where isolated metal atoms are stabilized on a support, represent the ultimate frontier in atom-efficient heterogeneous catalysis. Zeolites, with their crystalline microporous structures, regular coordination environments, and exceptional (hydro)thermal stability, have emerged as premier supports for SACs. The synergy between a well-defined single-site and the shape-selective, confining pores of a zeolite creates unique catalytic architectures, often termed "single-atom catalysts in a matrix" or "zeolite-trapped metal catalysts." This review focuses on advances from the past decade in synthesizing, characterizing, and applying zeolite-supported SACs, emphasizing the critical role of framework topology and synthesis methodology.

**Synthesis Strategies and Framework-Dependent Outcomes**
The synthesis of zeolite-supported SACs is broadly classified into two paradigms: *post-synthetic grafting* and *direct hydrothermal synthesis (DHS)*.

1.  **Post-Synthetic Grafting:** This involves anchoring metal precursors onto pre-formed, dealuminated, or defect-rich zeolites via techniques like ion exchange, atomic layer deposition (ALD), or surface organometallic chemistry. For example, Pt₁/FAU (zeolite Y) catalysts have been prepared via ion exchange with [Pt(NH₃)₄]²⁺, followed by careful thermal activation under O₂ and H₂ to avoid sintering. This method offers precise control over metal loading but can be limited by the accessibility of exchange sites and the stability of the grafted species under harsh conditions.

2.  **Direct Hydrothermal Synthesis (DHS):** This is a more transformative approach, where the metal precursor is introduced during the zeolite crystallization gel. The key challenge is to prevent the formation of metal oxide clusters or nanoparticles during the high-temperature, hydrothermal synthesis. Success relies on the use of *structure-directing agents (SDAs)* that can simultaneously complex the metal and direct zeolite crystallization. Seminal work by the groups of Xiao and Liu demonstrated this for MFI (ZSM-5) frameworks.
    *   For **Pt@MFI**, tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPA⁺) was found to complex Pt²⁺ as [Pt(NH₃)₂(TPA)₂]²⁺, leading to its incorporation as isolated Pt sites within the 5-1-1 channel intersections. Similar strategies using tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH) have been employed for **Pd@BEA (Beta)** zeolites.
    *   For small-pore frameworks like **CHA (SSZ-13)**, the use of a Cu-complexing SDA like *N,N,N-trimethyl-1-adamantammonium hydroxide (TMAdaOH)* is crucial. This has led to the creation of Cu-CHA SACs, now commercially deployed in the selective catalytic reduction (SCR) of NOx with NH₃. Here, isolated, mobile Cu ions ([Cu(OH)]⁺) in the cha cages are the active sites, showcasing the paradigm of a zeolite-stabilized SAC.
    *   Recent advances extend to noble metals like **Ir@MFI** and **Rh@MFI**, where metal precursors (e.g., [Ir(acac)₃]) are mixed with TPABr and subjected to crystallization at 170°C for 3-7 days, yielding exceptionally stable SACs.

**Characterization and Specific Catalytic Insights**
Advanced characterization, particularly aberration-corrected high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM), X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), and CO/NO probe molecule infrared spectroscopy, is indispensable. For instance, HAADF-STEM has visually confirmed isolated Pt atoms within the channels of MFI. XAS analyses (EXAFS and XANES) of Pt@MFI confirm Pt-O coordination with no Pt-Pt scattering paths, proving atomic dispersion. In catalysis:
*   **Pt@MFI** exhibits remarkable stability in propane dehydrogenation (at 590°C), outperforming nanoparticle counterparts due to suppression of coking and sintering.
*   **Ir@MFI** shows high efficiency in the non-oxidative coupling of methane, with ethylene selectivity >70% at high conversion, leveraging the shape selectivity of the MFI pores.
*   The aforementioned **Cu-CHA** SAC demonstrates near-100% NOx conversion in NH₃-SCR within a specific temperature window (200-450°C), a direct result of the precise coordination environment of the Cu ions.

**Current Challenges and Future Directions**
Despite progress, significant challenges remain:
1.  **Generalizable Synthesis:** Most DHS protocols are metal- and framework-specific. Developing universal SDAs or

# Silylation and Surface Passivation of Zeolites

## Introduction and Rationale

The external surface of zeolite crystals presents a significant challenge in heterogeneous catalysis, as acidic sites located on the external surface and at the crystal mouths can promote undesirable side reactions, including excessive coke formation and reduced product selectivity. Surface passivation through silylation has emerged as a powerful strategy to mitigate these limitations, enabling precise control over the accessibility of active sites and the diffusion pathways for reactants and products.

## Silylation Chemistry and Mechanisms

The silylation of zeolite surfaces employs organosilicon compounds that react with surface hydroxyl groups (silanols) to form hydrophobic silica-like layers. Trimethylchlorosilane (TMCS), hexamethyldisilazane (HMDS), and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) represent the most widely utilized silylating agents. TMCS (boiling point: 57°C) reacts with surface silanols via:

≡Si–OH + Cl–Si(CH₃)₃ → ≡Si–O–Si(CH₃)₃ + HCl

The reaction typically proceeds at temperatures between 80-200°C under inert atmosphere, with treatment durations ranging from 30 minutes to 6 hours depending on the zeolite framework and desired degree of passivation. HMDS, which decomposes at approximately 200°C, offers the advantage of producing no HCl byproduct, making it suitable for more sensitive catalytic systems.

## Experimental Procedures

Two principal approaches dominate silylation protocols. Liquid-phase treatments involve dispersing the zeolite in anhydrous toluene, xylene, or cyclohexane containing 1-10 wt% silylating agent, followed by refluxing at 110-140°C for 2-8 hours. Vapor-phase silylation, conducted under vacuum or nitrogen flow, utilizes temperatures of 150-300°C and proves particularly effective for achieving uniform coverage on high-silica zeolites such as silicalite-1.

Post-synthesis calcination at 400-550°C in air removes organic residues and stabilizes the siloxane bonds. Characterization by FT-IR spectroscopy reveals the disappearance of isolated silanol bands at 3740 cm⁻¹ and the emergence of CH₃ deformation modes at 1250-1280 cm⁻¹, confirming successful silylation.

## Effects on Catalytic Properties

Quantitative studies demonstrate that optimized silylation can reduce external surface acidity by 60-90% while preserving intra-crystalline acid sites. This selective passivation proves particularly valuable in shape-selective reactions such as xylene isomerization, where suppressingtrans-alkylation on the external surface enhances para-selectivity. Research by(Vieweg et al., 2004) on HMDS-modified H-ZSM-5 showed increased p-xylene selectivity from 24% to 41% in toluene methylation.

Coke formation rates decrease substantially following silylation, as the hydrophobic external surface inhibits the condensation of aromatic precursors.寿命 studies on silylated zeolite catalysts in methanol-to-olefins (MTO) reactions indicate extended catalyst lifetimes due to delayed pore mouth blockage.

## Characterization and Quantification

Solid-state ²⁹Si NMR spectroscopy provides quantitative insight into silylation depth, with Q³ sites (≈ -100 ppm) increasing relative to Q⁴ (-110 ppm) after treatment. Nitrogen physisorption confirms maintained microporosity, with minimal changes in BET surface area (<5% reduction). Temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia (NH₃-TPD) reveals reduced total acidity correlating directly with silylation severity.

## Conclusions and Outlook

Surface silylation represents a mature and versatile methodology for tailoring zeolite catalytic behavior. Future directions emphasize atomic layer deposition (ALD) techniques for atomic-scale precision in surface modification and the development of bifunctional

# Synthesis of Zeolite L with One-Dimensional Channel Systems

## Introduction

Zeolite L (LTL framework type) is a crystalline aluminosilicate characterized by a unique one-dimensional channel system with pore openings of approximately 7.1 Å. The structure consists of cancrinite cages linked by hexagonal prisms, forming straight channels that run parallel to the crystallographic c-axis. This specific architecture makes zeolite L particularly valuable for applications in catalysis, gas separation, and sensor technology. The synthesis procedure described herein follows a modified hydrothermal method based on the work of Ciric and subsequent optimizations reported in the literature.

## Reagents and Materials

The following reagents of analytical grade were employed: potassium hydroxide (KOH, ≥85%, 7.52 g), aluminum hydroxide (Al(OH)₃, ≥98%, 1.56 g), fumed silica (SiO₂, ≥99.8%, 4.50 g), and deionized water (68.0 mL). All glassware was cleaned with aqua regia and rinsed thoroughly with deionized water prior to use.

## Synthesis Procedure

**Step 1: Aluminate Solution Preparation**

The potassium hydroxide was dissolved in 38.0 mL of deionized water in a 150-mL polypropylene beaker under magnetic stirring at 300 rpm. The solution was heated to 80°C using a temperature-controlled hot plate. Once thermal equilibrium was achieved, aluminum hydroxide was added gradually over 15 minutes while maintaining constant stirring. The mixture was stirred for an additional 30 minutes at 80°C until a clear sodium aluminate solution was formed. This solution was subsequently cooled to room temperature (25°C) and aged for 2 hours.

**Step 2: Silicate Gel Preparation**

In a separate 250-mL polypropylene beaker, fumed silica was slowly added to 30.0 mL of deionized water under vigorous magnetic stirring at 400 rpm. The silica suspension was stirred for 45 minutes at ambient temperature until a homogeneous white suspension was obtained.

**Step 3: Gel Synthesis and Mixing**

The silicate suspension was transferred dropwise to the cooled aluminate solution over a period of 20 minutes while maintaining continuous stirring at 350 rpm. The combined mixture was stirred for an additional 60 minutes at room temperature to ensure complete homogenization. The final gel composition, expressed as molar ratios, was: 5.0 K₂O : 0.1 Al₂O₃ : 1.0 SiO₂ : 40.0 H₂O.

**Step 4: Hydrothermal Crystallization**

The homogeneous gel was transferred into a 125-mL PTFE-lined stainless steel autoclave (Parr Instrument Company, Moline, IL). The autoclave was sealed and placed in a forced-air convection oven (Lindberg/Blue M, Thermo Scientific). Crystallization was conducted statically at 180°C for 72 hours. The heating ramp rate was maintained at 2°C/min from ambient temperature to the target temperature.

**Step 5: Product Recovery**

After the crystallization period, the autoclave was cooled naturally to room temperature over approximately 4 hours. The solid product was recovered by vacuum filtration using a Buchner funnel fitted with Whatman Grade 542 filter paper. The filter cake was washed with deionized water (500 mL total volume) until the filtrate reached pH 9.0. The washed solid was subsequently dried in an air atmosphere at 110°C for 12 hours in a laboratory drying oven.

**Step 6: Calcination**

The dried zeolite was transferred to a muffle furnace (Thermolyne F4800) and heated at 1°C/min to 550°C, held for 6 hours

Microwave-assisted synthesis (MAS) has emerged as a transformative methodology in zeolite science, offering rapid, volumetric heating that significantly accelerates nucleation kinetics compared to conventional hydrothermal (CHT) methods. Over the past decade, research has focused on exploiting microwave dielectric heating (typically at 2.45 GHz) to achieve precise control over phase selectivity, crystallinity, and particle size distribution.

Recent advances highlight the efficacy of MAS in synthesizing high-silica frameworks. For the MFI framework (ZSM-5), utilizing tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH) as a structure-directing agent (SDA) under microwave irradiation at 150–180°C reduces crystallization times from 24–48 hours to less than 60 minutes. This rapid thermal processing generates a high supersaturation ratio, promoting homogeneous nucleation and yielding nanocrystals (<100 nm) that significantly enhance diffusion properties in catalysis. Similarly, the synthesis of the BEA (Beta) framework, conventionally a time-intensive process requiring tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH), has been optimized via MAS. Studies demonstrate that microwave heating at 140°C for approximately 4 hours produces phase-pure Beta zeolite with reduced defect densities and improved textural properties compared to CHT-derived analogues.

In the realm of small-pore zeolites, MAS has been pivotal for the CHA framework (SSZ-13), essential for NH3-SCR DeNOx applications. Using N,N,N-trimethyl-1-adamantammonium hydroxide (TMAdaOH) as the SDA, researchers have successfully synthesized CHA with Si/Al ratios ranging from 5 to 25 in under 3 hours. The microwave environment facilitates the formation of uniform nanosheets and cubic crystals, minimizing the formation of competing phases like GIS or ANA often observed in CHT. Furthermore, MAS has been applied to large-pore FAU (faujasite) synthesis. By optimizing the synthesis gel composition (e.g., high alkalinity with NaOH), NaY zeolite crystallization can be achieved within 30–60 minutes. The rapid kinetics often result in the formation of hierarchical porosity due to the "nanoparticle assembly" mechanism induced by microwave fields.

Comparatively, MAS consistently yields smaller crystal sizes and narrower particle size distributions than CHT due to the burst of nucleation. However, challenges remain regarding scale-up; the penetration depth of microwaves is limited, creating thermal gradients in large-volume batches. Additionally, the rapid kinetics can sometimes trap metastable phases or impede the incorporation of heteroatoms.

Future directions are pivoting toward continuous flow microwave reactors to overcome batch limitations and the integration of MAS with "seed-assisted" or "SDA-free" routes to improve sustainability. The development of in-situ monitoring techniques coupled with microwave reactors promises to further elucidate the crystallization mechanisms, allowing for the deterministic design of zeolites with tailored architectures.

### **Microwave-Assisted Synthesis of Zeolites: An Introduction and Historical Background**

Zeolites, crystalline microporous aluminosilicates with well-defined channel and cage structures, are indispensable materials in modern industrial catalysis, adsorption, and ion-exchange processes. Their synthesis traditionally relies on hydrothermal crystallization, a process involving the heating of an aluminosilicate gel under autogenous pressure at elevated temperatures (typically 80–180°C) for extended periods, ranging from hours to several days. This conventional method, while robust, is inherently energy-intensive and suffers from limitations in controlling nucleation kinetics and crystal size distribution. The advent of microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis represents a paradigm shift in zeolite fabrication, offering a pathway to enhanced crystallization kinetics, improved phase selectivity, and novel morphological control.

The historical development of microwave synthesis is rooted in the broader application of microwave dielectric heating to chemical processes, which gained significant traction in the 1980s. The first reported microwave synthesis of a zeolite, Zeolite A (LTA), was published in 1988, demonstrating a dramatic reduction in crystallization time from over 24 hours to under 30 minutes. This seminal work ignited interest in exploiting the unique heating mechanism of microwaves—where dipole rotation and ionic conduction lead to rapid, volumetric, and selective heating of the reaction mixture. Unlike conventional convective heating, which establishes a thermal gradient from the vessel walls inward, microwave irradiation can homogeneously couple with the polar solvents (typically water) and ionic species (e.g., Na⁺, OH⁻, aluminosilicate precursors) present in the synthesis gel, enabling instantaneous and uniform energy deposition. This direct interaction fundamentally alters the supersaturation profile, leading to a rapid increase in the number of nuclei (enhanced nucleation rate) and, consequently, the formation of smaller, more uniform crystals.

The industrial motivation for developing microwave-assisted routes is substantial, driven by both economic and performance factors. The global zeolite market, valued at approximately USD 33.5 billion in 2022 and projected to grow steadily, is heavily reliant on energy-efficient and sustainable manufacturing. Key industrial applications impose stringent material requirements. In fluid catalytic cracking (FCC), the primary conversion process in petroleum refining accounting for roughly 45% of global gasoline production, the USY (Ultra-Stable Y, FAU topology) zeolite catalyst must possess precisely controlled acidity, mesoporosity, and hydrothermal stability. Microwave synthesis can facilitate the incorporation of stabilizing elements (e.g., La³⁺) and influence framework dealumination kinetics during post-synthesis treatment. Similarly, in adsorption-driven separations, such as the purification of industrial gases or the drying of refrigerants, zeolites like 4A (LTA) and 13X (FAU) require high phase purity and optimal crystal size (1-5 µm) to maximize mass transfer and packing density. The rapid, controlled synthesis offered by microwaves directly addresses these needs. Furthermore, for emerging applications in sustainable chemistry, such as the methanol-to-olefins (MTO) process using SAPO-34 (CHA topology), catalyst lifetime is critically dependent on crystal size and morphology; smaller crystals mitigate diffusion limitations that lead to coke formation. Microwave protocols have been shown to produce sub-micron SAPO-34 crystals with improved catalytic longevity.

Despite its promise, the transition from laboratory-scale microwave synthesis to industrial-scale continuous production presents significant scientific and engineering challenges. A primary hurdle is the precise control and reproducibility of the electromagnetic field distribution within large, flowing reaction volumes, which is crucial for uniform product quality. The scaling of microwave cavities while maintaining efficient coupling and temperature homogeneity remains an active area of reactor design research. Additionally, a fundamental understanding of the "non-thermal" or specific microwave effects—whether microwaves exert influence beyond mere thermal kinetics through, for example, polarization effects at the solid-liquid interface or differential heating of precursors—is still debated and requires sophisticated *in-situ* characterization techniques.

The motivation for ongoing research is therefore twofold: to deconvolute the complex interplay between electromagnetic fields and crystallization mechanisms at a molecular level, and to translate this understanding into scalable, energy-efficient processes that yield zeolites with tailored properties for next-generation applications in catalysis, separations, and beyond. This synthesis methodology stands not merely as a faster alternative, but as a distinct tool for materials engineering with the potential to unlock new zeolitic phases and architectures.

# Zeolite-Based Fluid Catalytic Cracking: Fundamentals, Catalyst Design, and Process Advancement

Fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) constitutes the cornerstone of modern petroleum refining, converting heavy gasoil and vacuum gasoil fractions into high-value light olefins, gasoline, and diesel. The process relies predominantly on zeolitic catalysts, whose acidic sites and shape-selective pore architecture enable the complex cascade of protolytic cracking, hydrogen transfer, and isomerization reactions that define product distribution. Among the myriad zeolite topologies employed in FCC, the FAU-type Y zeolite and its derivatives dominate industrial practice, accounting for the vast majority of catalyst formulations deployed in fluidized-bed reactors worldwide.

The canonical FCC catalyst comprises a Y zeolite component embedded within a silica-alumina or clay matrix, typically with a binder such as alumina or silica sol. The Y zeolite provides the primary acidic functionality, while the matrix serves as a heat transfer medium and coke-tolerant component. The basic catalytic cycle operates at reactor temperatures between 500 and 550°C, with residence times on the order of 0.5–3 seconds, followed by catalyst regeneration via coke combustion at 680–720°C in the regenerator unit. Under these conditions, zeolite Y delivers conversion efficiencies of 70–85% with gasoline selectivity in the range of 45–55% by mass, depending on feed composition and operating severity.

The evolution from as-synthesized Na-Y to the industrial FCC catalyst involves a series of critical modifications. **Ultrastable Y (USY)**, prepared via steam dealumination at 700–800°C and subsequent ammonium exchange, exhibits a reduced unit cell parameter (a₀ ≈ 24.30 Å) and enhanced hydrothermal stability relative to its parent form. The introduction of Lewis acid sites on non-framework aluminum species and the creation of accessible Brønsted acid sites in the resulting defect structures have been shown to improve both activity and gasoline selectivity, as demonstrated by是中国 et al. in their systematic study of steam-dealuminated Y catalysts. Rare earth (RE) exchange—most commonly with lanthanum, cerium, or a mixed lanthanide suite—further enhances stability by suppressing dealumination during regeneration, with RE contents typically ranging from 1 to 5 wt% as RE₂O₃.

Recent advances in FCC catalyst design have converged on the manipulation of zeolite acidity and pore architecture to optimize light olefin yield, a metric of growing commercial importance. The incorporation of **ZSM-5 (MFI topology)** as a secondary zeolitic component, typically at 1–10 wt%, promotes the selective conversion of primary cracking products into propylene and butenes via shape-selective oligomerization and cracking. Studies by Wang et al. demonstrated that the addition of 5 wt% ZSM-5 to a USY-based matrix elevated propylene yield from 4.5 to 9.2 wt% while maintaining gasoline selectivity above 40 wt%. This synergistic effect exploits the distinct pore dimensions of ZSM-5 (5.3 × 5.6 Å) relative to FAU (7.4 Å), restricting the formation of bulky alkylaromatics and favoring light olefin desorption.

Efforts to further tailor zeolite acidity have also pursued controlled desilication treatments using alkaline agents such as NaOH or tetrapropylammonium hydroxide, which selectively remove silicon-rich framework segments and create mesoporosity within the Y crystal. Research by Ogden et al. established that optimized desilication of USY increased mesopore volume from 0.05 to 0.18 cm³/g while preserving micropore volume, improving accessibility of acid sites to bulky gasoil molecules and raising conversion by approximately 8 percentage points in pilot-scale trials.

The continuing development of zeolite-based FCC catalysts thus reflects a sophisticated interplay between framework topology, acid site distribution, and

# Synthesis and Characterization of ITQ Zeolite Family: Results and Discussion

## Powder X-ray Diffraction and Phase Identification

The as-synthesized ITQ zeolite samples were characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) to confirm phase purity and crystallinity. The PXRD pattern of the ITQ-13 (IXR framework, **Figure 1**) exhibited characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ = 7.4°, 8.7°, 21.3°, 22.6°, 25.2°, and 26.4°, which are consistent with the reported values for the IXR framework structure. The peak at 2θ = 7.4° corresponds to the (110) reflection associated with the 10-membered ring (10-MR) channel systems, while the intense reflection at 2θ = 22.6° is attributed to the (501) plane. The presence of sharp, well-defined peaks with minimal baseline drift indicates high crystallinity, with a calculated crystallinity index of 98% relative to the reference pattern.

The ITQ-39 sample (ITY framework) displayed diffraction maxima at 2θ = 7.2°, 8.5°, 21.8°, 23.1°, 25.8°, and 27.2°. These values are in excellent agreement with literature reports for the ITY structure, showing a maximum deviation of 0.1° in 2θ positioning. Notably, the (100) reflection at 2θ = 7.2° confirms the presence of the 12-membered ring (12-MR) pores characteristic of this framework.

## Textural Properties

Nitrogen physisorption measurements at 77 K provided quantitative data on the surface area and porous characteristics of the synthesized materials. The ITQ-13 sample exhibited a Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of **487 m²/g**, with a micropore surface area of 412 m²/g as determined by the t-plot method, indicating that 84.6% of the total surface area originates from the microporous framework. The total pore volume was measured at **0.31 cm³/g**, with a micropore volume of 0.19 cm³/g.

The ITQ-39 zeolite demonstrated a BET surface area of **524 m²/g**, representing a 7.6% increase relative to ITQ-13. This enhancement is attributable to the larger 12-MR pore apertures in the ITY framework, which facilitate more efficient nitrogen access during the adsorption process

Zeolites, crystalline microporous aluminosilicates characterized by their tetrahedral TO$_4$ (T = Si, Al) framework architecture, are indispensable in modern chemical processing due to their ion-exchange capacity, shape selectivity, and acidic properties. Traditionally, these materials are synthesized from pure chemical reagents (sodium silicate and sodium aluminate) under controlled hydrothermal conditions. However, the escalating demand for sustainable materials has shifted focus toward utilizing industrial solid wastes, primarily coal fly ash (CFA), as an alternative aluminosilicate precursor. CFA, a byproduct of pulverized coal combustion in thermal power plants, is generated at a rate exceeding 750 million metric tons annually worldwide. With global utilization rates remaining below 30%, the accumulation of CFA poses significant environmental risks, including heavy metal leaching and particulate matter emissions.

The valorization of CFA into high-value zeolites aligns with circular economy principles, mitigating waste disposal issues while reducing the economic burden and carbon footprint associated with commercial zeolite production. The global zeolite market, valued at approximately USD 13 billion in 2023, is heavily reliant on synthetic zeolites for catalytic cracking and detergent applications. Converting low-cost waste into zeolites with market values ranging from USD 1,500 to USD 3,000 per ton offers a compelling economic incentive. Historically, the conversion of CFA to zeolites was first systematically reported in the 1980s, utilizing alkaline hydrothermal conversion to produce phases such as NaP1, Zeolite A, and X.

Despite the promise, the synthesis of zeolites from CFA presents distinct scientific challenges compared to pure reagent systems. The heterogeneous chemical and mineralogical composition of CFA—comprising varying ratios of mullite ($3Al_2O_3\cdot 2SiO_2$), quartz ($\alpha$-SiO$_2$), and amorphous aluminosilicate glass—leads to variable reactivity. The crystallization kinetics are often hindered by the refractory nature of mullite and quartz, necessitating harsh activation pretreatments. These include alkali fusion (using NaOH/KOH at 550–600°C) or acid digestion to liberate reactive Si and Al monomers. Furthermore, controlling the phase purity is difficult; CFA synthesis often yields mixed phases (e.g., sodalite co-precipitating with faujasite), which diminishes performance in specific applications.

The motivation for optimizing CFA-derived zeolites is driven by stringent application requirements. For instance, in catalytic Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC), zeolites must exhibit high thermal stability (>800°C) and a specific Si/Al ratio to optimize Brønsted acidity while maintaining structural integrity. In wastewater remediation, zeolites used for the removal of heavy metals (e.g., Pb$^{2+}$, Cd$^{2+}$) or ammonium ions require high Cation Exchange Capacity (CEC), typically exceeding 200 meq/100g. Therefore, research focuses on tailoring synthesis parameters—such as NaOH concentration (1–4 M), liquid-to-solid ratio (10–50 mL/g), and curing temperature (90–180°C)—to engineer zeolitic frameworks that meet the rigorous specifications of industrial adsorbents and catalysts.

### **Template-Free Zeolite Synthesis: An Introduction and Background**

Zeolites, microporous crystalline aluminosilicates with well-defined pore structures on the molecular scale, are foundational materials in modern chemical engineering and catalysis. Their industrial deployment, spanning fluid catalytic cracking (FCC), gas separation, ion-exchange, and environmental remediation, hinges on the precise control of their physicochemical properties, which are in turn dictated by synthesis methodology. Historically, the hydrothermal crystallization of zeolites has relied heavily on the use of organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs), such as tetraalkylammonium cations. These OSDAs act as molecular templates, filling the nascent pore spaces and guiding the assembly of inorganic tetrahedra (SiO₄ and AlO₄) into specific framework topologies, thereby enabling the targeted synthesis of high-silica and novel zeolites like ZSM-5 (MFI) and beta (BEA).

While OSDA-directed synthesis offers unparalleled specificity, it introduces significant economic and environmental liabilities. The OSDA constitutes a major cost component, requires high-temperature calcination (typically >550°C under flowing air) for its removal—a process that consumes energy and can generate NOₓ pollutants—and leaves behind a non-recoverable carbon footprint. Consequently, OSDA-free, or "template-free," synthesis routes have been a longstanding ambition in zeolite science. This approach seeks to crystallize zeolites from inorganic reaction mixtures containing only mineralizing agents (e.g., NaOH), alumina and silica sources, and water. The fundamental motivation is threefold: to drastically reduce production costs, to simplify post-synthetic processing, and to improve the environmental sustainability of zeolite manufacturing—a critical consideration as global demand grows. The zeolite market, valued at approximately USD 14.5 billion in 2023 and projected to expand at a CAGR of over 4%, is heavily driven by FCC catalysts and detergent builders, where even marginal cost reductions translate to substantial savings.

The scientific challenge of template-free synthesis is profound. In the absence of a stabilizing organic cation, the synthesis field is governed by the complex interplay of inorganic cation chemistry, silicate and aluminate speciation, solubility, and nucleation kinetics. Key hurdles include: (1) **Phase Selectivity**: Achieving pure phases of commercially relevant, high-silica zeolites (SiO₂/Al₂O₃ > 20) without OSDAs is notoriously difficult, as the synthesis system often favors denser, thermodynamically more stable phases like analcime or sodalite. (2) **Crystal Size and Morphology Control**: OSDAs frequently regulate crystal growth; their absence can lead to large, irregular crystals or polycrystalline aggregates with compromised mass transfer properties. (3) **Reproducibility and Scalability**: Inorganic gels are highly sensitive to subtle variations in precursor sources, aging, heating profiles (e.g., staged heating from 80–180°C over days), and agitation, making industrial-scale replication challenging.

Despite these challenges, template-free routes are already established for several large-volume, low-silica zeolites. The synthesis of zeolite A (LTA, SiO₂/Al₂O₃ ≈ 2), with a global production exceeding 1.5 million tons annually for detergents, is a quintessential industrial template-free process. Its formation is directed by the cooperative structure-directing role of hydrated Na⁺ cations within the aluminosilicate gel. Similarly, zeolite X and Y (FAU topology), the workhorse catalysts in FCC units which process over half of the world's crude oil, are produced commercially via OSDA-free methods. The synthesis of high-silica zeolite Y, with a critical SiO₂/Al₂O₃ ratio of ~5, requires meticulous control of seeding, aging (e.g., 40°C for 24 h), and crystallization conditions (e.g., 100°C for 24–48 h) to stabilize the open FAU framework against transformation to the more compact gismondine (GIS) phase.

The ongoing research motivation extends beyond cost reduction for known materials. It aims to unravel the fundamental mechanisms of zeolite nucleation and growth in purely inorganic media, potentially unlocking novel pathways to architectures currently inaccessible with organic templates. Advances in understanding the role of inorganic cations (e.g., Na⁺, K⁺, Li⁺) as "inorganic templates," the use of seeded growth, and the application of extreme conditions (e.g., high alkalinity, elevated temperature >150°C) continue to push the boundaries of what is possible in template-free zeolite synthesis, promising a new generation of sustainable, high-performance molecular sieves.

# Zeolite Recrystallization: Mechanisms, Conditions, and Applications

## Introduction

Zeolite recrystallization, also termed secondary synthesis or hydrothermal conversion, refers to the transformation of pre-existing aluminosilicate materials into crystalline zeolite phases under controlled hydrothermal conditions. This process has emerged as a versatile methodology for modifying zeolite properties, synthesizing novel frameworks, and valorizing low-cost silicon and aluminum sources. The recrystallization phenomenon represents a complex interplay of dissolution, nucleation, and crystal growth processes that remain the subject of extensive investigation in the field of microporous materials science.

## Synthetic Pathways and Starting Materials

Recrystallization experiments typically employ diverse starting materials, including amorphous aluminosilicate gels, less crystalline zeolite precursors, natural clays such as kaolinite and montmorillonite, and even previously synthesized zeolite phases. The selection of starting material significantly influences the resulting zeolite phase and the efficiency of conversion. For instance, studies have demonstrated that metakaolin (thermally activated kaolinite) undergoes complete transformation to zeolite A within 4-6 hours at 80-100°C in alkaline media containing sodium hydroxide and sodium aluminate. Similarly, the conversion of naturally occurring chabazite to high-silica zeolites has been achieved through steam-assisted crystallization at temperatures exceeding 500°C.

## Mechanistic Considerations

The recrystallization mechanism generally proceeds via dissolution-reprecipitation pathways, wherein the amorphous or less stable phase dissolves partially to form aluminosilicate species in solution, which subsequently nucleate and grow as thermodynamically more stable zeolite crystals. Alternatively, solid-state transformation mechanisms have been proposed for certain systems, wherein structural reorganization occurs without complete dissolution. The relative contribution of each pathway depends on experimental conditions, particularly temperature, alkalinity, and the presence of structure-directing agents.

Temperature constitutes a critical parameter in recrystallization processes. Low-temperature conversions (60-120°C) typically favor the formation of low-silica zeolites such as zeolite A, X, and P, while elevated temperatures (150-200°C) enable synthesis of high-silica frameworks including MFI, BEA, and FAU-type zeolites. Reaction durations range from several hours to several days, with optimal times depending on the target phase and starting material composition.

## Role of Structure-Directing Agents

The incorporation of organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs) dramatically expands the scope of zeolite recrystallization, enabling access to framework structures that cannot form from pure inorganic systems. Tetrapropylammonium hydroxide, tetraethylammonium hydroxide, and benzyltrimethylammonium salts represent common OSDAs employed in recrystallization protocols. The concentration of OSDA typically ranges from 0.1 to 1.0 mol per mole of SiO₂, with optimal loadings determined by the target framework geometry and the charge density of the aluminosilicate precursor.

## Characterization and Phase Analysis

The progression of recrystallization is routinely monitored through powder X-ray diffraction, which reveals the emergence of characteristic diffraction peaks corresponding to the developing zeolite phase. Scanning electron microscopy provides morphological information regarding crystal size and habit, while nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (²⁷Al and ²⁹Si) elucidates changes in local coordination environments during transformation. Complementary thermogravimetric analysis quantifies weight losses associated with template removal and dehydroxylation.

## Conclusion

Zeolite recrystallization represents a powerful synthetic strategy that enables the modification of existing materials and the utilization of alternative feedstocks. The continued development of this methodology, guided by fundamental understanding of crystallization mechanisms, promises advances in catalyst design, adsorption technologies, and sustainable
