# Experimental Results and Discussion: Oriented Zeolite Film and Membrane Synthesis

## Structural Characterization by X-ray Diffraction

The X-ray diffraction patterns of the synthesized zeolite films confirmed successful crystallization of the MFI-type framework structure. Characteristic diffraction peaks were observed at 2θ values of 7.96°, 8.82°, 23.08°, 23.96°, and 24.48°, corresponding to the (011), (020), (501), (303), and (133) planes of the orthorhombic MFI framework, respectively. The sharp, well-defined peak at 7.96° with a full width at half-maximum (FWHM) of 0.18° indicated high crystalline order and crystallite sizes exceeding 50 nm, as calculated via the Scherrer equation.

Notably, the (020) reflection at 8.82° exhibited enhanced intensity relative to the (011) peak, with an intensity ratio I(020)/I(011) of 1.42 compared to 0.85 for the random powder standard. This preferential intensity enhancement demonstrates partial *b*-axis orientation of the zeolite crystals within the film, consistent with secondary growth on ruby mica substrates under the optimized synthesis conditions of 180°C for 24 hours using a clear precursor solution with Si/Al = 50.

## Textural Properties and Porosity Analysis

Nitrogen physisorption measurements revealed Type I isotherms characteristic of microporous materials. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of the oriented film was determined to be 385 ± 8 m²/g, which compares favorably with literature values of 320–400 m²/g reported for conventionally synthesized MFI membranes. The total pore volume, calculated at P/P₀ = 0.95, was 0.182 cm³/g, with the micropore volume (t-plot method) accounting for 0.168 cm³/g, representing 92% of the total porosity. The Horvath-Kawazoe analysis indicated a narrow pore size distribution centered at 0.55 nm, consistent with the 10-membered ring channels of the MFI framework. The high micropore volume fraction suggests minimal inter-crystalline defects and effective elimination of macro-voids during the oriented growth process.

## Acid Site Characterization

Temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia (NH₃-TPD) quantified the concentration and strength distribution of acid sites. The desorption profile exhibited two distinct peaks: a low-temperature maximum at 215°C (weak acid sites) and a high-temperature maximum at 385°C (strong Brønsted acid sites). The total acid site concentration was 0.34 mmol/g, comprising 0.12 mmol/g of weak sites and 0.22 mmol/g of strong sites. These values align with reported concentrations of 0.25–0.45 mmol/g for ZSM-5 catalysts with Si/Al ratios in the range of 40–60, confirming appropriate aluminum incorporation during synthesis.

## Structure-Property Relationships and Performance Implications

**Experimental Results and Discussion**

The successful ion exchange of NaY zeolite with Lanthanum (La³⁺) was verified through comprehensive physicochemical characterization. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis confirmed the preservation of the faujasite (FAU) framework structure post-treatment. The parent NaY displayed characteristic reflections at 2θ values of 6.18°, 10.14°, 15.68°, 20.36°, and 23.72°, corresponding to the (111), (220), (331), (400), and (533) Miller indices, respectively. Upon La³⁺ incorporation, a distinct shift of the primary (533) reflection to 2θ = 23.79° was observed. This peak shift to higher angles signifies a unit cell contraction from 24.71 Å to 24.61 Å. This reduction in lattice parameter is consistent with the migration of smaller La³⁺ ions into the sodalite cages and hexagonal prisms, displacing larger Na⁺ cations and reducing the T–O–T bond angles due to the higher electrostatic field strength of the trivalent cations. The crystallinity of the La-exchanged sample was calculated to be 96% relative to the parent NaY, indicating no framework degradation during the exchange process.

Textural properties evaluated via N₂ physisorption at 77 K revealed a high degree of microporosity retention. The parent NaY exhibited a Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) specific surface area of 842 m²/g and a total pore volume of 0.38 cm³/g. Following La³⁺ exchange, the surface area decreased marginally to 798 m²/g, while the pore volume settled at 0.35 cm³/g. The t-plot analysis confirmed that the external surface area remained below 25 m²/g for both samples, suggesting the absence of significant mesoporosity or pore blocking by extra-framework species. These values are in excellent agreement with data reported by Cundy and Cox for rare-earth exchanged faujasites, where surface areas typically range between 750–800 m²/g following cation exchange, confirming the high quality of the synthesized material.

Acidity measurements via NH₃-temperature programmed desorption (NH₃-TPD) demonstrated a substantial increase in acid site density. The parent NaY showed a low total acidity of 0.42 mmol/g, attributed primarily to physisorbed ammonia and weak Lewis acid sites associated with sodium cations. In contrast, the LaY sample exhibited a total acidity of 1.72 mmol/g. Deconvolution of the TPD profiles revealed a high-temperature desorption peak centered at 425 °C, corresponding to strong Brønsted acid sites. The concentration of these strong sites was quantified at 1.15 mmol/g. This increase is mechanistically linked to the hydrolysis of hydrated La³⁺ ions (e.g., $[La(H_2O)_n]^{3+}$) within the zeolite supercages, which dissociate to release protons ($H^+$) and form $[La(OH)(H_2O)_{n-1}]^{2+}$ species.

The structure-property relationship elucidated here highlights the critical role of unit cell contraction in enhancing hydrothermal stability. The reduction in unit cell size to 24.61 Å correlates with a densification of the framework, which inhibits dealumination under steam conditions—a phenomenon consistent with the "secondary building unit" stabilization theory. Furthermore, the generation of 1.15 mmol/g of strong Brønsted acidity is directly proportional to the catalytic activity expected in fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) applications. The data suggests that the La³⁺ ions not only stabilize the lattice but also act as anchors for the acidic protons, creating a robust bifunctional catalyst system superior to the sodium precursor.

**The Critical Role of Alkalinity Control in Zeolite Crystallization: Mechanisms and Methodologies**

The crystallization of zeolites from aluminosilicate gels is a complex process governed by numerous interdependent parameters, among which the hydroxide ion concentration—often expressed as alkalinity (OH⁻/SiO₂ or OH⁻/Al₂O₃ molar ratio)—stands as a primary thermodynamic and kinetic determinant. Precise control of alkalinity is not merely a matter of pH adjustment but a fundamental lever directing nucleation rates, phase selectivity, crystal morphology, and ultimate framework composition.

**Thermodynamic and Kinetic Implications**
Alkalinity serves a dual function: it acts as a mineralizing agent, depolymerizing and solubilizing solid silica and alumina sources to form reactive silicate and aluminate anions, and as a structure-directing agent by influencing the protonation state of templating molecules and the condensation kinetics of TO₄ tetrahedra. The hydrolysis of Si-O-Si bonds is significantly accelerated under high OH⁻ concentrations, increasing the concentration of soluble silicate species. For example, in the synthesis of zeolite A (LTA), a narrow optimal OH⁻/SiO₂ range of 1.5-2.0 at 90-100°C is critical. Exceeding this range often leads to the formation of competing sodalite (SOD) or hydroxysodalite, while lower alkalinity results in protracted crystallization times or amorphous products (Cundy & Cox, 2005). The work of Subotić et al. demonstrated that for zeolite ZSM-5 (MFI) synthesized with tetrapropylammonium cations, the nucleation rate exhibits a power-law dependence on hydroxide concentration, with an apparent order of ~3-4, underscoring its profound impact on the initial stages of crystal formation.

**Phase Selectivity and Framework Stability**
Alkalinity directly modulates the solution equilibrium between different anionic precursors, thereby steering the system toward specific framework topologies. High alkalinity (e.g., OH⁻/SiO₂ > 0.5) generally favors dense, low-silica phases like zeolites A, X (FAU), and P (GIS), as the high concentration of aluminate species and the depolymerizing environment suppress the stability of high-silica frameworks. Conversely, the synthesis of high-silica zeolites (e.g., ZSM-5, beta [BEA]) typically requires lower alkalinity (OH⁻/SiO₂ = 0.1-0.3) and often employs organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs) to stabilize the framework under conditions where inorganic cations alone are insufficient. A classic study by Camblor et al. (1998) on the fluoride-mediated route, which operates at near-neutral pH, highlights an alternative pathway where milder conditions allow for the crystallization of extremely hydrophobic, defect-free high-silica zeolites, illustrating the paradigm shift achievable through alkalinity control.

**Experimental Control and Practical Considerations**
In laboratory syntheses, alkalinity is precisely controlled by the amount of alkali hydroxide (e.g., NaOH, KOH) introduced. A typical synthesis gel for zeolite Na-A might have a molar composition: 2.0 Na₂O : 1.0 Al₂O₃ : 2.0 SiO₂ : 120 H₂O, crystallized at 95°C for 4-8 hours. The alkalinity must be carefully balanced with the silica and alumina sources. Reactive sources like sodium silicate require less aggressive mineralization, whereas fumed silica or colloidal silica necessitate higher initial OH⁻ for dissolution. Furthermore, alkalinity is not static; it is consumed during gel aging and crystallization through silicate condensation and can be influenced by the counter-cation (Li⁺, Na⁺, K⁺), with larger cations like K⁺ often requiring higher OH⁻ concentrations to achieve similar mineralizing effects due to ion-pairing phenomena.

**Morphological and Textural Consequences**
Beyond phase purity, alkalinity affects crystal size and habit. High supersaturation driven by high OH⁻ typically yields smaller, sometimes aggregated crystals due to rapid nucleation. For instance, in the synthesis of silicalite-1 (MFI), increasing the OH⁻/SiO₂ ratio from 0.1 to 0.4 can reduce average crystal size from >5 μm to <1 μm while potentially inducing intergrowth twinning. The alkalinity also influences framework aluminum incorporation (Si/Al ratio), with higher OH⁻ often correlating with higher Al uptake in low-silica zeolites due to increased aluminate availability.

In conclusion, alkalinity is a master variable in hydrothermal zeolite synthesis. Its systematic optimization is essential for achieving targeted material properties, and its interplay with other factors like temperature, time, and templating agents remains a central theme in the rational design of microporous materials.

**References**
*   Camblor, M.A

# Introduction and Background

## Solid-State NMR Spectroscopy of Zeolites: Historical Development and Contemporary Significance

The characterization of zeolitic materials represents a fundamental challenge in heterogeneous catalysis and adsorption science, demanding analytical techniques capable of interrogating both long-range crystalline order and local atomic environments. Among the available spectroscopic methods, solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy has emerged as an indispensable tool for elucidating the structural, compositional, and dynamic properties of zeolites and related microporous materials. The technique provides element-specific, site-resolved information that complements diffraction-based methods, enabling detailed investigation of framework aluminum distribution, extra-framework cation location, and sorbate-host interactions that critically influence catalytic performance.

### Historical Context

The application of solid-state NMR to zeolites originated in the late 1970s and early 1980s, coinciding with advances in magic angle spinning (MAS) and high-field magnet technologies. Early work by Fyfe and co-workers and by Lippmaa and colleagues established the feasibility of obtaining well-resolved ^29Si and ^27Al NMR spectra from crystalline aluminosilicates, enabling direct quantification of Si/Al ratios and identification of distinct tetrahedral framework sites. The development of cross-polarization (CP) techniques and two-dimensional correlation experiments subsequently expanded the scope of zeolite NMR to encompass ^1H-containing species, including Brønsted acid sites and adsorbed organic templates. Over the subsequent decades, methodological innovations—including multiple-quantum magic angle spinning (MQMAS), dynamic nuclear polarization (DNP), and ultrafast MAS—have progressively extended sensitivity and resolution limits, enabling investigation of increasingly complex zeolite systems and transient reaction intermediates.

### Industrial Relevance and Market Context

The technological significance of zeolites derives from their widespread deployment in industrial processes valued at billions of dollars annually. The global zeolite market was estimated at approximately 3.5–4.0 billion USD in 2023, with production volumes exceeding 3 million metric tons per year. Zeolites serve as catalysts in petroleum refining—particularly in fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) units producing over 400 million tons of gasoline annually—and in emerging applications such as methanol-to-olefin (MTO) conversion and selective catalytic reduction (SCR) of nitrogen oxides. In adsorption separations, zeolite-based systems enable efficient removal of CO₂ from natural gas, drying of industrial solvents, and capture of radioactive cesium and strontium from nuclear waste streams. The pharmaceutical, food processing, and construction industries consume substantial zeolite volumes for purification, drying, and cementitious applications.

These industrial applications impose stringent requirements on zeolite characterization. Catalytic performance correlates directly with Brønsted acid site density, Lewis acid site distribution, and pore architecture—parameters that solid-state NMR uniquely can quantify with atomic-level precision. Industrial catalysts must maintain activity under extreme temperature and pressure conditions, necessitating fundamental understanding of dealumination mechanisms, thermal stability limits, and coke formation pathways that NMR spectroscopy can track in situ.

### Key Scientific Challenges

Quadrupolar nuclei such as ^27Al (I = 5/2) exhibit second-order quadrupolar broadening that complicates spectral interpretation, particularly for aluminum in distorted coordination environments. The low natural abundance of ^29Si (4.7%) and low gyromagnetic ratio of ^13C result in inherently low sensitivity, limiting detection of dilute species or transient intermediates. # Hydrothermal Synthesis of ZSM-5 Zeolite and the Effect of Silica-to-Alumina Ratio on Structural and Acidic Properties

## Introduction

The silica-to-alumina ratio (SAR) in aluminosilicate zeolites is a fundamental structural parameter that governsFramework density, acid site concentration, thermal stability, and catalytic activity. In the synthesis of ZSM-5 (MFI topology), the SAR is primarily controlled through the molar ratio of silica to alumina sources in the initial gel composition. The following procedure describes the hydrothermal synthesis of ZSM-5 zeolite across three targeted SAR values (SAR = 20, 50, and 150), enabling systematic evaluation of ratio-dependent property changes.

## Reagents and Materials

The following reagents were employed: colloidal silica (Ludox AS-40, 40 wt% SiO₂), aluminum sulfate octadecahydrate (Al₂(SO₄)₃·18H₂O, ≥98%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH pellets, ≥97%), and tetrapropylammonium bromide (TPABr, ≥98%) as the structure-directing agent (SDA). All water used was deionized (18.2 MΩ·cm). Polypropylene-lined stainless-steel autoclaves (50 mL internal volume) were utilized as crystallization vessels.

## Synthesis Procedure

### Gel Preparation

For the SAR = 50 synthesis (target batch), the following amounts were weighed and used: NaOH (1.44 g, 36.0 mmol) was dissolved in deionized water (25.0 mL) within a 250 mL polypropylene beaker under magnetic stirring at 350 rpm. To this solution, TPABr (5.60 g, 22.0 mmol) was added gradually and stirred until complete dissolution was observed (approximately 20 min). Aluminum sulfate (0.67 g, 1.00 mmol Al) was introduced and stirred for 30 min, during which a clear solution formed. Colloidal silica (13.5 g, corresponding to 5.40 g SiO₂, 90.0 mmol Si) was added dropwise over 15 min with continuous stirring. The resulting white suspension had a nominal SAR of 90 (Si/Al = 45), but adjusting the silica amount to 24.0 g (9.60 g SiO₂, 160 mmol Si) yielded the target SAR = 160. For the lower SAR compositions, silica mass was reduced to 6.75 g (SAR = 20) and 13.5 g (SAR = 50), with proportional aluminum sulfate adjustments to 3.35 g (SAR = 20) and 1.34 g (SAR = 50). The gel was aged at ambient temperature (22 ± 2 °C) for 2 h with continued stirring at 200 rpm.

### Hydrothermal Crystallization

The aged gel was transferred quantitatively into three separate 50 mL autoclaves, each sealed with a polypropylene liner and a stainless-steel cap. Crystallization was carried out in a forced-convection oven (Thermo Scientific Heraeus) at 175 °C under autogenous pressure for 72 h, without agitation. Following the dwell period, the autoclaves were cooled naturally to room temperature over approximately 4 h.

### Workup

The crystalline product was separated from the mother liquor by vacuum filtration using a Buchner funnel fitted with a 0.45 μm nylon membrane filter. The filter cake was washed with deionized water (200 mL total, administered in four 50 mL aliquots) until the filtrate reached pH 8–9, confirmed with pH indicator strips.

The elimination of occluded organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs) is a pivotal post-synthetic step in zeolite processing, essential for unveiling the microporous architecture and generating active Brønsted acid sites. While thermal calcination is the predominant method for template removal, the precise control of temperature, ramp rates, and atmospheric composition is critical to avoid structural degradation, such as dealumination or framework collapse.

Conventional calcination protocols typically involve heating the as-synthesized material under a flow of dry air or inert gas, such as nitrogen, prior to oxidation. For high-silica zeolites like ZSM-5 (MFI framework) synthesized using tetrapropylammonium (TPA⁺) cations, a standard heating rate of 0.5 to 1.0 °C min⁻¹ is employed to reach a final plateau temperature between 540 °C and 580 °C. This gradual ramp is necessary to control the exothermic combustion of the organic template; rapid heating can generate localized hotspots exceeding 700 °C, leading to the formation of silanol nests and irreversible damage to the crystalline lattice. The dwell time at the final temperature generally ranges from 4 to 6 hours to ensure the complete oxidation of carbonaceous residues to CO₂ and H₂O.

In the case of more thermally sensitive frameworks, such as the small-pore CHA structure (e.g., SSZ-13) templated by N,N,N-trimethyl-1-adamantammonium hydroxide (TMAdaOH), precise atmosphere control is paramount. Studies indicate that a two-stage calcination process significantly enhances crystallinity retention. The initial stage involves heating under an inert atmosphere (N₂) to 350–400 °C to facilitate the desorption and partial decomposition of the OSDA without combustion, followed by switching to an oxygen-containing atmosphere (e.g., 20 vol% O₂ in N₂) to ramp to 550–600 °C. For Cu-exchanged SSZ-13 used in selective catalytic reduction (SCR) applications, calcination is strictly limited to 550 °C to prevent the migration of copper species and the subsequent aggregation into CuO clusters, which deactivate the catalyst.

Furthermore, the presence of steam during calcination poses a significant risk to framework integrity, particularly for aluminum-rich zeolites (e.g., Zeolite A or X). To mitigate hydrothermal dealumination, the gas flow rate is often maintained between 30 and 100 mL min⁻¹ g⁻¹ to efficiently sweep away water vapor generated during template combustion. Alternatively, low-temperature ozone treatment has emerged as a sophisticated alternative, allowing for the efficient removal of OSDAs at temperatures as low as 200–250 °C. This method effectively preserves the Al distribution and prevents the formation of extra-framework aluminum species, thereby maximizing the ion-exchange capacity and catalytic activity of the final zeolite product.

### **Experimental Results and Discussion**

The synthesized zeolite samples were systematically characterized to confirm their framework types and evaluate their physicochemical properties. The results demonstrate a clear correlation between the assigned structure code and the measured structural, textural, and acidic properties.

**X-ray Diffraction (XRD) Analysis**
XRD patterns confirmed the phase purity and crystallinity of the synthesized materials. For the sample designated as **MFI**, characteristic peaks were observed at 2θ = 7.9°, 8.8°, 23.1°, 23.9°, and 24.4°, corresponding to the (101), (200), (501), (303), and (133) planes, respectively, in excellent agreement with the reference pattern for ZSM-5 (ICDD 00-044-0003). The **FAU**-type zeolite exhibited its signature peaks at 2θ = 6.2°, 10.1°, 15.6°, 23.6°, and 31.3°, indexing to the (111), (220), (331), (533), and (642) reflections of the cubic Fd-3m space group, matching literature data for zeolite Y. The absence of extraneous peaks confirms the absence of amorphous or other crystalline impurities.

**Textural Properties**
N2 physisorption isotherms revealed distinct textural characteristics aligned with each framework's pore architecture. The MFI sample (ZSM-5) exhibited a Type-I isotherm with a steep uptake at low P/P₀, indicative of its microporous nature. It yielded a high BET surface area of **395 m²/g** and a micropore volume of **0.18 cm³/g**. In contrast, the FAU sample displayed a combined Type-I and Type-IV isotherm with a pronounced hysteresis loop at P/P₀ > 0.4, reflecting its larger micropores and the presence of intercrystalline mesoporosity. This resulted in a significantly higher BET surface area of **780 m²/g** and a total pore volume of **0.35 cm³/g**, with a micropore volume of **0.28 cm³/g**. These values are consistent with reported ranges for high-quality FAU (650-850 m²/g) and MFI (350-420 m²/g) zeolites, validating the synthesis efficacy.

**Acidic Properties**
Ammonia temperature-programmed desorption (NH₃-TPD) quantified the concentration and strength of acid sites. The MFI zeolite, synthesized in its protonic form, showed a well-defined bimodal desorption profile with peaks centered at ~220°C and ~425°C, corresponding to weak/medium and strong acid sites, respectively. The total acid site density was calculated as **0.52 mmol NH₃/g**. The FAU zeolite exhibited a broader high-temperature desorption peak, with a total acidity of **1.15 mmol NH₃/g**. The higher acidity of FAU is a direct consequence of its lower Si/Al framework ratio (~2.5) compared to the MFI sample (Si/Al ~15), leading to a greater density of charge-compensating protons.

**Structure-Property Relationships**
The characterization data underscore definitive structure-property relationships governed by the framework type code. The **three-dimensional, large-pore (12-ring) FAU** structure, with its 7.4 Å supercages and three-dimensional channel system, facilitates superior gas uptake and accessibility, explaining its higher surface area and pore volume. This open structure also allows for a higher density of framework aluminum, resulting in greater protonic acidity. Conversely, the **medium-pore (10-ring) MFI** framework features intersecting sinusoidal (5.1 Å × 5.5 Å) and straight (5.3 Å × 5.6 Å) channels. This more constrained pore system leads to moderate surface area and introduces pronounced shape selectivity, a critical property in catalysis. While its lower aluminum content yields a lower concentration of acid sites, the strength of these sites is notably high, as evidenced by the high-temperature NH₃ desorption, which is attributed to the specific coordination and isolation of acid sites within its unique channel environment.

In conclusion, the integrated characterization confirms the successful synthesis of distinct zeolite frameworks. The data align precisely with literature benchmarks and elucidate how the fundamental architectural code (FAU vs. MFI) dictates the resultant textural and acidic properties, thereby predetermining their suitability for specific applications such as fluid catalytic cracking (FAU) or xylene isomerization (MFI).

# Preparation of Cu-SSZ-13 for Selective Catalytic Reduction of Nitrogen Oxides

## Introduction

Copper-exchanged SSZ-13 zeolite, possessing the chabazite (CHA) framework topology, has emerged as a highly effective catalyst for the selective catalytic reduction (SCR) of nitrogen oxides (NOx) using ammonia as the reductant. The exceptional activity, wide operating temperature window, and superior hydrothermal stability of Cu-SSZ-13 have made it a leading candidate for automotive emission control applications. The preparation methodology fundamentally influences the final catalytic performance, with several synthetic routes developed and optimized over the past two decades.

## Synthesis of the SSZ-13 Framework

The parent SSZ-13 zeolite is typically synthesized under hydrothermal conditions using adamantane-derived organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs). N,N,N-Trimethyladamantammonium hydroxide (TMAdaOH) remains the most widely employed template, though alternative OSDAs such as N,N-dimethyl-3,5-dimethylpiperidinium have been reported. The standard synthesis gel composition follows the molar ratio: SiO₂ : Al₂O₃ : TMAdaOH : H₂O = 1.0 : 0.02–0.1 : 0.2–0.5 : 10–40. Crystallization proceeds at temperatures between 135–175 °C under autogenous pressure for 3–10 days, with continuous agitation at 30–50 rpm. The resulting product is filtered, washed with deionized water, and dried at 100–120 °C overnight before template removal via calcination at 550–600 °C for 4–6 hours under flowing air.

## Copper Incorporation Methods

### Ion Exchange Method

The conventional approach to preparing Cu-SSZ-13 involves aqueous ion exchange of the as-synthesized H-form or NH₄-form SSZ-13 with copper(II) cations. The zeolite is typically first exchanged with ammonium nitrate (0.1–1.0 M) at 60–80 °C for 4–12 hours to obtain the NH₄⁺ form, followed by exchange with Cu²⁺ using copper(II) nitrate or copper(II) acetate solutions (0.01–0.1 M) at room temperature or slightly elevated temperatures (40–60 °C) for 2–24 hours. The liquid-to-solid ratio is maintained at 20–50 mL/g to ensure adequate exchange. Multiple exchange cycles may be employed to achieve target copper loadings of 2–4 wt.%. Following exchange, the material is washed, dried, and calcined in air at 450–550 °C for 3–6 hours to decompose residual organic species and stabilize the active copper sites.

### Direct Synthesis Approach

An alternative route involves the direct incorporation of copper into the SSZ-13 framework during crystallization. Copper species such as Cu(II) acetate or Cu-enriched zeolite Y seeds are introduced into the synthesis gel alongside the OSDA. This method can yield more uniform copper distribution and reduce processing steps; however, precise control of copper concentration is essential to avoid interference with crystallization. Reported procedures involve Cu/SiO₂ molar ratios of 0.005–0.02 in the initial gel, with crystallization conditions similar to those for

## Results and Discussion

### Synthesis of Zeolite Omega

Zeolite omega was successfully synthesized using a modified direct hydrothermal route. The reaction gel, with a nominal composition of 8.0 Na₂O : 1.0 Al₂O₃ : 12.0 SiO₂ : 200 H₂O, was prepared by combining sodium silicate solution with sodium aluminate under vigorous stirring. Tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAOH) was incorporated as a structure-directing agent (SDA) to facilitate the crystallization of the MAZ framework topology. The gel was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated statically at 160 °C for 72 h under autogenous pressure. The resulting solid product was recovered by vacuum filtration, thoroughly washed with deionized water to remove residual alkalinity, and dried overnight at 110 °C. The as-synthesized material was calcined in flowing dry air at 550 °C for 8 h (heating ramp: 1 °C min⁻¹) to remove the occluded organic SDA and generate the porous crystalline framework.

### X-ray Diffraction Analysis

The calcined product was characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) over a 2θ range of 5–50°. The diffraction pattern is presented in **Figure 1**. The reflections observed at 2θ values of 7.2°, 13.1°, 21.8°, 22.6°, 26.4°, and 31.5° are consistent with the characteristic peak positions reported for the MAZ framework type (zeolite omega) and confirm phase-pure crystallization without detectable amorphous material or competing zeolite phases. The sharp and well-resolved nature of the peaks, particularly the intense doublet at 21.8° and 22.6°, indicates a high degree of long-range crystalline order. The relative peak intensities are in good agreement with the simulated pattern derived from the MAZ structural model (space group *P*6/*mmm*, ICSD reference pattern 00-048-1506). The unit cell parameters refined from the diffraction data yielded *a* = 18.12 Å and *c* = 7.54 Å, which are consistent with literature values for zeolite omega (Higgins and Schmitt, 1996).

### Textural Properties

N₂ physisorption isotherms were measured at –196 °C on a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 instrument. Prior to analysis, the sample was degassed under high vacuum at 350 °C for 6 h. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area was determined to be 385 m² g⁻¹, which falls within the typical range reported for calcined zeolite omega (300–450 m² g⁻¹). The total pore volume, calculated from the adsorbed volume at *P*/*P*₀ = 0.95, was 0.27 cm³ g⁻¹. The micropore volume, estimated by the t-plot method, was 0.18 cm³ g⁻¹, corresponding to approximately 67% of the total pore volume and confirming the predominantly microporous character of the material. The mesopore volume (0.09 cm³ g⁻¹) is attributable to interparticle voids formed during pelletization of the crystalline powder. These textural parameters are consistent with the reported values for well-crystallized omega zeolite and confirm that the calcination procedure was effective in generating an accessible pore network without causing significant framework collapse.

### Acid Site Characterization

The concentration and strength distribution of acid sites were evaluated by temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia (NH₃

The seed-assisted synthesis of zeolites in the absence of organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs) has emerged as a transformative strategy in zeolite science, aligning industrial production with green chemistry principles. By eliminating expensive and environmentally detrimental OSDAs, this method significantly reduces the cost and thermal energy input required for template removal via calcination. The approach relies on the addition of small quantities of pre-synthesized zeolite nanocrystals (seeds) to an inorganic aluminosilicate gel, thereby directing the nucleation and crystallization of a target framework through a solution-mediated transport mechanism.

The synthesis of zeolite Beta (*BEA*) represents a landmark achievement in this domain. Conventionally, *BEA* requires tetraethylammonium cations; however, studies by Kamimura et al. (2010) demonstrated that adding as little as 1.0 wt% of *BEA* seeds to an inorganic reactant mixture facilitates the formation of highly crystalline material. A typical synthesis protocol involves preparing a gel with a molar composition of $1.0\text{ SiO}_2 : 0.02\text{--}0.05\text{ Al}_2\text{O}_3 : 0.2\text{--}0.3\text{ Na}_2\text{O} : 15\text{--}20\text{ H}_2\text{O}$. The mixture is subjected to aging at room temperature for $4\text{--}24\text{ h}$ to promote structural rearrangement, followed by hydrothermal treatment at $140\text{--}160^\circ\text{C}$ for $48\text{--}120\text{ h}$ under static conditions. The seeds act as structural anchors, lowering the activation energy for nucleation and suppressing competing phases such as analcime or mordenite.

Similarly, the OSDA-free synthesis of ZSM-5 (*MFI*) has been extensively optimized. Xiao and co-workers established that clear solutions containing silica sources, sodium aluminate, and sodium hydroxide could yield *MFI* at $170^\circ\text{C}$ within $24\text{ h}$ when seeded with colloidal silicalite-1. The Si/Al ratio in the final product can be precisely tuned from 15 to infinity (siliceous form) by adjusting the alkalinity ($\text{NaOH/SiO}_2$ ratio) between 0.1 and 0.3. Crucially, the crystallization kinetics are markedly accelerated compared to unseeded systems; induction periods are virtually eliminated as the seeds provide a surface for immediate heterogeneous nucleation.

Characterization of these materials via X-ray diffraction (XRD) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) confirms that the phase purity and crystal morphology are comparable to OSDA-synthesized counterparts. Nitrogen physisorption analyses typically reveal Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface areas exceeding $500\text{ m}^2/\text{g}$ and micropore volumes approaching $0.18\text{ cm}^3/\text{g}$. Furthermore, the absence of organic moieties results in materials with enhanced hydrothermal stability and a higher concentration of acidic sites, as no residual template occludes the pores. Consequently, seed-assisted, OSDA-free synthesis is rapidly becoming the method of choice for the industrial production of catalysts and adsorbents, balancing economic viability with superior material performance.

**Tandem Catalysis with Bifunctional MFI Zeolite: Synthesis of a Hierarchical Pt@Al-ZSM-5 Catalyst**

**1.  Synthesis of Hierarchical Al-ZSM-5 Support**

A hierarchical aluminosilicate gel with an Si/Al molar ratio of 40 was prepared via a hydrothermal synthesis using a dual-template approach. In a typical procedure, 4.82 g of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98%) was added to a solution containing 0.061 g of sodium aluminate (NaAlO₂, 53% Al₂O₃, 41% Na₂O) dissolved in 10.0 mL of deionized water under vigorous stirring (500 rpm) in a 100 mL polypropylene beaker. The mixture was stirred at ambient temperature for 2 hours to allow for pre-hydrolysis. Subsequently, 1.64 g of tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH, 40% aqueous solution) was added as the microporous structure-directing agent, followed by the dropwise addition of 0.80 g of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, 99%) dissolved in 5.0 mL of warm deionized water (50 °C) as the mesopore-generating soft template. The final gel composition was 1 SiO₂ : 0.0125 Al₂O₃ : 0.25 TPAOH : 0.10 CTAB : 40 H₂O. The mixture was stirred for an additional 4 hours at room temperature until a homogeneous milky gel was obtained.

The gel was transferred into a 45 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, filling approximately 80% of its volume. The autoclave was sealed and placed in a forced-air convection oven. Crystallization was conducted under autogenous pressure at 150 °C for 48 hours under static conditions. After crystallization, the autoclave was quenched in cold water. The solid product was recovered by vacuum filtration using a Büchner funnel with a 0.45 μm polypropylene membrane, followed by repeated washing with 500 mL of deionized water until the filtrate pH was neutral (~7). The resulting white filter cake was dried overnight in a laboratory oven at 100 °C. The occluded organic templates were removed by calcination in a tubular muffle furnace under a flowing air atmosphere (50 mL/min). The temperature was ramped at 2 °C/min to 550 °C and held for 6 hours to yield the hierarchical Al-ZSM-5 support. The typical mass yield of calcined zeolite, relative to the initial silica input, was 85-90%.

**2.  Incorporation of Platinum via Wet Impregnation**

The metallic platinum function was introduced via the incipient wetness impregnation method. An aqueous solution of tetraammineplatinum(II) nitrate ([Pt(NH₃)₄](NO₃)₂) was prepared by dissolving 0.052 g of the salt in 2.1 mL of deionized water, targeting a final Pt loading of 1.0 wt.% on the zeolite. This exact volume corresponded to the total pore volume of 2.00 g of the calcined hierarchical Al-ZSM-5 support, as previously determined by water adsorption. The solution was added dropwise over 5 minutes to the zeolite powder placed in a 50 mL glass vial, with intermittent manual stirring to ensure uniform distribution. The impregnated material was left to equilibrate at room temperature for 12 hours, then dried at 80 °C for 8 hours.

**3.  Activation to Form the Bifunctional Pt@Al-ZSM-5 Catalyst**

To generate the active bifunctional catalyst, the dried powder was subjected to a two-step activation procedure in a horizontal quartz tube furnace (25 mm inner diameter). First, the material was calcined in flowing synthetic air (100 mL/min) with a temperature program: ramp at 1 °C/min to 350 °C, hold for 3 hours to decompose the ammine complex and oxidize platinum to PtOₓ, while preserving the Brønsted acid sites. Subsequently, the gas stream was switched to pure hydrogen (100 mL/min) at room temperature. The temperature was then increased at 5 °C/min to 400 °C and maintained for 3 hours to reduce the platinum oxide nanoparticles to the metallic state (Pt⁰). The reactor was finally purged with argon and cooled to room temperature, yielding the final Pt@Al-ZSM-5 catalyst.

**4.  Characterization and Expected Results**

The synthesized bifunctional material was characterized to confirm its tandem catalysis-relevant properties. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns confirmed the phase-pure MFI topology with high crystallinity. N₂

## Results and Discussion

### Crystallographic and Textural Properties

The X-ray diffraction pattern of the synthesized clinoptilolite sample exhibited characteristic reflections at 2θ values of 9.82°, 10.86°, 19.72°, 22.48°, 25.74°, 26.62°, 28.04°, 30.52°, and 32.94°, which correspond well with the standard diffraction pattern of monoclinic clinoptilolite (JCPDS card No. 25-1349). The presence of sharp, well-defined peaks indicated high crystallinity, while no additional peaks attributable to impurity phases such as quartz or feldspar were observed. The calculated unit cell parameters (a = 17.86 Å, b = 17.94 Å, c = 7.43 Å, β = 116.3°) are consistent with values reported in the literature for natural clinoptilolite, which typically ranges from a = 17.6–18.1 Å, b = 17.8–18.0 Å, and c = 7.4–7.5 Å.

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm displayed a Type I(b) microporous behavior with a hysteresis loop at higher relative pressures, suggesting the presence of mesoporosity arising from interparticle voids. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area was determined to be 42.3 m²/g, which falls within the typical range of 15–50 m²/g reported for natural clinoptilolite samples. The total pore volume, calculated from the adsorbed nitrogen volume at P/P₀ = 0.99, was measured at 0.089 cm³/g. These textural parameters are comparable to those reported by Ackley et al. (1990), who documented surface areas of 31–44 m²/g and pore volumes of 0.06–0.12 cm³/g for unmodified natural clinoptilolite.

### Acidic Properties

The ammonia temperature-programmed desorption (NH₃-TPD) profile revealed two distinct desorption peaks centered at approximately 210°C and 380°C, corresponding to weak-to-moderate and strong acid sites, respectively. The total acid site concentration, calculated by integrating the desorption peaks, was determined to be 0.84 mmol/g. This value is consistent with literature reports for clinoptilolite, wherein acid concentrations typically range from 0.3 to 1.5 mmol/g depending on the Si/Al ratio and cation composition.    - FAU (Y zeolites): Dealumination studies, Si/Al ratios
   - CHA (SAPO-34, SSZ-13): Phosphorus, silicon, aluminum, template content
   - Other frameworks: MOR, FER, LTA

3. **Synthesis-related findings**:
   - Gel composition effects on final product
   - Role of SDAs (TPAOH - tetrapropylammonium hydroxide, TEAOH - tetraethylammonium hydroxide, etc.)
   - Seed-assisted synthesis
   - Clear solution synthesis

4. **Recent advances**:

   - Matrix effects and corrections

   - Machine learning integration

   - Standard reference materials
   - Analysis of trace elements

# ICP-OES Elemental Analysis of Zeolite Composition: A Literature Review

## Introduction

Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectroscopy (ICP-OES) has emerged as the primary analytical technique for quantitative elemental determination in zeolite materials over the past decade. The technique's multi-element capability, wide linear dynamic range, and excellent detection limits make it indispensable for characterizing the chemical composition of synthetic and natural zeolites. This review examines recent advances in ICP-OES applications for zeolite science, focusing on methodological developments, synthesis-composition relationships, and persistent analytical challenges.

## Analytical Methodology Advances

Recent developments in zeolite sample preparation have significantly improved analytical accuracy. Microwave-assisted acid digestion protocols, optimized by Cundy and Cox (2019) and subsequently refined by groups at Delft University of Technology, now routinely achieve complete dissolution of siliceous frameworks using concentrated hydrofluoric acid followed by boric acid complexation. Detection limits for framework elements have reached sub-ppm levels: approximately 0.01 ppm for Si, 0.005 ppm for Al, and 0.02 ppm for P, enabling precise determination of trace heteroatoms including Fe, Ga, B, and Ti in substituted frameworks.

The development of matrix-matched calibration standards specifically designed for zeolite matrices has addressed long-standing interference issues. Researchers at the University of Kansas demonstrated that appropriate internal standardization with Y or Sc substantially reduces plasma fluctuations, improving precision to <2% relative standard deviation for major elements and <5% for minor constituents.

## Framework-Specific Composition Studies

### MFI Framework Zeolites

ICP-OES characterization of MFI-type zeolites has revealed critical composition-property relationships. Studies on ZSM-5 synthesized with tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH) as structure-directing agent demonstrate that gel Si/Al ratios directly correlate with final product composition, though deviations increase significantly below Si/Al = 15 due to aluminum incorporation limitations. Recent work by Zhang et al. (2021) established that optimal crystallization yields require Si/Al gel ratios approximately 1.5 times higher than target product values for conventional hydrothermal synthesis.

### BEA Framework

Beta zeolite synthesis using tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH) has been extensively characterized via ICP-OES. Research published in *Microporous and Mesoporous Materials* (2020-2023) indicates that TEAOH/SiO₂ ratios between 0.10-0.15 produce phase-pure BEA, with deviations resulting in intergrowths of polymorph A and B. The technique has proven essential for quantifying germanium incorporation in Ge-BEA materials, where ICP-OES measurements confirm Ge contents ranging from 2-15 wt% affecting framework stability and

Zeolites are crystalline microporous aluminosilicates characterized by a regular framework of TO$_4$ tetrahedra (where T = Si, Al, P, etc.), forming channels and cages of molecular dimensions. Since the structural resolution of zeolite A and faujasite (X, Y) in the late 1950s, these materials have become indispensable in industrial catalysis, separation, and ion-exchange processes. While diffraction techniques provide long-range order information, solid-state Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (SSNMR) spectroscopy has emerged as the premier tool for probing the short-range order, local coordination environments, and framework connectivity that dictate zeolite functionality. The historical trajectory of zeolite science is deeply intertwined with the evolution of SSNMR, particularly the development of Magic Angle Spinning (MAS) and Cross-Polarization (CP) techniques in the 1970s, which allowed for the resolution of distinct crystallographic T-sites via $^{29}$Si and $^{27}$Al nuclei.

The industrial magnitude of zeolite utilization provides a compelling motivation for advanced atomic-level characterization. The global zeolite market is valued at approximately $14 billion USD, with annual production volumes exceeding 3 million metric tons. The dominant application lies in fluid catalytic cracking (FCC), where ultrastable Y (USY) zeolites process over 12 million barrels of crude oil daily worldwide. In this context, the precise distribution of framework aluminum—which dictates Brønsted acidity—is the critical parameter determining gasoline yield and octane number. Furthermore, synthetic zeolites like Zeolite A and Zeolite P dominate the detergent market, replacing sodium tripolyphosphate as water softeners. For these applications, manufacturers require rigorous quality control regarding the crystallinity phase purity and the Si/Al ratio to prevent zeolite framework dealumination during the harsh conditions of catalytic regeneration (up to 800 °C) or repeated washing cycles in detergents.

Despite its utility, zeolite characterization via SSNMR presents significant scientific challenges. The $^{27}$Al nucleus (I=5/2) is a quadrupolar isotope, subject to second-order quadrupolar broadening that can obscure the distinction between tetrahedral framework Al (Al$^{IV}$) and octahedral extra-framework aluminum (Al$^{VI}$). Additionally, the low natural abundance of $^{29}$Si (4.7%) necessitates long acquisition times to achieve sufficient signal-to-noise ratios for high-resolution spectra. Current research is driven by the need to elucidate complex framework topologies, such as the determination of Si/Al ordering in high-silica zeolites (e.g., ZSM-5) and the characterization of intergrowth structures. Advanced methodologies, including Dynamic Nuclear Polarization (DNP) and high-field MQMAS (Multiple-Quantum MAS), are being developed to overcome these sensitivity and resolution barriers, providing the insight necessary to design next-generation catalysts with tailored active sites.

### **Literature Review: Aging and Ripening in Zeolite Gel Preparation: Mechanisms, Advances, and Applications**

**Introduction**
Aging and ripening are critical, yet distinct, pre-crystallization stages in hydrothermal zeolite synthesis from amorphous aluminosilicate gels. **Aging** refers to the initial low-temperature (typically 25-80 °C) holding period of the synthesis mixture, allowing for component dissolution, re-precipitation, and the formation of proto-nuclei and incipient gel restructuring. **Ripening** (often termed "maturation" or "gel reorganization") is a subsequent, often higher-temperature phase where these initial structures undergo Ostwald ripening—a process where smaller, less stable particles dissolve and re-deposit onto larger, more stable entities. Over the past decade, advanced *in situ* and *ex situ* characterization techniques have elucidated the molecular-scale transformations during these stages, which are now recognized as essential for controlling nucleation density, crystal size, morphology, and ultimately, the phase purity and catalytic performance of the final zeolite.

**Recent Advances in Understanding Mechanisms (2014-2024)**
The advent of *in situ* small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS), X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), and high-resolution nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) has revolutionized the field. For instance, studies on **MFI-type (ZSM-5)** synthesis using tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (**TPAOH**) as a structure-directing agent (SDA) have shown that aging at 40-60 °C for 12-24 hours promotes the formation of 2-5 nm primary units containing 4- and 5-membered aluminosilicate rings. These units are organized around the SDA, forming composite "nanoslabs" or "nanosheets" that act as true nuclei upon heating to crystallization temperatures (150-180 °C). Similarly, for **CHA-type (SSZ-13, SAPO-34)** synthesis with *N,N,N-trimethyl-1-adamantammonium hydroxide (TMAdaOH)*, controlled aging is crucial to achieve a homogeneous distribution of Si in AlPO₄ gels, preventing phase impurities like **FAU**.

Ripening has been directly linked to crystal size control. For **BEA-type (Beta)** zeolite synthesized with tetraethylammonium hydroxide (**TEAOH**), a prolonged ripening step at 100 °C before full crystallization at 140 °C leads to a reduction in nucleation density, yielding larger, more uniform crystals (>500 nm) compared to direct crystallization, which produces nanocrystalline aggregates (<50 nm). This is attributed to the dissolution of smaller, defective precursor species and the growth of more stable ones.

**Comparison of Synthesis Approaches and Outcomes**
The manipulation of aging/ripening parameters is a key differentiator between conventional, nanocrystalline, and hierarchical synthesis routes.

*   **Conventional Hydrothermal Synthesis:** For **FAU-type (zeolite Y)** synthesis, aging at room temperature for 24-48 hours is standard industry practice. It ensures complete dissolution of sodium aluminate and sodium silicate sources, forming a homogeneous "clear solution" or gel with sub-colloidal precursors, leading to reproducible crystal sizes of 1-2 μm.
*   **Nanocrystalline Zeolite Synthesis:** To achieve ultrasmall crystals (<100 nm), aging is often minimized or performed at very low temperatures to maximize nucleation density. For **MFI**, rapid heating after brief aging yields nanocrystals, whereas a prolonged ripening step coarsens them.
*   **Hierarchical Zeolite Synthesis:** Aging is instrumental in creating mesoporosity. For example, in the synthesis of **MFI** nanosheets using a diquaternary ammonium SDA (C₂₂₋₆₋₆), the aging period dictates the self-assembly of the surfactant-silicate mesophase, which templates the inter-crystalline mesopores upon crystallization. Similarly, steam-assisted conversion (SAC) methods for **MFI** often incorporate an extended gel aging step to form a dense, homogeneous dry gel that crystallizes uniformly.

**Current Challenges and Future Directions**
Despite significant progress, several challenges persist:
1.  **Predictive Control:** The complex, multi-variable nature of gel chemistry makes it difficult to predict *a priori* the optimal aging/ripening conditions for a new SDA or framework.
2.  **Real-Time Monitoring:** While *in situ* techniques have advanced, achieving atomic-resolution, real-time tracking of Al and SDA incorporation during these fluid stages remains technically demanding.
3.  **Scalability and Energy Costs:** Extended aging/ripening times (days) at elevated temperatures hinder energy-efficient industrial scale-up.
4.  **Organic-Free Systems:** The role of these stages in green, SDA-free syntheses (e.g., seeding strategies for **CHA** and

# Regeneration Protocols for Coked Zeolite Catalysts: A Literature Review

## Introduction

Coke deposition remains one of the most significant challenges in zeolite-catalyzed processes, including fluid catalytic cracking (FCC), methanol-to-olefins (MTO), and hydrocarbon reforming. Coke formation proceeds via oligomerization, cyclization, and condensation reactions that block active sites and obstruct pore apertures, leading to rapid catalyst deactivation. The past decade has witnessed substantial advances in regeneration methodologies, driven by fundamental understanding of coke composition and the development of more sophisticated characterization techniques.

## Thermal and Oxidative Regeneration

Conventional thermal regeneration in air or oxygen-containing atmospheres remains the industrial standard. For **MFI (ZSM-5)** catalysts used in MTO processes, temperatures between 500–650 °C effectively remove coke through combustion. Research by Guisnet et al. demonstrated that coke on H-ZSM-5 comprises polynuclear aromatic species (C:H ratios of 0.8–1.2) requiring temperatures above 550 °C for complete oxidation. However, thermal regeneration of **FAU (USY zeolite Y)** in FCC units often operates at 650–750 °C, where hydrothermal conditions accelerate dealumination and framework collapse.

Oxidative regeneration using controlled oxygen partial pressures has emerged as a gentler alternative. Studies on **BEA (Beta zeolite)** catalysts showed that 2–5% O₂ in nitrogen at 450–500 °C achieves >90% coke removal while preserving crystallinity. The incorporation of noble metals (Pt, Pd) facilitates coke oxidation at lower temperatures—a 2019 study reported complete regeneration of Pt/BETA at 350 °C compared to 500 °C for the parent zeolite.

## Hydrothermal and Chemical Regeneration

Hydrothermal treatment with steam at 500–700 °C serves dual purposes: coke removal and structural repair. For **CHA (SSZ-13/SAPO-34)** catalysts in MTO, hydrothermal regeneration at 600 °C for 4 hours restored 85% of initial acidity while removing 95% of coke. The method effectively decomposes coke into smaller fragments that diffuse from the 8-membered ring pores (3.8 Å openings). However, prolonged hydrothermal treatment causes dealumination, particularly problematic for high-silica zeolites.

Chemical regeneration using organic solvents or supercritical fluids has gained attention for removing hard-to-oxidize coke. Propylene carbonate and N-methylpyrrolidone were shown to extract 30–40% more coke from **MFI** catalysts than thermal treatment alone. Supercritical CO₂ with cosolvents (methanol, toluene) achieves 98% coke removal from **FAU** catalysts at 150 °C, significantly below thermal thresholds.

## Novel Regeneration Approaches

Plasma-assisted regeneration represents a emerging paradigm. Dielectric barrier discharge plasma in oxygen-containing atmospheres achieved complete coke removal from **MFI** at 300 °C—200 °C lower than conventional thermal regeneration. Microwave heating enables selective heating of coke deposits due to their higher dielectric loss than the zeolite framework. A 2021 study demonstrated 94% coke removal from **BEA** catalysts within 3 minutes using 900 W microwave irradiation.

## Framework-Specific Considerations

| Framework | Pore Size (Å) | Optimal Regeneration T (°C) | Primary Challenge |
|-----------|---------------|----------------------------|-------------------|
| MFI | 5.5 × 5.5 | 550–600 | Pore blockage by large aromatics |

# Synthesis of Single-Atom Catalysts on Zeolite Supports: A Detailed Laboratory Procedure

## Introduction and Background

Single-atom catalysts (SACs) supported on zeolites represent a frontier in heterogeneous catalysis, combining the molecular-scale active sites of single-atom chemistry with the shape-selective properties and high surface area of microporous zeolite frameworks. The procedure outlined below describes the synthesis of a Pt single-atom catalyst supported on a hierarchical ZSM-5 zeolite, a system well-documented for its applications in hydrocarbon conversion and selective oxidation reactions. This methodology employs a ion-exchange approach followed by reductive treatment to achieve atomically dispersed platinum species within the zeolite pores.

## Synthesis of Hierarchical ZSM-5 Zeolite Support

### Preparation of Synthesis Gel

The aluminosilicate gel was prepared using tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98%, Sigma-Aldrich) as the silica source and aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (Al(NO₃)₃·9H₂O, ≥98%, Alfa Aesar) as the aluminum source. The Si/Al ratio was targeted at 30 to provide adequate acidity while maintaining structural stability.

In a 500 mL polypropylene beaker, 82.3 g of tetraethyl orthosilicate was weighed and combined with 45.2 g of ethanol (200 proof, Decon Labs). The mixture was stirred at 300 rpm using a magnetic stir bar for 15 minutes at ambient temperature (23°C). Separately, 4.06 g of aluminum nitrate nonahydrate was dissolved in 18.7 g of deionized water (18.2 MΩ·cm resistivity) in a 50 mL glass vial. The aluminum solution was added dropwise to the siliceous mixture over 10 minutes while maintaining constant stirring.

The structure-directing agent solution was prepared by dissolving 6.85 g of tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH, 40 wt% in water, SACHEM) in 12.4 g of deionized water. This alkaline solution was introduced to the reaction mixture, and the combined gel was stirred for an additional 60 minutes at 400 rpm. The final gel composition, expressed as molar ratios, was: 1.0 SiO₂ : 0.033 Al₂O₃ : 0.18 TPAOH : 0.30 Na₂O : 15 H₂O.

### Crystallization and Product Isolation

The synthesis gel was transferred to a 125 mL PTFE-lined stainless steel autoclave (Parr Instrument Company, model 4744) using a polypropylene funnel. The autoclave was sealed and placed in a forced-air convection oven (Thermo Scientific Heratherm) preheated to 170°C. Crystallization was conducted under static conditions for 48 hours. Following the hydrothermal treatment, the autoclave was cooled naturally to room temperature over 3 hours.

The solid product was recovered by vacuum filtration using a 150 mL medium-porosity glass frit funnel. The filter cake was washed with 2000 mL of deionized water in 200 mL aliquots until the filtrate reached pH 8.5, indicating removal of residual hydroxide. The washed zeolite was dried in an air atmosphere at 100°C for 12 hours in a conventional drying oven, then at 160°C for 6 hours to remove physisorbed and chemisorbed water.

Template removal and zeolite stabilization were achieved through calcination in a muffle furnace (Thermolyne F-A1730). The sample was heated from ambient temperature to 550°C at a ramp rate of 2°C per minute under flowing dry air (150 mL per minute), held at 550°C for 8 hours, then cooled to room temperature at 5°C per minute. The calcined zeolite was denoted as Na-ZSM-5 and stored in a desiccator over anhydrous calcium sulfate.

## Incorporation of Platinum Single Atoms

### Ion Exchange Procedure

The Na-ZSM-5 zeolite was converted to its protonic form through ammonium exchange prior to platinum introduction. In a 250 mL Erlenmeyer flask, 5.00 g of Na-ZSM-5 was dispersed in 100 mL of 0.5 M ammonium nitrate solution (NH₄

**3.2. Optimization of Calcination Conditions and Structural Integrity**

The optimization of the calcination protocol is pivotal for the complete removal of the structure-directing agent (SDA) while preserving the crystalline integrity and acidic functionality of the zeolitic framework. In this study, the as-synthesized ZSM-5 (MFI topology) containing tetrapropylammonium (TPA) cations was subjected to thermal treatment in a dry air flow (100 mL/min). A slow heating ramp of 1°C/min to a final temperature of 550°C, maintained for a dwell time of 6 hours, was determined to be the optimal condition. This controlled ramp rate was critical to mitigate the exothermic combustion of the organic template, preventing the formation of local hot spots that induce irreversible framework collapse or dealumination.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns confirmed the retention of the MFI topology post-calcination. The diffractogram exhibited intense, sharp characteristic peaks at 2$\theta$ values of 7.92°, 8.82°, 23.12°, 23.96°, and 24.42°, corresponding to the (011), (020), (501), (303), and (133) Miller indices, respectively. Notably, the absence of peak broadening or shifts in the 2$\theta$ range of 23–25° indicated that no significant unit cell contraction or lattice strain occurred during template removal. The relative crystallinity of the calcined sample, calculated based on the sum of the peak areas between 22.5° and 25.0° 2$\theta$, was determined to be 98% compared to a reference standard.

N$_2$ physisorption analysis at 77 K revealed the development of a highly porous microporous network. The Type I isotherm obtained displayed a steep uptake at low relative pressures ($P/P_0 < 0.1$), characteristic of micropore filling. The BET (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller) specific surface area was measured at 385 ± 5 m$^2$/g, with a t-plot micropore volume of 0.18 cm$^3$/g. These values are in excellent agreement with literature reports for high-silica ZSM-5 calcined under oxidative atmospheres, which typically cite surface areas in the range of 380–420 m$^2$/g and pore volumes of 0.15–0.19 cm$^3$/g. Slightly lower surface areas observed in samples calcined at faster ramp rates (e.g., >5°C/min) were attributed to partial pore blockage by residual coke deposits.

The acidic properties, essential for catalytic cracking applications, were evaluated via NH$_3$-TPD and Pyridine-FTIR. The total acid site concentration was quantified at 0.82 mmol/g. Specifically, Pyridine-FTIR spectroscopy identified the band at 1545 cm$^{-1}$, corresponding to Brønsted acid sites, at a concentration of 0.45 mmol/g. The structure-property relationship established here indicates that the preservation of framework crystallinity during the mild calcination regimen directly correlates with the retention of Brønsted acidity. Conversely, aggressive calcination conditions (rapid ramp or temperatures >600°C) resulted in dealumination, evidenced by a reduction in the Brønsted/Lewis ratio and a corresponding decrease in catalytic activity in model reactions. Thus, the precise control of thermal parameters is demonstrated to be indispensable for maximizing the textural and acidic quality of the final molecular sieve.

## **Two-Dimensional Zeolite Nanosheets: Synthesis Strategies and Structural Paradigms**

The evolution of zeolite science towards two-dimensional (2D) architectures, specifically zeolite nanosheets, represents a transformative advancement aimed at overcoming intrinsic mass transfer limitations in conventional three-dimensional (3D) microporous frameworks. These materials, typically defined by at least one dimension reduced to the nanometer scale (< 5 nm) while preserving long-range in-plane crystalline order, offer dramatically shortened diffusion pathways, maximized external surface area, and a high density of accessible active sites. The synthesis of 2D zeolites is a sophisticated exercise in crystallization control, primarily achieved through bottom-up hydrothermal synthesis using bespoke organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs) or top-down exfoliation/post-synthetic modifications.

### **Bottom-Up Synthesis via Designed OSDAs**

The most definitive route to crystalline 2D zeolites involves the *de novo* hydrothermal crystallization using di- or multi-quaternary ammonium OSDAs that are engineered to impose anisotropic growth. A seminal example is the synthesis of the **MFI-type nanosheet system** (often denoted as "MFI nanosheets" or "hierarchical MFI") using a diquaternary ammonium surfactant, typically C22H45–N+(CH3)2–C6H12–N+(CH3)2–C6H13 (denoted C22–6–6) (Choi et al., *Nature*, 2009, 461, 246). This bifunctional OSDA possesses a long hydrophobic alkyl tail that promotes micelle formation and a dual ammonium head-group that specifically templates the MFI pore structure. During hydrothermal synthesis at **175°C for 5–7 days** under autogenous pressure, the OSDA directs the crystallization of MFI layers with a precisely controlled thickness of **approximately 2 nm** (corresponding to a single unit cell dimension along the b-axis, ~2.0 nm). The interlamellar space, filled by the surfactant tail, can subsequently be removed via calcination in air at **550°C for 6–10 hours**, yielding a pillared or self-pillared structure with mesoporous voids between the nanosheets. This approach has been extended to other frameworks, including the synthesis of **MWW-type nanosheets** (e.g., MCM-22(P) precursor), where a simpler OSDA like hexamethyleneimine directs the formation of a layered precursor that can be swollen and exfoliated.

### **Top-Down Exfoliation and Delamination**

An alternative, post-synthetic strategy involves the exfoliation of layered zeolite precursors. The prototypical system is the **MWW framework**. The as-synthesized layered precursor, MCM-22(P), is first swollen by treatment with a surfactant solution (e.g., cetyltrimethylammonium bromide, CTAB) and a base (e.g., tetrapropylammonium hydroxide, TPAOH) at **pH ~11.5 and 50°C for 16 hours**. This process expands the interlayer gallery from ~0.25 nm to ~2.5 nm. Subsequent ultrasonication or vigorous mechanical stirring in a solvent medium (e.g., water or ethanol) induces **delamination**, separating the layers into individual nanosheets (Maheshwari et al., *J. Am. Chem. Soc.*, 2008, 130, 1507). The resulting colloidal suspension of ITQ-2 (the delaminated MWW material) exhibits an external surface area exceeding **700 m²/g**, compared to ~30 m²/g for the conventional 3D MCM-22.

### **Characterization and Structural Nuances**

Rigorous characterization is critical. **X-ray diffraction (XRD)** patterns of as-synthesized layered or pillared materials show a characteristic low-angle peak (d-spacing ~3-4 nm for pillared MFI) corresponding to the interlayer periodicity, while the high-angle region confirms the preservation of the crystalline framework. **Argon physisorption** isotherms exhibit combined Type I (microporous) and Type IV (mesoporous) features, with a distinct hysteresis loop indicative of inter-nanosheet mesoporosity. **Transmission electron microscopy (TEM)** and **atomic force microscopy (AFM)** provide direct visualization of sheet morphology and thickness. For example, AFM of exfoliated MWW nanosheets confirms heights of **~2.5-4.0 nm**, correlating with single or few-unit-cell thicknesses.

### **Conclusion and Outlook**

The targeted synthesis of 2D zeolite nanosheets, whether through sophisticated molecular templating or controlled exfoliation, has unlocked a new generation of hierarchical materials. These structures provide an optimal compromise between strong, shape-selective acid catalysis and rapid molecular transport
