# Introduction and Background

## Post-Synthetic Modification of Zeolites: Historical Context and Industrial Significance

Zeolites are microporous, crystalline aluminosilicates belonging to the tectosilicate family, characterized by frameworks comprising TO₄ tetrahedra (where T = Si, Al) arranged in well-defined pore systems with aperture dimensions typically ranging from 3 to 10 Å. Since their first industrial deployment in the 1950s as molecular sieves for adsorption processes, zeolites have become indispensable materials across the chemical, petrochemical, and refining industries. The unique combination of acidic properties, shape-selective character, and ion-exchange capacity has established zeolites as heterogeneous catalysts of paramount importance, with the fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) process representing the largest single application, consuming over 50,000 metric tons annually worldwide.

**Post-synthetic modification** encompasses a diverse array of chemical treatments applied to as-synthesized zeolites to alter their physicochemical properties without destroying the crystalline framework. These transformations include: (1) ion-exchange of extra-framework cations (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, rare earth ions) to tune acidity and selectivity; (2) dealumination via acid leaching or steam calcination to adjust Si/Al ratios and create mesoporosity; (3) framework substitution involving isomorphous replacement of Al³⁺ with Fe³⁺, Ga³⁺, or B³⁺; (4) phosphorus and boron modification to enhance thermal stability; and (5) calcination protocols to remove organic structure-directing agents from the pore volume.

The rationale for post-synthetic modification stems from limitations inherent to direct synthesis. While hydrothermal conversion during zeolite synthesis allows some control over framework composition and Si/Al ratio, the range of achievable compositions remains constrained by thermodynamic and kinetic factors. For instance, the synthesis of high-silica zeolites with Si/Al ratios exceeding 100 often proves synthetically challenging, yet such materials exhibit superior hydrothermal stability required for harsh industrial environments. Similarly, direct synthesis rarely produces zeolites with optimal acid site distributions for specific catalytic transformations. Post-synthetic dealumination of Y-type zeolites (FAU framework) to produce ultrastable forms exemplifies this approach, achieving Si/Al ratios of 50-200 while maintaining crystallinity—a milestone that revolutionized fluid catalytic cracking catalysts and enabled operations at elevated temperatures and regenerator temperatures exceeding 700°C.

The global zeolite market, valued at approximately USD 7.8 billion in 2023 with projected compound annual growth rates of 6-8% through 2030, reflects the critical role of these materials across diverse sectors. Beyond catalysis, zeolites serve essential functions in adsorption for gas purification (CO₂ capture, VOC removal), detergent builders for water softening, and agricultural applications as soil amendments. The petroleum refining industry alone accounts for approximately 40% of total zeolite consumption, with hydrocracking, alkylation, and isomerization processes relying on specifically modified zeolite catalysts.

Despite remarkable advances, significant challenges persist in post-synthetic zeolite chemistry. Controlling the spatial distribution of acid sites within the framework remains difficult, as conventional ion-exchange procedures produce heterogeneous acid site arrangements. The creation of hierarchical pore architectures without compromising microporosity and shape selectivity presents an ongoing synthetic challenge. Furthermore, the mechanistic understanding of modification processes at the molecular level—in particular, the pathways governing dealumination and mesopore formation—requires elucidation to enable rational catalyst design. These knowledge gaps motivate continued fundamental research into post-synthetic modification strategies, with particular emphasis on developing methodologies that provide precise control over acid site strength, distribution, and environment.

The convergence of industrial demand for improved catalyst performance—particularly in terms of selectivity, lifetime, and tolerance to feedstream contaminants—with advances in characterization techniques and computational modeling has created unprecedented opportunities for rational design of modified

# Experimental Results and Discussion: Automated Robotic Synthesis and Characterization of Zeolite ZSM-5

## Results

The automated robotic synthesis platform successfully produced phase-pure ZSM-5 zeolite (MFI topology) with high reproducibility across 24 parallel reactions. X-ray diffraction analysis confirmed the formation of the characteristic MFI framework structure, with all observed reflections matching the reference pattern (Figure 1). The diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 7.96°, 8.82°, 23.18°, 23.98°, and 24.48° correspond to the (101), (200), (501), (303), and (313) lattice planes, respectively, in excellent agreement with the framework structure database (JCPDS Card No. 42-0024). No characteristic peaks corresponding to crystalline impurities such as quartz (2θ = 20.86°, 26.64°) or mullite were detected, indicating complete phase selectivity under the optimized robotic synthesis conditions.

Nitrogen physisorption measurements revealed that the robotically synthesized ZSM-5 exhibited a BET surface area of 387 ± 12 m²/g, with an external surface area of 52 m²/g determined by the t-plot method. The micropore surface area was calculated as 335 m²/g, representing 86.6% of the total surface area. The Horvath-Kawazoe analysis indicated a narrow pore size distribution centered at 5.53 Å, consistent with the 10-membered ring channels characteristic of the MFI framework. The total pore volume was determined to be 0.263 cm³/g at P/P₀ = 0.99, with a micropore volume of 0.168 cm³/g as calculated using the t-plot method. These textural properties demonstrate that the automated synthesis protocol produces zeolite with textural parameters comparable to conventionally synthesized materials.

Temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia (NH₃-TPD) was employed to quantify the acid site population and strength distribution. The desorption profile exhibited two distinct peaks: a low-temperature maximum at 218°C corresponding to weak acid sites (0.34 mmol/g) and a high-temperature maximum at 398°C attributed to strong Brønsted acid sites (0.58 mmol/g). The total acid site concentration was calculated as 0

Offretite (OFF) and erionite (ERI) are structurally related small-pore zeolites belonging to the ABC-6 family, characterized by the stacking of hexagonal prisms (can units) and 6-membered rings. The distinction between the two topologies arises from the stacking sequence of these layers along the c-axis: OFF follows an AABB sequence, whereas ERI exhibits an ABC sequence. A critical structural consequence of this relationship is the propensity for intergrowth formation, where random stacking faults result in a mixed-phase material often referred to as OFF/ERI intergrowths. These faults physically block the 12-membered ring channels inherent to the OFF framework, effectively converting the pore system into the smaller 8-membered ring apertures typical of erionite, thereby significantly altering diffusional properties.

The hydrothermal synthesis of these materials is highly sensitive to the chemical composition of the reaction gel, particularly the Si/Al ratio and the nature of the inorganic and organic cations present. Classical synthesis routes, established by Barrer and Kerr, utilize aluminosilicate gels with high potassium concentrations, as $K^+$ ions act as structure-directing agents (SDAs) for the OFF framework. A typical synthesis involves a gel molar composition of $3.3 K_2O : 1.0 Na_2O : 1.0 Al_2O_3 : 10 SiO_2 : 120 H_2O$, crystallized under static conditions at temperatures ranging from 90°C to 120°C for durations of 48 to 120 hours. Lower synthesis temperatures generally favor OFF crystallization; however, the product is rarely phase-pure and usually contains 10 to 30% erionite intergrowths.

Conversely, the synthesis of phase-pure erionite is notably difficult without the use of organic SDAs. The introduction of tetramethylammonium (TMA) cations or choline into the synthesis gel favors the formation of the ERI topology. Studies indicate that a gel composition of $4.5 (TMA)_2O : 0.5 K_2O : 1.0 Al_2O_3 : 10 SiO_2 : 200 H_2O$, hydrothermally treated at 100°C for approximately 7 days, yields erionite with minimized stacking faults. The role of TMA is to stabilize the smaller cages and the specific ABC stacking sequence required for ERI. Furthermore, the Si/Al ratio influences the cation distribution; lower ratios (Si/Al < 3.5) promote higher $K^+$ occupancy in the cancrinite cages, which stabilizes the OFF structure, whereas higher silica content often increases the likelihood of intergrowths due to the reduced charge density and weaker electrostatic directionality.

Post-synthesis modification is frequently employed to tailor the acidity and porosity of these intergrown materials. Mild steaming or acid leaching, typically using 0.1 to 0.5 M hydrochloric or nitric acid at temperatures between 60°C and 80°C, serves to remove extra-framework aluminum and alkali metal cations. For example, dealumination of OFF/ERI intergrowths has been shown to create secondary mesoporosity while preserving the microporous framework, enhancing catalytic activity for reactions such as the selective catalytic reduction (SCR) of $NO_x$ or the isomerization of light paraffins. Advanced characterization techniques, particularly high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and solid-state NMR, are essential for quantifying the degree of intergrowth, revealing that even samples identified as pure offretite by X-ray diffraction often contain nanoscale erionite interlayers that critically impact molecular transport.

### **Machine Learning in Zeolite Discovery and Synthesis: A Paradigm Shift in Microporous Materials Engineering**

The discovery and targeted synthesis of zeolites and zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) represent a central challenge in materials chemistry, driven by their paramount importance in catalysis, separation, and ion exchange. Traditional methods, reliant on empirical trial-and-error and heuristic knowledge of hydrothermal chemistry, are inherently slow and resource-intensive. The integration of machine learning (ML) into this domain is catalyzing a paradigm shift, transitioning from serendipitous discovery to data-driven, predictive design of microporous materials with tailored properties.

**Predictive Discovery and Property Optimization**
ML models, particularly supervised learning algorithms like gradient-boosted trees and graph neural networks (GNNs), are now routinely employed to predict zeolite properties from structural descriptors. For instance, GNNs trained on databases such as the International Zeolite Association (IZA) structure collection and hypothetical zeolite databases can accurately predict key characteristics, including framework density (FD), theoretical surface area, and **Heckman diffusion barriers**, with R² values exceeding 0.95. A seminal study by Goldsmith et al. (*Chem. Mater.*, 2018, 30, 15) demonstrated that a random forest model could screen over 2.6 million hypothetical zeolite structures for optimal **CO₂/N₂ adsorption selectivity**, identifying promising candidates like **PCOD-8167523** with a predicted selectivity >120 at 298 K and 1 bar. This *in silico* screening accelerates the identification of targets for synthesis, focusing experimental efforts on the most promising regions of chemical space.

**Guiding Synthesis Parameters**
Beyond static property prediction, ML is revolutionizing the understanding and control of dynamic synthesis processes. By training on historical synthesis data—extracted from literature via natural language processing (NLP)—models correlate complex precursor mixtures and conditions with specific outcomes. For example, a Bayesian optimization model successfully navigated the multi-parameter synthesis space of **ZSM-5** (MFI-type), identifying optimal conditions to maximize crystallinity and phase purity: a gel composition of **1 SiO₂ : 0.033 Al₂O₃ : 0.27 TPAOH : 35 H₂O**, subjected to **hydrothermal treatment at 170°C for 48 hours** in a Teflon-lined autoclave. Such models deconvolute non-linear interactions between variables (e.g., alkalinity, silica source, aging time, temperature) that are intractable to human intuition alone.

Furthermore, reinforcement learning (RL) agents are being developed to autonomously design synthesis routes. An RL agent, as reported by Shan et al. (*Sci. Adv.*, 2021, 7, eabg8183), learned to propose viable synthesis protocols for **chabazite (CHA-type)** zeolites by interacting with a simulated chemical environment, optimizing for both phase purity and yield. The agent discovered non-conventional recipes, including the use of **N,N,N-trimethyl-1-adamantammonium hydroxide** as an organic structure-directing agent (OSDA) at **150°C for 72 hours**, which were subsequently validated experimentally.

**Characterization and Phase Analysis**
ML also augments characterization. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) can analyze **powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD)** patterns with superhuman speed and consistency, classifying phases (e.g., distinguishing **FAU**, **BEA**, and **MFI** topologies) and even quantifying amorphous content from baseline features. Similarly, ML models applied to **scanning electron microscopy (SEM)** images can automatically characterize particle morphology and size distribution, providing rapid feedback on synthesis outcomes.

**Challenges and Future Outlook**
Despite these advances, critical challenges remain. The availability of high-quality, standardized, and large-scale synthesis data is the primary bottleneck. Many published studies lack exhaustive meta-data (e.g., exact stirring rates, precursor batch impurities), limiting model generalizability. Future efforts must focus on creating open, curated databases and integrating *operando* characterization data (e.g., from **synchrotron X-ray diffraction** or **Raman spectroscopy**) into closed-loop autonomous synthesis platforms.

In conclusion, machine learning is transitioning from an ancillary tool to a core component of zeolite science. By enabling the predictive design of frameworks and the rational optimization of synthesis pathways, ML promises to unlock novel zeolites with unprecedented properties for sustainable energy and chemical processes, marking a new era of intelligent materials discovery.

# In-Situ and Operando Spectroscopy of Zeolite Catalysis: A Literature Review

## Introduction

The complexity of zeolite-catalyzed reactions demands advanced characterization techniques that capture active sites and reaction intermediates under realistic conditions. Over the past decade, **in-situ** and **operando** spectroscopy have emerged as indispensable tools for elucidating structure-performance relationships in zeolite catalysis, enabling observation of transient species and deactivation mechanisms that conventional ex-situ methods cannot resolve.

## Major Spectroscopic Techniques and Applications

**Infrared (IR) spectroscopy** remains foundational for zeolite characterization. Operando DRIFTS studies of MFI zeolites in methanol-to-olefins (MTO) reactions have identified surface methoxy species and alkene cycle intermediates, demonstrating the dual-cycle mechanism proposed by Haw and coworkers. Recently, **2D-IR correlation spectroscopy** has resolved overlapping bands in BEA zeolites during n-hexane cracking, distinguishing Brønsted acid sites from extra-framework aluminum species.

**Raman spectroscopy**, historically challenged by fluorescence, has advanced through UV-resonant excitation. Operando Raman of CHA zeolites (SSZ-13) during the methanol-to-gasoline process has tracked coke precursor evolution, revealing that graphitic carbon deposits form preferentially at the 8-membered ring windows.

**X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS)** provides element-specific information on framework aluminum and transition metal sites. Operando XANES/EXAFS of Fe-ZSM-5 during benzene oxidation to phenol identified Fe(IV)=O as the active oxidant, while studies of Cu-SSZ-13 in NH₃-SCR have captured Cu⁺/Cu²⁺ redox cycling under lean conditions.

**Solid-state NMR** has progressed with **operando** capabilities, using rapid magic-angle spinning (MAS) up to 60 kHz. ¹³C MAS NMR of MFI catalysts during MTO has quantified the "oleum pool" species, confirming their role in the autocatalytic cycle.

## Synthesis-Structure-Performance Relationships

Zeolite synthesis employing **tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH)** for MFI yields highly crystalline materials with minimal defects, as confirmed by in-situ X-ray diffraction showing unit cell parameters decreasing by 0.3% upon calcination. **Triethanolamine (TEAOH)** in BEA synthesis produces larger crystal sizes (2-5 μm) beneficial for diffusion-limited reactions, while **choline chloride** as a low-cost SDA has enabled green synthesis of CHA zeolites with comparable acidity to SSZ-13 prepared with expensive N,N,N-trimethyladamantylammonium.

Hierarchical zeolites synthesized via **hard-templating** (carbon aerogels) or **soft-templating** (surfactants) show distinct spectroscopic signatures—enhanced Bro̷ populations accessible to 2,6-di-tert-butylpyridine in ¹H MAS NMR, correlating with improved catalytic lifetime in 1,3,5-triisopropylbenzene hydroconversion.

## Current Challenges

Despite advances, several limitations persist. **Temporal resolution** remains insufficient for capturing fast elementary steps; recent efforts with **quasi-operando** techniques using stopped-flow reactors approach millisecond timescales. **Pressure and temperature gaps** between laboratory reactors and industrial conditions still challenge spectroscopic cells. **Spectroscopic interpretation** of overlapping contributions from framework and adsorbates requires advanced multivariate analysis, though machine learning approaches are now being applied.

## Future Directions

Emerging opportunities include **multimodal operando cells** combining IR, Raman, and XAS simultaneously, and **ambient pressure X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (AP-XPS)** for studying ze

# Results and Discussion

## 3.1 Elemental Composition and Framework Characterization

Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) analysis of the calcined zeolite samples revealed Si/Al molar ratios of 12.4 ± 0.3, 25.7 ± 0.6, and 48.2 ± 1.1 for samples designated ZSM-5(12), ZSM-5(25), and ZSM-5(48), respectively. These values were determined from measured silicon concentrations of 34.2, 36.8, and 38.1 wt% and aluminum concentrations of 2.74, 1.41, and 0.78 wt%, confirming progressive framework dealumination consistent with the gel-phase Si/Al ratios employed during hydrothermal synthesis. Sodium residuals following NH₄⁺ exchange and subsequent calcination at 550°C were uniformly below 0.05 wt%, confirming near-complete conversion to the proton form and negligible charge compensation by extraframework cations.

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns confirmed the MFI topology in all three samples, with characteristic reflections at 2θ = 7.9°, 8.8°, 13.2°, 14.8°, 23.1°, 23.9°, and 24.4° (Cu Kα radiation, λ = 1.5406 Å), corresponding to the (011), (020), (051), (151), (501), (151̄), and (303) crystallographic planes of the orthorhombic MFI framework (space group *Pnma*). Relative crystallinity, calculated by comparison of integrated peak areas between 22.5° and 25.0° 2θ against a commercial reference standard (Zeolyst CBV-2314), exceeded 94% for all samples, indicating well-developed long-range structural order uncompromised by the varying aluminum incorporation levels.

Nitrogen physisorption measurements at 77 K yielded BET surface areas of 418, 432, and 441 m²/g for ZSM-5(12), ZSM-5(25), and ZSM-5(48), respectively, with micropore volumes of 0.162, 0.171, and 0.178 cm³/g derived from t-plot analysis. The systematic increase in both surface area and micropore volume with increasing Si/Al ratio is consistent with reduced framework distortion at lower aluminum contents, as previously reported by Vjunov et al. (2015), who documented micropore volumes of 0.16–0.18 cm³/g for analogous H-ZSM-5 materials. Mesopore volumes, quantified by the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method applied to the desorption branch, were comparatively modest at 0.031–0.047 cm³/g, confirming predominantly microporous architectures without significant secondary porosity introduced during synthesis.

Temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia (NH₃-TPD) revealed bimodal desorption profiles characteristic of weak and strong acid sites, with low-temperature peaks centered at 185–195°C and high-temperature peaks at 385–420°C. Total acid site concentrations, quantified by integration of calibrated NH₃ mass spectrometer signals, decreased systematically from 0.812 mmol/g in ZSM-5(12) to 0.394 mmol/g in ZSM-5(25) and 0.198 mmol/g in ZSM-5(48). Strong Brønsted acid site concentrations, determined independently by ²⁷Al MAS NMR and pyridine-adsorption FTIR at 1545 cm⁻¹ following evacuation at 350°C, were 0.641, 0.301, and 0.149 mmol/g, respectively. The near-linear correlation between framework aluminum content derived from ICP-OES and Brønsted acid

ZSM-5, a medium-pore zeolite possessing the MFI framework topology, has served as a cornerstone of industrial acid catalysis since its discovery by Mobil researchers in the early 1970s. Defined by its intersecting 10-membered ring channels measuring approximately 0.51 × 0.55 nm and 0.53 × 0.56 nm, ZSM-5 offers exceptional shape selectivity and robust hydrothermal stability. However, the exclusive presence of micropores imposes severe intracrystalline diffusion limitations on reactants and products, particularly in processes involving bulky molecules. This constraint leads to reduced effective turnover frequencies (TOF) and accelerated catalyst deactivation via coke deposition within the pore channels. To circumvent these limitations, the synthesis of hierarchical zeolites—materials incorporating a secondary network of mesopores (2–50 nm)—has emerged as a critical strategy to improve mass transfer while retaining the intrinsic shape selectivity of the microporous framework.

Among the various methodologies for introducing mesoporosity, post-synthetic desilication via alkaline treatment has established itself as a versatile, scalable, and economically viable route. Seminal work by Groen et al. (2004) elucidated the mechanism of controlled silicon extraction in alkaline media (typically 0.1–0.5 M NaOH), demonstrating that the hydrolysis of framework Si-O-Si bonds creates intracrystalline mesopores. This process is highly dependent on the framework Si/Al ratio; optimal mesoporosity development is generally achieved in ZSM-5 samples with Si/Al ratios between 25 and 50. In this range, the hydrophobicity of the silica matrix facilitates NaOH penetration, while the presence of aluminum induces a "framework protection" effect, preventing excessive amorphization and preserving crystallinity. The resulting hierarchical structure typically exhibits a mesopore surface area exceeding 150 m²/g and a substantial reduction in diffusion path length, thereby enhancing the accessibility of active Brønsted acid sites.

The industrial significance of ZSM-5 is underscored by the expansive global zeolite market, which was valued at approximately USD 4.5 billion in 2023 and is projected to surpass USD 6 billion by 2030. A substantial portion of this market share is driven by the demand for Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) additives and methanol-to-hydrocarbons (MTH) catalysts. In FCC operations, hierarchical ZSM-5 is utilized at dosages of 0.5 to 2.0 wt% to selectively crack heavy naphtha fractions into light olefins (propylene and butylene). The industry requirement for high propylene yields (>20 wt%) necessitates catalysts that can minimize secondary hydrogen transfer reactions—a goal achieved by reducing residence time through improved mesoporous diffusion. Similarly, in the conversion of biomass-derived oxygenates, such as the catalytic fast pyrolysis of lignocellulosic biomass, the bulky nature of pyrolysis vapors (kinetic diameters > 0.8 nm) strictly requires hierarchical pore systems to prevent pore blockage and rapid catalyst coking.

Despite the progress, key scientific challenges persist in the precise control of mesopore architecture. Uncontrolled desilication can lead to extensive silicon leaching, resulting in a loss of micropore volume and partial dealumination, which diminishes the density of strong acid sites. Furthermore, the random nature of the mesopore network generated by simple alkaline leaching lacks the uniformity desired for specific shape-selective applications. Consequently, the primary motivation for current research is the development of advanced desilication protocols, such as the use of pore-directing agents (e.g., cetyltrimethylammonium bromide) or chelating masks (e.g., tetrapropylammonium ions), to tailor the mesopore size distribution and spatial arrangement. This optimization is essential for meeting the rigorous demands of next-generation petrochemical and refining processes, which require catalysts that combine high activity with extended operational lifetimes under severe regeneration conditions.

### **Literature Review: Synthesis and Crystallization of Zeolite Beta**

Zeolite Beta (structure code ***BEA***) is a high-silica, large-pore zeolite characterized by a three-dimensional 12-membered ring pore system and intergrowth of polymorphs A and B, which imparts significant catalytic utility in acid-catalyzed reactions such as fluid catalytic cracking, alkylation, and isomerization. Its synthesis, first reported in 1967 using tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH) as the structure-directing agent (SDA), has evolved considerably, with recent research focused on reducing cost, enhancing sustainability, and controlling polymorph intergrowth.

**Classical Hydrothermal Synthesis and Mechanism**
The conventional synthesis of zeolite Beta involves hydrothermal crystallization from an aluminosilicate gel with a composition typically in the range: SiO₂/Al₂O₃ = 10–100, H₂O/SiO₂ = 7–20, OH⁻/SiO₂ = 0.1–0.6, and SDA/SiO₂ = 0.1–0.5. TEAOH remains the benchmark SDA for directing the ***BEA*** topology. The crystallization mechanism is non-classical, proceeding through the aggregation of nanosized (~2–3 nm) precursor particles into larger, partially ordered domains before long-range crystallographic order is established. This pathway, elucidated through techniques like cryo-TEM and SAXS, results in the characteristic intergrown nature and high external surface area of Beta crystals. Comparative studies with ***MFI*** (ZSM-5, directed by TPAOH) show that ***BEA*** nucleation has a higher energetic barrier, often requiring longer crystallization times (3–10 days) at moderate temperatures (100–150°C).

**Recent Advances in Synthesis Methodologies (Past Decade)**

1.  **SDA Innovation and Cost Reduction:** A major thrust has been to replace or reduce the use of expensive, commercially pure TEAOH. Successful strategies include:
    *   **Mixed SDA Systems:** Combining TEAOH with cheaper amines like diethanolamine or using tetraethylammonium halides (TEAX, X = Cl, Br) with an additional alkali hydroxide. This can reduce TEAOH consumption by up to 50% while maintaining crystallinity.
    *   **Alternative Organic SDAs:** Bulky, rigid dications derived from derivatives of 1,4-diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane (DABCO) have been shown to produce ***BEA*** with high phase purity and tailored Si/Al ratios (15–∞).
    *   **SDA-Free Synthesis:** Significant progress has been made using seeding strategies. The addition of 5–10 wt.% calcined ***BEA*** seeds to a gel with low alkalinity (Na₂O/SiO₂ < 0.2) can yield fully crystalline material without any organic SDA, dramatically improving process economics and sustainability.

2.  **Faujasite (***FAU***) Interzeolite Conversion (IZC):** This green chemistry approach uses a pre-existing zeolite as a nutrient source. The transformation of ***FAU*** (zeolite Y) into ***BEA*** in the presence of TEAOH or mixed SDAs is highly efficient, occurring within 24–48 hours at 140°C. The mechanism involves the dissolution of the ***FAU*** framework and reassembly of the secondary building units (4-membered rings) into the ***BEA*** structure. This method often yields materials with hierarchical porosity.

3.  **Control of Crystal Size and Morphology:** Nanosized Beta (crystals < 50 nm) is increasingly sought for diffusion-limited reactions. Techniques include:
    *   **Low-Temperature Aging:** Prolonged aging at 40–80°C before crystallization promotes the formation of a dense population of nuclei.
    *   **Confined Synthesis:** Using carbon black or polymers as hard templates to create intracrystalline mesopores.
    *   **Comparison with Other Zeolites:** Unlike the tightly controlled nanocrystals of ***CHA*** (SSZ-13) made with expensive TMAdaOH, nanocrystalline Beta synthesis aims for cost-effectiveness.

**Current Challenges and Future Directions**

*   **Polymorph Control:** Precisely synthesizing a single polymorph of ***BEA*** (A, B, or C) rather than an intergrowth remains a fundamental challenge. The use of germanium as a heteroatom to stabilize the D4R units of polymorph B has been explored but is costly and yields less stable frameworks.
*   **Aluminum Zoning and Acid Site Distribution:** Controlling the spatial distribution of aluminum within Beta crystals is difficult but critical for catalytic selectivity, especially in bulky molecule conversions. Advanced characterization (e.g., Atom Probe Tomography) is needed to correlate synthesis

# Synthesis of ZSM-5 Zeolite with MFI Framework Structure

## Introduction

Zeolites are microporous crystalline aluminosilicates characterized by framework structures designated by three-letter codes established by the International Zeolite Association. The MFI framework code corresponds to the ZSM-5 structure, featuring a two-dimensional channel system with straight 10-membered ring channels (5.3 × 5.6 Å) and sinusoidal 10-membered ring channels (5.1 × 5.5 Å). This synthesis procedure describes the hydrothermal preparation of ZSM-5 using a conventional tetrapropylammonium (TPA) template method.

## Materials and Equipment

**Reagents (for one synthesis batch):**
- Sodium hydroxide (NaOH): 0.45 g
- Sodium aluminate (NaAlO₂): 0.18 g
- Tetrapropylammonium bromide (TPABr): 1.20 g
- Colloidal silica (30 wt% SiO₂): 15.0 g
- Deionized water: 80.0 mL

**Equipment:**
- Polypropylene autoclave (100 mL capacity)
- Stainless steel autoclave liner
- Magnetic stirrer with heating plate
- Programmable muffle furnace
- Centrifuge (5000 rpm)
- Analytical balance (±0.001 g)
- Pt/Rh thermocouple

## Synthesis Procedure

### 1. Gel Preparation

Sodium hydroxide (0.45 g) and sodium aluminate (0.18 g) were dissolved in deionized water (40.0 mL) in a 150 mL polypropylene beaker under magnetic stirring at 300 rpm. The solution was heated to 80°C and maintained for 30 minutes to ensure complete dissolution of the aluminate. Tetrapropylammonium bromide (1.20 g) was added to the alkaline aluminate solution and stirring continued for 15 minutes at 80°C.

Colloidal silica (15.0 g, 30 wt% SiO₂) was slowly added dropwise over 20 minutes while maintaining temperature at 80°C and stirring at 400 rpm. The mixture became progressively more viscous as silica was incorporated. Following silica addition, the remaining deionized water (40.0 mL) was added to achieve the final composition. The gel was stirred for 2 hours at 80°C to ensure homogeneity.

The final gel composition expressed as molar ratios was: Al₂O₃: 30 SiO₂: 4.5 Na₂O: 9 TPAOH: 1500 H₂O.

### 2. Crystallization

The homogeneous gel was transferred into a 100 mL polypropylene autoclave liner using a polypropylene funnel. The sealed autoclave was placed in a convection oven preheated to 175°C. Crystallization proceeded for 72 hours under static conditions. The autoclave was then removed and cooled naturally to ambient temperature (25°C) over 4 hours.

### 3. Product Recovery

The solid product was separated from the mother liquor by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 15 minutes. The supernatant was decanted, and the solid was resusupended in deionized water (100 mL) and centrifuged again. This washing procedure was repeated three times to remove residual template and alkalis.

### 4. Drying and Calcination

The washed solid was dried in an air atmosphere at 110°C for 12 hours in a convection dryer. The dried powder was placed in a ceramic combustion boat and transferred to a muffle furnace. Calcination was performed under flowing air (100 mL/min) with the following temperature program: heating from 25°C to 120°C at 2°C/min (hold 2 hours), then from 120°C to 550°C at 5°C/min (hold 6 hours

# Regeneration Procedure for Spent Zeolite Y Catalyst

## Materials and Equipment

- Spent zeolite HY/USY catalyst (coke content: 5-8 wt% as determined by thermogravimetric analysis)
- Deionized water
- 0.1 M ammonium nitrate solution (NH₄NO₃)
- Analytical balance (precision: ±0.01 g)
- Muffle furnace capable of 600°C with programmable temperature controller
- Tubular furnace with gas delivery system
- 500 mL borosilicate beaker
- 250 mL porcelain crucible
- Glass filtration apparatus with Whatman Grade 1 filter paper
- Forced-air convection drying oven (max 200°C)
- Magnetic stirrer with heating capability

## Procedure

**Step 1: Pretreatment and Coke Removal**

The spent zeolite catalyst (50.0 g, particle size 0.5-1.0 mm) was placed in a 500 mL borosilicate beaker and transferred to a muffle furnace preheated to 200°C. The material was heated at a ramp rate of 5°C/min under static air atmosphere for 2 hours to remove physisorbed water and volatile hydrocarbons. Subsequently, the temperature was increased to 500°C at a ramp rate of 10°C/min and maintained for 4 hours to combust accumulated coke deposits. The furnace was cooled naturally to room temperature, and the decarbonized zeolite was recovered.

**Step 2: Aqueous Washing**

The decarbonized zeolite (approximately 48.2 g, indicating 3.6% coke removal) was transferred to a 500 mL borosilicate beaker containing 300 mL of deionized water. The suspension was heated to 80°C under magnetic stirring at 300 rpm and maintained for 1 hour to remove soluble inorganic impurities and residual alkali metal cations. The mixture was cooled to 25°C and filtered through Whatman Grade 1 filter paper using aBuchner funnel under vacuum. The filtrate was discarded, and the solid was washed with three sequential 100 mL portions of deionized water.

**Step 3: Ammonium Ion Exchange**

The washed zeolite was immersed in 250 mL of 0.1 M NH₄NO₃ solution in a 500 mL beaker. The mixture was heated to 70°C with stirring at 250 rpm for 2 hours to exchange residual protons and restore the ammonium form. After cooling to 25°C, the slurry was filtered and the solid was washed with 200 mL of deionized water. This ion exchange procedure was repeated two additional times to ensure complete ammonium incorporation.

**Step 4: Drying**

The ion-exchanged zeolite was spread uniformly in a thin layer (approximately 5 mm thickness) on a porcelain crucible and placed in a forced-air convection drying oven at 120°C for 12 hours. The material was ground gently with a mortar and pestle to break agglomerates and ensure homogeneous particle size distribution.

**Step 5: Calcination and Template Removal**

The dried zeolite (45.8 g, representing 91.6% mass recovery) was transferred to a clean 250 mL porcelain crucible and placed in a tubular furnace. Calcination was performed under dry

**Introduction and Background**

The evolution of heterogeneous catalysis has progressively shifted from bulk metals to nanoscale particles, and most recently, to the atomic limit: Single-Atom Catalysts (SACs). SACs are defined by the presence of isolated metal atoms dispersed on a solid support, representing the ultimate utilization efficiency of precious metals—approaching 100%. Zeolites, comprising crystalline microporous aluminosilicates with well-defined pore architectures and high surface areas (typically 300–800 m²/g), serve as ideal hosts for stabilizing these isolated atoms. The rigid framework of zeolites provides spatial confinement, while ion-exchange sites and framework defects offer specific anchoring points, creating uniform coordination environments essential for catalytic precision.

The industrial relevance of zeolite-based catalysis is immense, underpinning global refining and petrochemical sectors. The global zeolite market was valued at approximately USD 13.5 billion in 2022 and is projected to expand at a CAGR of over 5%, driven largely by demand for fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) and hydrocracking catalysts. In FCC alone, daily processing capacity exceeds 12 million barrels of oil equivalent, utilizing zeolite Y-based formulations. The integration of SACs into these systems addresses critical economic and environmental imperatives. For instance, the propane dehydrogenation (PDH) industry, which produces over 10 million metric tons of propylene annually, relies on Pt-Sn or Ga-based catalysts. Transitioning to Pt single-atom sites within MFI or CHA zeolite frameworks has demonstrated the potential to significantly reduce noble metal loading while minimizing undesirable cracking reactions and coking, thereby extending catalyst lifecycle.

However, the deployment of zeolite-supported SACs faces formidable scientific challenges. The primary obstacle is thermodynamic instability; isolated metal atoms possess high surface free energy, driving them to aggregate into nanoparticles under reaction conditions, particularly at elevated temperatures (often >600°C for industrial processes). Furthermore, the hydrothermal stability of the metal-support interface is critical in applications like automotive exhaust abatement. For example, Cu-exchanged SSZ-13, the state-of-the-art catalyst for selective catalytic reduction (SCR) of NOx, must withstand temperatures exceeding 800°C and high steam partial pressures without losing active Cu²⁺ sites or dealuminating the framework.

Current research is motivated by the need to decouple activity from scaling relations that limit conventional catalysts. By precisely tailoring the local coordination geometry of single atoms (e.g., changing the metal’s coordination number from 4 to 2), researchers can modulate the adsorption energies of intermediates, unlocking unique selectivity profiles. Consequently, the synthesis of robust zeolite-supported SACs via strategies such as wet impregnation, atomic layer deposition (ALD), or post-synthetic modification is not merely an academic exercise but a necessary pathway to the next generation of sustainable chemical manufacturing.

**Automated Robotic Synthesis of Zeolites: Advancing High-Throughput Discovery and Optimization**

**Abstract:** The integration of automation and robotics into zeolite synthesis represents a paradigm shift in the field of microporous and mesoporous materials research. By enabling high-throughput experimentation (HTE), these systems accelerate the exploration of complex, multi-parameter synthesis space, facilitating the discovery of novel frameworks and the optimization of existing materials with unprecedented efficiency. This review examines the principles, experimental configurations, and significant outcomes of robotic zeolite synthesis, emphasizing its role in advancing both fundamental science and applied materials development.

**1. Introduction to Robotic Synthesis Platforms**
Modern automated platforms for hydrothermal synthesis are typically centered on liquid-handling robots capable of precise reagent dispensing (with volumes as low as 10 µL) into arrays of chemically inert, multi-well autoclaves (e.g., 48- or 96-well Teflon-lined reactors). These systems are integrated with programmable ovens or heating blocks for static or tumbling hydrothermal crystallization. A key advancement is the use of Symyx-style reactor arrays, where each well is individually sealed, allowing for parallel synthesis under varied conditions. A standard protocol involves the robotic sequential addition of reagents: an aluminum source (e.g., sodium aluminate or aluminum isopropoxide), a silica source (e.g., fumed silica, tetraethyl orthosilicate, or sodium silicate), structure-directing agents (SDAs) such as tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH) for MFI or *N,N,N-trimethyl-1-adamantammonium hydroxide* for CHA, and a mineralizer (NaOH, KOH). The gel composition is rigorously homogenized by the robotic arm through mixing cycles before sealing and transfer to the heating station.

**2. Experimental Parameters and High-Throughput Design**
Robotic systems excel at constructing multidimensional parameter matrices. A single campaign can systematically vary critical factors including:
*   **Gel Composition:** SiO₂/Al₂O₃ ratios from 10 to ∞ (all-silica), OH⁻/SiO₂ (0.1–0.7), H₂O/SiO₂ (5–50), and SDA/SiO₂ (0.1–0.5).
*   **Crystallization Conditions:** Temperatures from 80–180°C (±0.5°C) and durations from 12 hours to 14 days.
*   **Post-Synthetic Treatments:** *In-situ* pH adjustment or the addition of heteroatoms (e.g., Ti, Sn, B).

For instance, a seminal study by Corma et al. (2018) utilized a robotic platform to screen over 2,500 gel compositions in pursuit of extra-large pore zeolites, systematically varying diquaternary ammonium SDAs and fluoride concentrations. This HTE approach led to the identification of critical nucleation domains for frameworks like **ITQ-43** and **ITQ-55**.

**3. Characterization Integration and Data Management**
The throughput of robotic synthesis necessitates equally high-throughput characterization. Automated workflows typically couple the synthesis platform to parallel centrifugation, washing, and drying stations. Subsequent analysis employs rapid-scan X-ray diffraction (XRD) with automated phase identification via reference databases (ICDD), coupled with automated electron microscopy (SEM) for morphology analysis. This integration was demonstrated effectively by Moliner et al. (2016) in optimizing the synthesis of the aluminophosphate **STA-30**, where robotic synthesis combined with combinatorial XRD and microcalorimetry identified the narrow gel composition (Al₂O₃:P₂O₅:SDA:H₂O = 1:1:2:100) and crystallization window (175°C for 48h) required for phase purity.

**4. Key Outcomes and Applications**
The primary impact of robotic synthesis lies in two domains:
a)  **Framework Discovery:** By enabling the testing of non-intuitive SDA molecules and gel chemistries, robotics has been instrumental in discovering novel zeotypes, such as the chiral, extra-large pore **SU-102** (IZA code **SYT**), synthesized from a complex bis-imidazolium SDA.
b)  **Process Optimization:** It allows for precise mapping of crystallization fields. For example, the optimization of **SSZ-13** (CHA) synthesis for NOx SCR applications has been streamlined, defining the optimal Na₂O/(TEA)₂O ratio of 0.25 and crystallization temperature of 160°C for 7 days to maximize yield and crystal size uniformity.

**5. Conclusion and Future Perspectives**
Automated robotic synthesis has evolved from a screening tool to an indispensable component of rational zeolite design. It provides a robust empirical foundation for machine learning and computational prediction models by generating large, consistent datasets. Future developments will likely focus on closed-loop systems, where *in-situ* spectroscopic data (e.g

# Alkalinity Control in Zeolite Crystallization: A Literature Review

## Introduction

The precise control of alkalinity (OH⁻ concentration) represents one of the most critical parameters in zeolite synthesis, fundamentally influencing nucleation kinetics, crystal growth rates, phase selectivity, and final product morphology. Over the past decade, significant advances have been made in understanding and manipulating the hydrothermal conditions to achieve enhanced control over zeolite crystallization.

## Mechanisms of Alkalinity Influence

The hydroxide ion serves multiple essential functions in zeolite synthesis: it acts as a mineralizer facilitating silica and alumina dissolution, generates silicate and aluminate anions through depolymerization, and serves as the chargebalancing counterion for cationic structure-directing agents (SDAs). Recent studies have demonstrated that OH⁻ concentration directly modulates the Si/Al ratio of the gel phase and the supersaturation levels governing nucleation. Rabinaw et al. (2021) established that optimal OH⁻ concentrations typically range between 0.5–4.0 mol/L for most zeolite syntheses, with specific maxima varying by framework type.

## Comparative Synthesis Approaches

### Organic Template-Directed Synthesis

The framework types MFI (ZSM-5) and MEL (ZSM-11) are predominantly synthesized using tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH) and tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH) as SDAs. Recent work by Liu et al. (2022) demonstrated that controlling NaOH/TPAOH ratios between 0.1–0.5 enables exquisite control over crystal size (50 nm to 15 μm) in TPA⁺-MFI systems. The alkalinity provided by TPAOH itself contributes significantly to the overall OH⁻ concentration, often eliminating the need for additional alkali metal hydroxides.

### Pure Silica Systems

For BEA (Beta zeolite), the synthesis typically employs TEAOH as both SDA and alkalinity source. Recent advances by Zhang and coworkers (2020) showed that TEAOH concentrations of 0.3–0.5 mol/L combined with seed-directed approaches yield phase-pure BEA with Si/Al ratios exceeding 200. The FAU (faujasite) framework requires lower OH⁻ concentrations (typically 0.8–1.5 mol/L NaOH) when using Na⁺ as the structure-directing cation alongside crown ethers or other organic additives.

### Chaotic and Small-Pore Frameworks

The CHA (chabazite) framework has received considerable attention for its applications in catalysis and gas separation. Syntheses using N,N,N-trimethyladamantanammonium hydroxide (TMAdaOH) or benzyltrimethylammonium hydroxide require precise OH⁻ control (1.5–2.5 mol/L) to avoid competing phases such as GIS or ANA. Studies by Shamzhy et al. (2023) demonstrated that incremental alkalinity adjustment during synthesis enables phase-selective CHA crystallization with Si/Al ratios tunable from 3 to 50.

## Advanced Control Strategies

Recent innovations include dynamic alkalinity modulation, where OH⁻ concentration is varied during synthesis (Bai et al., 2022), and the use of weak bases such as Ba(OH)₂ or Ca(OH)₂ for sustained alkalinity release. Steam-assisted conversions and dry gel synthesis methods have also emerged as alternatives to manage alkalinity profiles, reducing water consumption and enhancing product crystallinity.

## Current Challenges and Future Directions

Despite significant progress, challenges remain in achieving complete alkalinity control, particularly in predicting phase competition and managing silica consolidation. The development of in situ characterization methods for real-time alkalinity monitoring and computational models predicting crystallization behavior under varying OH⁻ conditions represent active research frontiers. Future directions include integrating machine learning approaches for alkalinity optimization and exploring sustainable, low-alkalinity synthesis routes using eco-friendly templates and renewable alkali sources.

# Experimental Results and Discussion

## Zeolite Framework Identification and Structural Characterization

The successful synthesis of four distinct zeolite framework types—MFI, FAU, BEA, and LTA—was confirmed through powder X-ray diffraction analysis. The XRD patterns of the calcined samples exhibited characteristic diffraction peaks consistent with published JCPDS reference patterns (card numbers 44-0661, 38-0241, 47-0182, and 39-0223 for the respective frameworks).

The MFI framework displayed the diagnostic (011), (200), and (051) reflections at 2θ values of 23.09°, 23.38°, and 23.96°, respectively, confirming the orthorhombic Pnma space group symmetry characteristic of ZSM-5 topology. The FAU sample exhibited the expected cubicFd-3m symmetry with prominent peaks at 2θ = 6.18°, 10.15°, 11.92°, and 15.62° corresponding to the (111), (220), (311), and (331) planes. The BEA framework, belonging to the tetragonal P4₂/mmc space group, showed characteristic reflections at 2θ = 7.70° and 22.44°, while the LTA framework demonstrated cubic Pm-3m symmetry with the (111) and (200) reflections at 2θ = 7.18° and 10.16°. All synthesized materials exhibited high phase purity with no detectable crystalline impurities.

## Textural Properties

Nitrogen physisorption measurements at 77 K provided quantitative textural parameters for the synthesized zeolites. The BET surface areas followed the order: FAU (687 m²/g) > BEA (624 m²/g) > LTA (543 m²/g) > MFI (412 m²/g). These values fall within the ranges reported in the literature for conventionally synthesized zeolites of corresponding framework types, where typical BET areas of 400–500 m²/g for MFI, 500–900 m²/g for FAU, 600–750 m²/g for BEA, and 500–700 m²/g for LTA are well-established.

The microporous volumes, determined by the t-plot method, showed excellent agreement with theoretical values based on crystal structure data: FAU (0.33 cm³/g), BEA (0.28 cm³/g), LTA

The post-synthetic modification of zeolitic frameworks via organosilane grafting, commonly termed silylation, represents a sophisticated methodology for tailoring surface polarity, hydrophobicity, and catalytic selectivity. This process predominantly targets surface silanol groups ($\equiv$Si–OH) situated on the external crystal faces and defect sites within the micropores. By converting these hydrophilic moieties into hydrophobic organosilicon entities, researchers can effectively engineer the zeolite interface for advanced applications in catalysis and separation.

In liquid-phase silylation, reagents such as hexamethyldisilazane (HMDS) or chlorotrimethylsilane (TMCS) are frequently employed to functionalize high-silica zeolites like ZSM-5 and Beta. For instance, the hydrophobization of Zeolite Beta is typically achieved by refluxing the calcined material in anhydrous toluene containing HMDS at 110°C for 12 to 24 hours under an inert nitrogen atmosphere. This procedure results in the formation of $\equiv$Si–O–Si(CH$_3$)$_3$ linkages, confirmed by the attenuation of the IR band at 3745 cm$^{-1}$ corresponding to isolated silanols, alongside the emergence of C–H stretching vibrations near 2960 cm$^{-1}$. The modification significantly alters the adsorption isotherms; silylated faujasite, for example, exhibits a marked reduction in water vapor uptake, shifting the isotherm from Type I (hydrophilic) to Type V (hydrophobic), while maintaining high affinity for non-polar organics like $n$-hexane.

For precise pore-size engineering, Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) is the preferred technique. Seminal work by Niwa et al. demonstrated the controlled deposition of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) vapor onto HZSM-5. By controlling the reaction temperature between 150°C and 300°C and the duration of exposure (typically 0.5 to 5 hours), the thickness of the silica layer can be tuned to sub-nanometer precision. This "pore-mouth engineering" reduces the effective aperture diameter of the 10-membered ring channels without blocking the internal volume. Consequently, this steric hindrance enhances shape selectivity in catalytic reactions, such as the disproportionation of toluene or the isomerization of xylenes, by restricting the transition state formation or product diffusion of bulkier molecules like $m$-xylene while favoring $p$-xylene.

Beyond steric modification, silylation acts as a potent method for surface passivation, essential for suppressing non-selective reactions. The neutralization of external Brønsted acid sites minimizes undesirable side reactions, such as over-cracking or coking, which frequently occur on the external surface during fluid catalytic cracking (FCC). Characterization via $^{29}$Si MAS NMR typically reveals the appearance of T-sites ($R$–Si(OSi)$_3$) resonating near –55 to –65 ppm, confirming the covalent anchoring of the organic moiety. Thus, silylation not only optimizes the zeolite’s hydrothermal stability but also refines its catalytic precision, making it indispensable in the production of fine chemicals.

### **The Influence of Silica-to-Alumina Ratio on Zeolite Properties: A Foundational Parameter in Molecular Sieve Science**

#### **Introduction and Historical Context**
Zeolites, crystalline microporous aluminosilicates, represent a cornerstone of modern heterogeneous catalysis and separation science. Their defining characteristic is a three-dimensional framework of SiO₄ and AlO₄⁻ tetrahedra linked via shared oxygen atoms, generating uniform channels and cages of molecular dimensions. The substitution of Si⁴⁺ by Al³⁺ introduces a framework negative charge, which is balanced by extra-framework cations (e.g., H⁺, Na⁺, NH₄⁺) that confer acidity and ion-exchange capacity. The **Silica-to-Alumina Ratio (SAR)**, defined as the molar ratio of SiO₂ to Al₂O₃ in the framework, is arguably the most critical compositional parameter determining a zeolite's physicochemical properties, stability, and ultimate application.

Historically, the discovery and development of zeolites were intrinsically linked to SAR. Natural zeolites, such as clinoptilolite (SAR ~5-10) and mordenite (SAR ~10), possess relatively low SARs and were first exploited in the 1950s for ion-exchange processes. The synthetic zeolite revolution began with the pioneering work of Milton and Breck at Union Carbide in the 1950s, leading to the commercialization of Linde Type A (LTA, SAR ~1-2) and faujasites (X, Y; SAR ~2.5-5). These low-SAR materials exhibited high cation content and strong electric fields, making them excellent adsorbents and catalysts for early petroleum refining processes like catalytic cracking. The subsequent development of high-silica zeolites, most notably ZSM-5 (MFI topology) by Mobil researchers in the 1970s, with SARs tunable from ~30 to beyond 1000, unlocked unprecedented hydrothermal stability and shape-selective catalysis, fundamentally transforming chemical process engineering.

#### **Industrial Relevance and Market Context**
The global zeolite market, valued at approximately **USD 33.5 billion in 2022**, is heavily segmented by application, with catalyst and adsorbent functions dominating. SAR is a primary specification driving this segmentation. In **fluid catalytic cracking (FCC)**, the largest catalytic application by volume, ultra-stable Y (USY) zeolites with SARs of **5 to 6** are standard. Their moderate acidity and hydrothermal stability are essential for converting heavy gas oils into gasoline and olefins, processing over **14 million barrels of oil per day globally**. In contrast, the **methanol-to-olefins (MTO)** process, a multi-billion-dollar route to ethylene and propylene, relies exclusively on SAPO-34 (CHA topology) or high-silica ZSM-5 (SAR >200), whose low acid site density and reduced coking propensity are critical for catalyst longevity. The detergent industry, consuming over **1.5 million metric tons annually** of zeolite A (LTA, SAR ~1), requires a low SAR to maximize calcium ion-exchange capacity for water softening.

#### **Scientific Challenges and Research Motivation**
The precise control and characterization of SAR present enduring scientific challenges. During synthesis, SAR is influenced by a complex interplay of parameters: gel composition, mineralizing agent (e.g., OH⁻, F⁻), template structure, temperature (typically 80-180°C), and time. Achieving a target SAR does not guarantee uniform aluminum distribution; framework Al atoms can exhibit zoning or clustering, affecting acid site strength and proximity. Furthermore, post-synthetic modifications such as **dealumination** (via steam calcination or acid leaching) or **isomorphous substitution** can alter the *framework* SAR without changing the bulk composition, complicating accurate characterization. Advanced techniques like ²⁷Al MAS NMR, FTIR of probe molecules (e.g., pyridine, CO), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are required to distinguish framework from extra-framework aluminum.

The core research motivation lies in the direct, non-linear relationships between SAR and key properties:
1.  **Acidity:** The number of Brønsted acid sites (Si-(OH)-Al bridges) scales directly with Al content (inverse of SAR). However, acid *strength* often increases with SAR due to reduced site isolation and changes in the local electronic environment.
2.  **Hydrophilicity/Hydrophobicity:** Low-SAR zeolites (<10) are highly hydrophilic, favoring water adsorption. High-SAR zeolites (>30) become increasingly hydrophobic, selective for organic molecules—a critical property for VOC removal or organic/water separations.
3.  **Thermal and Hydrothermal Stability:** Higher SAR correlates strongly with enhanced stability. The degradation of the

## Results and Discussion

### Crystalline Phase Analysis

X-ray diffraction patterns of the synthesized materials revealed progressive zeolite crystallization with increasing thermal treatment duration. The precursor geopolymer exhibited broad, diffuse diffraction maxima at approximately 2θ = 28° and 40°, corresponding to the amorphous aluminosilicate network characteristic of this material. Following hydrothermal treatment at 150°C for 6 hours, the formation of zeolite A (LTA structure) was confirmed by the emergence of sharp diffraction peaks at 2θ = 7.1°, 10.1°, 12.4°, 16.1°, 21.6°, 24.0°, 26.9°, and 30.7°, which matched reported values within ±0.2° (Breck, 1974). Extended treatment to 12 hours resulted in the complete disappearance of amorphous contributions, indicating phase-pure zeolite A formation.

Conversion to zeolite X (FAU structure) was achieved at 180°C using higher Si/Al ratios in the starting gel. Characteristic reflections appeared at 2θ = 6.2°, 10.1°, 15.6°, 20.4°, 23.6°, 26.8°, and 31.3°, consistent with the literature (Derrouch et al., 2018). Notably, the zeolite Y sample (Si/Al = 2.5) displayed the diagnostic (111), (220), (311), (331), (533), and (642) reflections at 2θ values of 6.1°, 10.0°, 15.4°, 20.3°, 23.5°, and 31.1°, respectively. Crystallite sizes calculated using the Scherrer equation ranged from 45 nm (zeolite A) to 120 nm (zeolite Y), reflecting different nucleation and growth kinetics under the applied synthesis conditions.

### Textural Properties

Nitrogen sorption isotherms of the converted zeolites displayed Type I behavior characteristic of microporous materials. The geopolymer precursor exhibited a BET surface area of 42 m²/g and a cumulative pore volume of 0.18 cm³/g, values consistent with previous reports for metakaolin-based geopolymers (Duxson et al., 2007). Upon zeolitization, substantial enhancements in textural parameters were observed.

Zeolite A demonstrated a BET surface area of 687 m²/g with a total pore volume of 0.34 cm³/g. These values exceed the theoretical surface area for pure zeolite A (520 m²/g) reported by Möhmel et al. (2005), suggesting the presence of additional mesoporosity arising from incomplete crystallite coalescence. Zeolite X exhibited a BET surface area of 524 m²/g and pore volume of 0.29 cm³/g, while zeolite Y displayed the lowest surface area (412 m²/g) despite having larger crystal dimensions. This observation correlates with the higher framework density of FAU-type structures, which possess fewer accessible void spaces per unit mass.

# Synthesis of Zeolite Na-Y (FAU Framework Type): A Standard Hydrothermal Procedure

## Introduction

Zeolites are crystalline microporous aluminosilicate materials characterized by well-defined pore architectures and exceptional ion-exchange properties. The International Zeolite Association (IZA) assigns three-letter structure codes to each framework type; the faujasite framework is designated **FAU**. Zeolite Na-Y, with a Si/Al ratio of approximately 1.5–2.5, is synthesized hydrothermally from aluminosilicate gels under controlled conditions. This procedure yields phase-pure FAU material suitable for catalytic and adsorption applications.

## Materials and Equipment

**Reagents:**
- Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥97%, 4.00 g)
- Sodium aluminate (NaAlO₂, technical grade, 2.08 g)
- Colloidal silica sol (SiO₂, 30 wt% suspension in water, 24.67 g)
- Deionized water (88.0 mL)

**Equipment:**
- 250 mL high-density polyethylene (HDPE) bottle with screw cap
- Mechanical overhead stirrer with PTFE propeller
- Forced-air convection oven
- Programmable muffle furnace
- Centrifuge (or Buchner filtration apparatus)
- Analytical balance (±0.01 g precision)

## Synthesis Procedure

### Gel Preparation

The sodium aluminate (2.08 g) was dissolved in sodium hydroxide solution prepared by dissolving NaOH (4.00 g) in deionized water (50.0 mL) contained within a 250 mL HDPE bottle. This solution was stirred magnetically at 200 rpm for 15 minutes at ambient temperature (25 ± 2 °C) until complete dissolution was achieved. The colloidal silica sol (24.67 g) was added dropwise to the aluminate solution over 10 minutes under continuous stirring at 300 rpm. The resulting white suspension was diluted with the remaining deionized water (38.0 mL), and stirring was maintained for an additional 30 minutes. The final gel composition, expressed as molar oxide ratios, was: 3.5 Na₂O : Al₂O₃ : 10 SiO₂ : 180 H₂O.

### Hydrothermal Crystallization

The sealed HDPE bottle was transferred to a forced-air convection oven preheated to 100 °C. Crystallization was conducted under static conditions for 24 hours. The temperature was monitored continuously using a calibrated Type K thermocouple with ±2 °C accuracy. Following the crystallization period, the vessel was removed and cooled naturally to room temperature over 2 hours.

### Product Isolation and Washing

The solid product was separated from the mother liquor via centrifugation at 4,000 rpm for 15 minutes. The supernatant was decanted and discarded. The isolated solid was washed by re-suspension in deionized water (200 mL), followed by repeated centrifugation (three cycles total). The washing endpoint was determined by measuring the filtrate pH, which was reduced to <9.0 following the final wash.

### Drying and Calcination

The washed solid was dried in a convection oven at 110 °C for 12 hours under ambient atmosphere. The dried zeolite cake was mechanically ground using an agate mortar and pestle to obtain a homogeneous powder. Calcination was performed in a programmable muffle furnace using the following temperature program: ramp rate of 2 °C/min to 550 °C, hold for 6 hours, then natural cooling to ambient temperature. The calcined product was stored in a desiccator over anhydrous CaSO₄.

## Characterization Results

The FAU framework structure was confirmed by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). Characteristic diffraction peaks were observed at 2θ = 6.39°, 10.2°, 11.

**1. Introduction and Background**

Zeolites constitute a distinct class of crystalline microporous aluminosilicates characterized by a tetrahedral framework of $SiO_4$ and $AlO_4^-$ units linked by shared oxygen atoms. This architecture generates uniform molecular-sized channels and cages, alongside a permanent negative charge balanced by exchangeable cations. Since their discovery by the mineralogist Axel Fredrik Cronstedt in 1756, who coined the name from the Greek "zeo" (to boil) and "lithos" (stone) due to their intumescence upon heating, zeolites have evolved from mineralogical curiosities to indispensable industrial commodities.

The industrial utilization of zeolites bifurcates into two primary sources: natural and synthetic. While natural zeolites, such as clinoptilolite, mordenite, and chabazite, form in geological environments through the low-temperature alteration of volcanic glass in alkaline saline waters, synthetic zeolites are produced via precise hydrothermal synthesis. The synthetic era began in the late 1940s through the pioneering work of Richard Barrer and Robert Milton, leading to the discovery of zeolites A (LTA) and X (FAU). Today, the global zeolite market is dominated by synthetic materials, with annual production volumes exceeding 4 million metric tonnes, largely driven by the demand for fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) catalysts and detergents. In contrast, natural zeolite production is approximately 3 million tonnes annually, primarily utilized in low-value bulk applications.

The dichotomy between natural and synthetic zeolites presents a significant scientific and engineering trade-off. Synthetic zeolites, such as ZSM-5 (MFI) and Zeolite Y (FAU), offer superior physicochemical properties, including high crystallinity, tunable Si/Al ratios (typically ranging from 1 to infinity), and the absence of extraneous impurities. These attributes are critical for high-performance applications; for instance, FCC catalysts require a faujasite framework with a unit cell size below 24.35 Å and high hydrothermal stability to withstand regeneration temperatures exceeding 700°C. Conversely, natural zeolites are significantly more cost-effective and environmentally benign, requiring only mining and minor beneficiation. However, their application is often restricted by the presence of gangue minerals (e.g., quartz, feldspars), limited cation exchange capacity (CEC) compared to optimized synthetic counterparts, and pore blockages that restrict diffusion kinetics.

The current research landscape is motivated by the need to bridge this performance gap while leveraging the economic advantages of natural resources. Key scientific challenges include the modification of natural zeolites to enhance their selectivity and capacity for high-value applications, such as the removal of heavy metals or radioactive isotopes ($Cs^+$, $Sr^{2+}$) from nuclear waste streams. Furthermore, the industry faces pressure to reduce the environmental footprint of synthetic processes, which often involve expensive organic structure-directing agents (SDAs) and high-purity chemical reagents. Consequently, a major thrust in zeolite science involves the "valorization" of natural zeolites as raw materials or low-cost precursors for the synthesis of high-purity zeolitic membranes and advanced adsorbents, aiming to combine the structural perfection of synthetic phases with the sustainability and abundance of natural ores.

### **Comparative Analysis of Natural and Synthetic Zeolites: Introduction and Background**

Zeolites, a class of microporous, crystalline aluminosilicates, constitute a cornerstone of modern materials science and industrial chemistry. Defined by their uniform, molecular-sized pores, high thermal stability, and tunable acidity, their utility spans catalysis, ion exchange, and separation processes. A fundamental dichotomy exists between **natural zeolites**, formed over geological timescales, and their **synthetic counterparts**, engineered under controlled laboratory and industrial conditions. This introduction delineates the historical evolution, industrial relevance, and scientific challenges that underpin the comparative study of these two material classes, motivating ongoing research into their tailored synthesis and application.

**Historical Context and Discovery**
The term "zeolite," from the Greek *zein* (to boil) and *lithos* (stone), was coined in 1756 by Swedish mineralogist Axel Fredrik Cronstedt, who observed steam release upon heating the mineral stilbite. Natural zeolites, primarily of volcanic origin, were geological curiosities for nearly two centuries. The paradigm shifted in the mid-20th century with the pioneering hydrothermal synthesis of zeolites A, X, and Y by Robert Milton and Donald Breck at Union Carbide in the 1950s. This breakthrough demonstrated that zeolite frameworks could be rationally designed and produced, launching the field of **molecular sieve science**. While natural zeolites provided the structural blueprint, synthetic zeolites enabled precise control over composition, porosity, and crystal size—a control unattainable in nature.

**Industrial Relevance and Market Dynamics**
The global zeolite market, valued at approximately **USD 33.5 billion in 2022**, is projected to grow at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of over 4.5%, driven by environmental regulations and process intensification. This market is segmented by source, with synthetic zeolites dominating high-value applications, constituting roughly **70-75% of the market by value**, despite natural zeolites accounting for a larger volume by mass (millions of tonnes annually) in bulk applications.

The application portfolio is sharply differentiated by material origin. **Natural zeolites**, such as clinoptilolite and chabazite, are exploited in bulk, cost-sensitive roles: as soil amendments and slow-release fertilizers in agriculture (utilizing their cation exchange capacity, CEC), as adsorbents for ammonia and heavy metals in wastewater treatment, and as additives in animal feed and construction materials. Their requirements are primarily low cost (< USD 100/tonne) and adequate, albeit variable, CEC (often 1.5–2.5 meq/g).

In contrast, **synthetic zeolites** command premium prices (e.g., zeolite Y at USD 2,000–4,000/tonne) and are indispensable in high-performance, technology-critical sectors. Their applications include:
1.  **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC):** Zeolite Y, dealuminated and stabilized via ultra-stabilization processes, is the principal catalyst for vacuum gasoil cracking, requiring exceptional hydrothermal stability (>800°C in steam) and precisely tuned acidity (controlled by the Si/Al ratio, typically 2.5 to >30).
2.  **Detergent Builders:** Zeolite A (LTA framework) has largely replaced phosphate builders, requiring high calcium ion exchange capacity (>300 mg CaO/g), controlled particle size (<5 µm to prevent fabric damage), and rapid kinetics.
3.  **Gas Separation & Adsorption:** Zeolite 5A (Ca-exchanged LTA) is used for pressure swing adsorption (PSA) to separate n-/iso-paraffins, demanding uniform pore aperture of 5 Å and high adsorption capacity.
4.  **Fine Chemical Synthesis:** Titanosilicate zeolites like TS-1 (MFI framework) catalyze selective oxidations with H₂O₂, requiring atomically dispersed, framework titanium sites and hydrophobic character (high Si/Ti ratio).

**Key Scientific Challenges and Research Motivation**
The central scientific challenge lies in bridging the **performance-cost chasm** between natural and synthetic zeolites. Natural zeolites suffer from inherent limitations: **compositional and phase heterogeneity**, **non-uniform porosity** (often with significant mesoporous and macroporous contributions from impurities), **lower thermal and acid stability**, and **limited framework diversity** (only about 40 of over 250 known frameworks occur naturally). These factors restrict their use to non-exacting applications.

Synthesis, while offering precision, faces its own challenges: the use of costly **organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs)** like tetrapropylammonium hydroxide for ZSM-5, **high energy intensity** from hydrothermal treatments (100–180°C for 24–72 hours), and **environmental concerns**

# Clinoptilolite: Properties and Applications in Microporous Materials Science

## Introduction and Structural Framework

Clinoptilolite (Na₆[Al₆Si₃₀O₇₂]·20H₂O) is the most abundant natural zeolite in the heulandite group, characterized by a monoclinal crystal system and a distinctive framework topology consisting of 8-membered ring channels (approximately 3.8 × 5.4 Å) running parallel to the *c*-axis. The unit cell parameters (a = 17.68 Å, b = 17.82 Å, c = 7.40 Å, β = 116.3°) provide a three-dimensional pore architecture that enables selective molecular sieving and cation exchange properties. The framework Si/Al ratio typically ranges from 4.5 to 5.5, determining thermal stability and cation exchange capacity.

## Physicochemical Characteristics

The cation exchange capacity (CEC) of clinoptilolite ranges from 150–220 meq/100g, primarily governed by extraframework cations (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) occupying exchangeable positions within the channel system. Studies by Mumpton (1960) and Alietti et al. (1977) demonstrated that the thermal stability of natural clinoptilolite depends critically on dehydration behavior: complete dehydroxylation occurs at approximately 250–300°C, while framework collapse initiates above 700°C in dry atmospheres. However, in hydrated conditions, structural degradation begins at lower temperatures (450–500°C), as documented by Goto (1979).

Surface area measurements using the BET method yield values between 200–400 m²/g for natural samples, though activation procedures at 200°C under vacuum can enhance microporosity. Pore volume determinations via nitrogen sorption isotherms indicate micropore volumes of 0.08–0.15 cm³/g, with hierarchical mesoporosity developing during natural weathering or thermal treatment.

## Characterization Methodologies

Comprehensive clinoptilolite characterization employs multiple complementary techniques. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns exhibit characteristic reflections at 2θ = 9.8°, 22.2°, and 26.6°, enabling phase identification and quantification in natural deposits. In situ high-temperature XRD studies by Alberti (1973) revealed reversible unit cell contraction during dehydration (a = 17.55 Å at 400°C), followed by irreversible amorphization upon heating to 750°C.

Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) identifies framework vibrations at 1050–1100 cm⁻¹ (asymmetric stretching), 720–790 cm⁻¹ (symmetric stretching), and 450–500 cm⁻¹ (T-O bending), while O-H stretching bands at 3400–3600 cm⁻¹ and bending at 1620–1640 cm⁻¹ confirm hydration state. ²⁹Si and ²⁷Al solid-state NMR studies by Maggioni et al. (1981) resolved framework silicon environments, confirming ordered Si/Al distribution in the heulandite structure.

## Applications

### Environmental Remediation

Clinoptilolite's selective cation exchange properties enable effective remediation of aqueous streams contaminated with radionuclides (²⁹⁰Sr, ¹³⁷Cs, ²³⁸U) and heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺, Hg²⁺). Batch exchange experiments by Mimura et al. (1991) demonstrated ⁹⁰Sr uptake capacities of 45 mg/g at pH 7, with distribution coefficients (Kd) exceeding 10⁴ mL/g. Fixed-bed column studies with natural clinoptilolite from Semnan, Iran, achieved >95% Pb²⁺ removal at bed heights of 30 cm and flow rates of 5 mL/min (Akhtar et al., 2007).
